name: Coquelets sur Canapés (Roast Squab Chickens with Chicken Liver Canapés and Mushrooms)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 15 ml minced shallots or green onions
6 poultry or game livers from the birds
45 ml fresh, raw pork fat; OR fat bacon simmered in water for 10 minutes, rinsed, and dried
Six 10- to 340 g, ready-to-cook squab chickens, game hens, squab pigeons, or game birds
30 ml finely minced shallots or green onions
6 strips of bacon simmered in water for 10 minutes, rinsed, and dried
A shallow roasting pan just large enough to hold the birds easily on their sides
45 ml butter melted with 15 ml good cooking oil
15 ml minced shallots or green onions
355 ml brown chicken stock, brown stock, or canned beef bouillon
A handful of water-cress leaves or parsley sprigs
authorNotes: Also for: squab pigeons, game hens, partridge, quail, dove | This is one of the classic French recipes for serving small roast birds. The livers are chopped, seasoned, and spread over sautéed bread rectangles; just before serving, these are run under the broiler. Then the roast birds are placed on them, and the dish is garnished with a wine-flavored deglazing sauce and sautéed mushrooms. | Only sautéed, shoestring, or souffléed potatoes, or homemade potato chips are suggested.

name: POULET POÊLÉ À L’ESTRAGON (Casserole-roasted Chicken with Tarragon)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 3 or 4 sprigs of fresh tarragon or 2 ml of dried tarragon
A heavy fireproof casserole just large enough to hold the chicken on its back and on its side
3 or 4 sprigs of fresh tarragon or 2 ml dried tarragon
A tight-fitting cover for the casserole
475 ml brown chicken stock, or 235 ml canned beef bouillon and 235 ml canned chicken broth
15 ml cornstarch blended with 30 ml Madeira or port
30 ml fresh minced tarragon or parsley
Optional but attractive: 10 to 12 fresh tarragon leaves blanched for 30 seconds in boiling water then rinsed in cold water, and dried on paper towels
authorNotes: For: roasters, large fryers, and capons | When a chicken is cooked this way, it is trussed, browned in butter and oil, then set to roast in a covered casserole with herbs and seasonings. It is a lovely method, as the buttery, aromatic steam in the casserole gives the chicken great tenderness and flavor. While oven cooking is more even, the top of the stove may be used if your casserole is heavy; then the chicken must be turned and basted frequently, and the cooking will be a little longer than for oven cooking. | They are the same as for a roast chicken .

name: Farce Duxelles (Mushroom Stuffing)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 20 ml minced shallots or green onions
The chicken gizzard, peeled and minced
60 ml dry, white crumbs from homemade-type of bread
2 ml minced fresh or dried tarragon
authorNotes: A chicken will need 10 to 15 minutes more cooking if you fill it with this stuffing. | A handful at a time, twist the mushrooms into a ball in the corner of a towel to extract their juice. Sauté them in hot butter and oil with the shallots or onions for 5 to 8 minutes, until the pieces begin to separate from each other. Place them in a mixing bowl. | Sauté the gizzard for 2 minutes in hot butter. Add the liver and sauté 2 minutes more. Add to the mixing bowl.

name: Poulet en Cocotte Bonne Femme (Casserole-roasted Chicken with Bacon, Onions, and Potatoes)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A fireproof casserole for cooking the chicken
A 1.4 kg, ready-to-cook roasting chicken, trussed and buttered
15 to 25 peeled white onions about 1 inch in diameter
455–680 g “boiling” potatoes or small new potatoes
A medium herb bouquet: 4 parsley sprigs, ½ bay leaf, and 1 ml thyme tied in washed cheesecloth
A tight-fitting cover for the casserole
authorNotes: This is an all-in-one dish where bacon and vegetables are cooked with the chicken and each item takes on a bit of flavor from its neighbors. No other vegetables are needed to make up a main course, but you may wish to serve broiled tomatoes along with it for color. | Remove the rind and cut the bacon into lardons (rectangular strips ½ inch wide and 1½ inches long). Simmer for 10 minutes in 2 quarts of water. Rinse in cold water, and dry. In the casserole, sauté the bacon for 2 to 3 minutes in butter until very lightly browned. Remove to a side dish, leaving the fat in the casserole. | Brown the chicken in the hot fat, as described in the master recipe . Remove it to a side dish and pour the fat out of the casserole.

name: POULET SAUTÉ (Sautéed Chicken)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A heavy, 10-inch casserole, skillet, or electric skillet
30 ml butter and 15 ml oil, more if necessary to keep bottom of pan filmed with fat
Optional: 5–10 ml fresh green herbs: thyme, basil, and tarragon, or tarragon only; or 5 ml dried herbs
15 ml minced shallot or green onions
Optional: 120 ml dry white wine or 80 ml dry white vermouth
175–235 ml brown chicken stock, canned beef bouillon, or canned chicken broth
Optional: 15–30 ml minced parsley or fresh green herbs
authorNotes: In a true sauté the cut-up chicken is cooked entirely in butter, or butter and oil, with seasonings. No liquid comes in contact with it until the very end. It is a quick and delicious way to cook chicken, but should be served as soon as possible after it is done or it loses the fresh and juicy characteristics of a sauté. The fricassees, however, as they cook in a sauce, take well to reheating. | French chicken is disjointed so that each wing includes a strip from the lower part of the breast. The breast minus ribs is cut in two, crosswise. The drumsticks are separated from the second joints. This makes 8 good serving pieces, plus the back cut in two, crosswise, if you wish to include it. | American chicken is usually disjointed into 2 drumsticks, 2 second joints, the 2 halves of the breast, and the 2 wings with no breast meat attached. So that the breasts will cook evenly, slip a knife under the ribs and remove them. Each breast half may be chopped in two, crosswise, if you wish.

name: Poulet Sauté à la Crème (Deglazing Sauce with Cream)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 15 ml minced shallots or green onions
120 ml dry white wine or 80 ml dry white vermouth
Optional: 15–30 ml minced parsley or mixed green herbs
authorNotes: Sauté the chicken as described in the preceding recipe and place it on a hot platter. Remove all but 1 tablespoon of fat from the casserole. Stir in the shallots or onions and cook slowly for 1 minute. Then add the wine and boil it rapidly down to about 3 tablespoons, scraping up coagulated cooking juices. Add the cream and boil it down until it has thickened slightly. | Correct seasoning. Off heat and just before serving, swirl in the butter and optional herbs. | Pour the sauce over the chicken.

name: Poulet Sauté aux Herbes de Provence (Chicken Sautéed with Herbs and Garlic, Egg Yolk and Butter Sauce)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A heavy- 10-inch fireproof casserole, skillet, or electric skillet set at 300 degrees
1.1–1.4 kg of cut-up frying chicken dried in a towel
160 ml dry white wine or 120 ml dry white vermouth
15 ml dry white wine or white vermouth
30 ml fresh minced basil, fresh fennel tops, or parsley
authorNotes: Basil, thyme or savory, a pinch of fennel, and a bit of garlic give this sauté a fine Provençal flavor that is even more pronounced if your herbs are fresh. The sauce is a type of hollandaise, as the herbal, buttery pan juices are beaten into egg yolks to make a thick and creamy liaison. Serve this dish with potatoes sautéed in butter or potato crêpes , broiled tomatoes and a chilled rosé wine. | Heat the butter until it is foaming, then turn the chicken pieces in it for 7 to 8 minutes, not letting them color more than a deep yellow. Remove the white meat. Season the dark meat with herbs, salt, and pepper, and add the garlic to the casserole. Cover and cook slowly for 8 to 9 minutes. Season the white meat and add it to the casserole, basting the chicken with the butter. Cook for about 15 minutes, turning and basting 2 or 3 times, until the chicken is tender and its juices run pale yellow when the meat is pricked with a fork. | When the chicken is done, remove it to a hot platter, cover, and keep warm.

name: FRICASSÉE DE POULET À L’ANCIENNE (Old-fashioned Chicken Fricassee with Wine-flavored Cream Sauce, Onions, and Mushrooms)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A heavy, 10-inch, fireproof casserole or electric skillet
1 thinly sliced onion, carrot, and celery stalk
710 ml boiling white chicken stock, white stock, or canned chicken bouillon
235 ml dry white wine or 160 ml dry white vermouth
A small herb bouquet: 2 parsley sprigs, ⅓ bay leaf and 1 ml thyme tied in washed cheesecloth
225 g fresh mushrooms stewed in butter, lemon juice, and water
Salt and white pepper Drops of lemon juice Pinch of nutmeg
authorNotes: For this traditional Sunday dinner dish, which is not difficult to execute, the chicken pieces are turned in hot butter, sprinkled with flour and seasonings, then simmered in wine and white stock. The sauce is a reduction of the cooking liquid, enriched with cream and egg yolks. Braised onions and mushrooms accompany the chicken. Include also steamed rice or risotto , or buttered noodles. If you want other vegetables, buttered peas or asparagus tips may serve as a garnish. | Serve a chilled, fairly full-bodied white Burgundy, Côtes du Rhône, or Bordeaux-Graves. | Preliminary cooking in butter

name: Fricassée de Poulet à l’Indienne (Curry Sauce)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
authorNotes: After the chicken has had its preliminary turning of 5 minutes in butter, blend in the curry powder. Cover, and proceed with the 10-minute cooking period. Then continue with the recipe.

name: Fricassée de Poulet au Paprika (Paprika Sauce)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
authorNotes: After the chicken has had its preliminary turning of 5 minutes in butter, blend in the paprika. Cover, and proceed with the 10-minute cooking period. Then continue with the recipe. | After completing the sauce, stir in more paprika if the sauce needs color. It should be a creamy pink.

name: Fricassée de Poulet à l’Estragon (Tarragon Sauce)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 4 or 5 sprigs fresh tarragon or 10 ml dried tarragon
30 ml fresh minced tarragon or parsley
authorNotes: Add the tarragon to the wine and stock for the simmering of the chicken. | Stir fresh tarragon or parsley into the finished sauce.

name: FONDUE DE POULET À LA CRÈME (Chicken Simmered with Cream and Onions)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 80 ml cognac, Calvados, Madeira, or port; or 175 ml dry white wine, or 120 ml dry white vermouth
710 ml whipping cream brought to the boil in a small saucepan
authorNotes: In this rich and delectable dish, the chicken is cooked in butter and onions, then simmered with wine and heavy cream. Serve it with steamed rice or risotto , buttered green peas or baked cucumbers , and a fairly full-bodied white Burgundy or white Bordeaux-Graves. | Dry the chicken thoroughly. Turn it in hot butter for 4 to 5 minutes, until the meat has stiffened slightly but has not browned. Remove it to a side dish. | Stir the onions into the butter in the pan. Cover and cook very slowly for 5 minutes, or until the onions are fairly tender but not browned.

name: COQ AU VIN (Chicken in Red Wine with Onions, Mushrooms, and Bacon)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A 3- to 115 g chunk of lean bacon
A heavy, 10-inch, fireproof casserole or an electric skillet
710 ml young, full-bodied red wine such as Burgundy, Beaujolais, Côtes du Rhône, or Chianti
235–475 ml brown chicken stock, brown stock, or canned beef bouillon
authorNotes: This popular dish may be called coq au Chambertin, coq au riesling , or coq au whatever wine you use for its cooking. It is made with either white or red wine, but the red is more characteristic. In France it is usually accompanied only by parsley potatoes; buttered green peas could be included if you wish a green vegetable. Serve with it a young, full-bodied red Burgundy, Beaujolais, or Côtes du Rhône. | Remove the rind and cut the bacon into lardons (rectangles ¼ inch across and 1 inch long). Simmer for 10 minutes in 2 quarts of water. Rinse in cold water. Dry. | Sauté the bacon slowly in hot butter until it is very lightly browned (temperature of 260 degrees for an electric skillet). Remove to a side dish.

name: POULETS GRILLÉS À LA DIABLE (Chicken Broiled with Mustard, Herbs, and Bread Crumbs)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: Two ready-to-cook, 1.1 kg broilers, halved or quartered
A saucepan containing 90 ml melted butter and 30 ml oil
90 ml prepared mustard of the strong Dijon type
45 ml finely minced shallots or green onions
1 ml pepper Pinch of cayenne pepper
1 l fresh, white crumbs from homemade-type of bread (make the crumbs in an electric blender, 3 or 4 slices of bread at a time)
The rest of the basting fat
authorNotes: Here is a fine method for broiled chicken which is good either hot or cold. The chicken is partially cooked under the broiler, then smeared with mustard and herbs, rolled in fresh bread crumbs, and returned to the broiler to brown and finish cooking. A practical attribute is that it can be almost entirely cooked ahead of time, set aside or refrigerated, and then finished off in the oven. With the mustard dip, a sauce is not a necessity. But if you want one, serve melted butter mixed with lemon juice and minced herbs, or sauce diable (herbal brown sauce with shallots and wine) . Baked, whole tomatoes and green beans would go well with it, and a chilled rosé wine. | Preheat oven broiler to moderately hot. | Dry the chicken thoroughly, paint it with butter and oil, and arrange it skin-side down in the bottom of the broiling pan. Place it so that the surface of the chicken is 5 to 6 inches from the hot broiling element and broil 10 minutes on each side, basting every 5 minutes. The chicken should be very lightly browned. Salt it lightly.

name: SUPRÊMES DE VOLAILLE À BLANC (Breast of Chicken with Cream)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 4 suprêmes (boned breasts from two fryers; see directions in paragraph preceding recipe)
A heavy, covered, fireproof casserole about 10 inches in diameter
A round of waxed paper 10 inches in diameter and buttered on one side
60 ml white or brown stock or canned beef bouillon
60 ml port, Madeira, or dry white vermouth
authorNotes: Serve these with buttered asparagus tips, green peas, artichoke hearts, or creamed spinach, a good risotto cooked in chicken stock, and a bottle of chilled white Burgundy or Traminer. | Preheat oven to 400 degrees. | Rub the suprêmes with drops of lemon juice and sprinkle lightly with salt and pepper. Heat the butter in the casserole until it is foaming. Quickly roll the suprêmes in the butter, lay the buttered paper over them, cover casserole and place in hot oven. After 6 minutes, press top of suprêmes with your finger. If still soft, return to oven for a moment or two. When the meat is springy to the touch it is done. Remove the suprêmes to a warm platter and cover while making the sauce (2 to 3 minutes).

name: Suprêmes de Volaille Archiduc (Chicken Breasts with Paprika, Onions, and Cream)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 4 suprêmes and the sauce ingredients in the preceding master recipe
authorNotes: This is a delicious combination, the onions giving the chicken and the sauce a subtle flavor, while the paprika lends fragrance and rosiness to the finished dish. | Drop the minced onions into boiling water for 1 minute. Drain, run cold water over them, and drain again. Cook the onions with the salt, paprika, and butter in the covered casserole for about 10 minutes over very low heat until the onions are tender and translucent, but not browned. | Following the master recipe, cook the suprêmes in the onions, paprika, and butter. Remove when done, leaving the onions in the casserole. Complete the sauce, also as described in the master recipe.

name: Suprêmes de Volaille à l’Écossaise (Chicken Breasts with Diced Aromatic Vegetables and Cream)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: The following vegetables cut into neat 1 / 16 -inch cubes, making 160–175 ml in all:
4 suprêmes and the sauce ingredients in the master recipe
authorNotes: Cook the diced vegetables slowly with the salt and butter for about 10 minutes in the covered casserole until tender, but not browned. | Following the master recipe, cook the suprêmes in the vegetables and butter. Remove them when done, leaving the vegetables in the casserole. Complete the sauce, as described in the master recipe.

name: Suprêmes de Volaille aux Champignons (Chicken Breasts with Mushrooms and Cream)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 15 ml minced shallot or green onion
115 g diced or sliced fresh mushrooms
4 suprêmes and the sauce ingredients in the master recipe
authorNotes: Heat the butter in the casserole over moderate heat until foaming. Stir in the minced shallots or green onion and sauté a moment without browning. Then stir in the mushrooms and sauté lightly for a minute or two without browning. Sprinkle with salt. | Following the master recipe, cook the suprêmes in the mushrooms and butter. Remove when done, leaving the mushrooms in the casserole. Complete the sauce, as described in the master recipe.

name: SUPRÊMES DE VOLAILLE À BRUN (Chicken Breasts Sautéed in Butter)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 235 ml flour spread on an 8-inch plate
90–120 ml clarified butter (note that you will need 60 ml more for your sauce)
authorNotes: Here the chicken breasts are lightly dusted with flour and are sautéed in clarified butter. (Ordinary butter will burn and form black specks on the suprêmes. Clarified butter may be heated to a higher temperature before burning.) A good accompaniment for this dish would be grilled or stuffed tomatoes, buttered green peas or beans, and potato balls sautéed in butter. Serve with it a red Bordeaux-Médoc. | Just before sautéing, sprinkle the suprêmes with salt and pepper, roll them in the flour, and shake off excess flour. | Pour clarified butter into skillet to a depth of about 1 / 16 inch. Set over moderately high heat. When the butter begins to deepen in color very slightly, put in the suprêmes. Regulate heat so butter is always hot but does not turn more than a deep yellow. After 3 minutes, turn the suprêmes and sauté on the other side. In two minutes, press tops of suprêmes with your finger. As soon as they are springy to the touch, they are done. Remove to a hot platter, leaving the butter in the skillet.

name: Brown Deglazing Sauce with Wine
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 15 ml minced shallot or green onion
160 ml brown stock or canned beef bouillon
authorNotes: After removing the sautéed suprêmes , stir minced shallot or onion into skillet and sauté a moment. Then pour in the wine and stock or bouillon and boil down rapidly over high heat until liquid is lightly syrupy. Pour over the suprêmes , sprinkle with parsley, and serve.

name: Deglazing Sauce with Truffles
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 1 minced canned truffle and the juice from its can
Ingredients for the preceding brown deglazing sauce minus the parsley
authorNotes: After sautéing the shallots or onions, as in the preceding master recipe, add the wine, stock or bouillon, and the truffle and its juice. Boil down liquid until syrupy, and pour over the suprêmes.

name: Suprêmes de Volaille à la Milanaise (Chicken Breasts Rolled in Parmesan and Fresh Bread Crumbs)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 235 ml flour spread on an 8-inch plate
1 egg, 1 ml salt, and 2 ml olive oil beaten together in an 8-inch soup plate
120 ml freshly grated Parmesan cheese and 120 ml fine, white, fresh bread crumbs mixed together in an 8-inch dish
Ingredients for brown butter sauce, master recipe
authorNotes: Season the suprêmes with salt and pepper. One at a time, roll them in the flour and shake off excess. Dip in beaten egg. Then roll in the cheese and bread crumbs, patting them in place with the flat of a knife. Lay the suprêmes on waxed paper and allow cheese and bread crumbs to set for 10 to 15 minutes or several hours. | Sauté on both sides in clarified butter until resilient to the pressure of your finger. Serve with brown butter sauce as described in the master recipe.

name: CANETON RÔTI (Roast Duckling)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A pinch of thyme or sage
A shallow roasting pan just large enough to hold the duck easily
355–475 ml brown duck stock, beef stock, or canned beef bouillon
authorNotes: Estimated roasting time: 1 hour and 20 to 40 minutes | Preheat oven to 425 degrees. | Season the inside of the duck with salt, pepper, herbs, and the sliced onion. Secure the legs, wings, and neck skin to the body. Prick the skin around the thighs, back, and lower breast. Dry the duck thoroughly.

name: Caneton Rôti à l’Alsacienne (Roast Duck with Sausage and Apple Stuffing)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 60 ml stock or canned beef bouillon
authorNotes: Apples and duck are a fine combination, and sausages make it an even better one. The platter may be garnished with more apples and sausages if you wish, braised onions, and sautéed potatoes or potato crêpes. A chilled Alsatian Traminer would go well with it, or hard cider. | Estimated roasting time: 1 hour and 45 to 60 minutes | Sausage and apple stuffing

name: Caneton à l’ Orange (Roast Duck with Orange Sauce)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 475 ml strong, brown duck stock (follow directions for brown chicken stock , using duck giblets instead of chicken giblets)
30 ml arrowroot blended with 45 ml port or Madeira
The rest of the blanched orange peel
Drops of orange bitters or lemon juice
authorNotes: One of the most well known of all the duck dishes, caneton à l’orange , is roast duck decorated with fresh orange segments and accompanied by an orange-flavored brown sauce. Its most important element is its sauce—a rich, strong, meaty, duck essence darkened with caramel, flavored with wine and orange peel, and given a light liaison of arrowroot. You can and should prepare the sauce well ahead of time so that when the duck is roasted, the dish is within 2 to 3 minutes of being done. | Nothing should interfere with the flavors of the duck, the sauce, and the oranges. Sautéed or shoestring potatoes, or homemade potato chips are your best choice. Serve a good red Bordeaux-Médoc, or a chilled white Burgundy—Meursault, Montrachet, or Corton-Charlemagne. | Note: Under the ingredients needed for the sauce are 2 cups of excellent duck stock. This should be prepared ahead of time, as it must simmer about 2 hours.

name: Caneton aux Cerises Caneton Montmorency (Roast Duck with Cherries)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 36 to 48 red or black pitted cherries (if frozen, thaw and drain)
authorNotes: Cherries or peaches are also good as a garnish for roast duck. Roast the bird as directed in the master recipe . Make the caramel-colored and arrowroot-thickened sauce described for the preceding caneton à l’orange , omitting the orange peel and orange liqueur. The fruit is heated in the sauce as follows: | Toss the cherries in the saucepan with the lemon juice, port or cognac, and sugar. Let them soak for at least 20 to 30 minutes. | After the duck has roasted, and the pan juices have been deglazed with wine and added to the sauce, pour the sauce into the cherries. Heat to below the simmer for 3 to 4 minutes to poach the cherries (if liquid simmers, the fruit may shrivel). Remove the cherries with a slotted spoon and distribute them over and around the duck.

name: Caneton aux Pêches (Roast Duck with Peaches)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 3 large or 6 small, firm, ripe, freestone peaches (or drained canned peaches, minus the sugar below)
authorNotes: If using fresh peaches, peel and halve them not more than 30 minutes before serving so they will not discolor. Arrange the peaches in a fireproof dish and baste them with the liquids and sugar. Baste several times more before using. | After the duck has roasted and the pan juices have been deglazed with wine and added to the sauce, pour the sauce over the peaches. Proceed as for the preceding duck with cherries recipe.

name: CANETON POÊLÉ AUX NAVETS (Casserole-roasted Duck with Turnips)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A heavy, oval casserole just large enough to hold the duck easily
45 ml rendered fresh pork fat or cooking oil
A medium herb bouquet: 4 parsley sprigs, ½ bay leaf, and 1 ml thyme tied in washed cheesecloth
0.9 kg firm, crisp, white or yellow turnips
authorNotes: In casserole roasting, the duck is browned on all sides, then set to roast in a covered casserole. Cooked in its own steam, the duck’s flesh becomes wonderfully tender, and the layer of subcutaneous fat is even more effectively dissolved than by roasting. The turnips, which finish their cooking with the duck absorbing cooking juices, are particularly succulent. No other vegetable is necessary, but you could serve green peas or broccoli. A red Bordeaux, Beaujolais, or Côtes du Rhône would be the choice of wines. | Estimated roasting time: 1 hour and 20 to 40 minutes | Preheat oven to 325 degrees.

name: Canard Braisé aux Choux Rouges (Duck Braised in Red Cabbage)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: Ingredients for 0.9 kg of braised sauerkraut , or braised red cabbage
A casserole large enough to include the duck as well
authorNotes: These two classic combinations are both done in the same way: after the sauerkraut or cabbage is about two thirds braised, the browned duck is added to cook in the casserole, and all ingredients benefit from their mutual exchange of flavors. Parsley potatoes or braised chestnuts and a chilled Alsatian Traminer go well with this. | Follow the recipe for braised sauerkraut or braised red cabbage, and cook for 3½ hours. | Season, truss, prick, and dry the duck. Brown it in hot fat as described in the preceding recipe. Salt it and bury it in the casserole with the sauerkraut or cabbage. Cover, and braise for about 1½ hours more, or until the duck is done.

name: OIE RÔTIE AUX PRUNEAUX (Roast Goose with Prune and Foie Gras Stuffing)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 235 ml white wine or 160 ml dry white vermouth
475 ml brown goose stock, brown stock, or canned beef bouillon
30 ml finely minced shallots or green onions
120 ml or 115 g of foie gras (goose liver), or very good liver paste
A 4.1 kg ready-to-cook young roasting goose
authorNotes: Goose is roasted exactly like duck, the only exception being that the goose is basted every 15 to 20 minutes with boiling water to help in the dissolution of its subcutaneous fat, which is more copious for goose than for duck. Prunes and goose are an exceptionally fine combination. With the goose you can serve braised onions and chestnuts, and a full red wine such as a Burgundy or Châteauneuf-du-Pape. | Estimated roasting time: About 2½ hours | Note: A good brown goose stock will give you an excellent sauce, but it must be prepared in advance; see preceding remarks.

name: OIE BRAISÉE AUX MARRONS (Braised Goose with Chestnut and Sausage Stuffing)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 680 g of fresh chestnuts, or 1 l of drained, canned, and unsweetened chestnuts
1 l of the fresh ground veal and pork stuffing described
The goose liver, chopped, and sautéed in butter
A 4.1 kg ready-to-cook young roasting goose
A covered roaster just large enough to hold the goose easily
The goose neck, wing tips, gizzard, and heart
60 ml rendered goose fat, rendered fresh pork fat, or cooking oil
1 l boiling brown stock or canned beef bouillon
710 ml dry white wine or 475 ml dry white vermouth
authorNotes: There are many who prefer braised goose to roast goose because the meat is more tender and more flavorful, and the closed, moist cooking of a braise renders out more fat than open-pan roasting. A good combination to go with this would be more chestnuts, either braised or pureéd, and braised lettuce, onions or leeks. Brussels sprouts, or braised green or red cabbage are other choices. Serve a red Burgundy, Côtes du Rhône, Châteauneuf-du-Pape, or chilled Alsatian Traminer. | For a 9-lb. bird, serving 8 to 10 people — because of the meat stuffing the goose will go further. | Estimated roasting time: 2½ hours

name: BIFTECK SAUTÉ AU BEURRE (Pan-broiled Steak)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: One or two heavy skillets just large enough to hold the meat easily in one layer
20 ml butter and 20 ml oil, or rendered fresh beef suet, more if needed
0.9–1.1 kg steak ¾ to 1 inch thick
120 ml stock, canned beef bouillon, red wine, dry white wine, dry white vermouth, or water
authorNotes: Pan-broiled steak is very French and also a very nice method for cooking small steaks. None of the juice essences are lost, and it is easy to tell when the steak is done. | A 1-inch steak takes 8 to 10 minutes to cook, and the sauce, or pan gravy, 1 to 2 minutes to prepare after the steak is on its platter. The sauce, you will observe, is a deglazing of the pan with stock, wine, or water, and a swish of butter at the end. It is purely an extension of the pan juices, and amounts to only 1 or 2 tablespoons of buttery, concentrated essence per serving. | In France you would select an entrecôte, romsteck, faux-filet , or bifteck. In America buy any tender, well-aged ¾- to 1-inch steak or steaks which will fit easily into a skillet such as:

name: Bifteck Sauté Bercy (Pan-broiled Steak, with Shallot and White Wine Sauce)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 45 ml minced shallots or green onions
120 ml dry white wine or dry white vermouth
Optional: 30–45 ml diced, poached beef marrow
authorNotes: For broiled steak, use a beurre Bercy , and spread it over the steak just before serving. | For 4 to 6 people, depending on your menu | Sauté the steak as described in the master recipe and remove it to a hot platter. Pour the fat out of the skillet. Add the butter. Stir in the shallots or onions and cook slowly for a minute.

name: Bifteck Sauté Béarnaise (Pan-broiled Steak with Béarnaise Sauce)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 120 ml brown stock, canned beef bouillon, dry white wine, or dry white vermouth.
authorNotes: For 4 to 6 people, depending on your menu | Sauté the steak as described in the master recipe, preceding. Deglaze skillet with stock, bouillon, or wine, boiling it down rapidly to reduce it to 1½ spoonfuls. Beat the liquid by droplets into the sauce béarnaise. | Decorate the steak platter with sautéed or fried potatoes and fresh water cress. Serve the sauce in a warmed sauceboat.

name: Steak au Poivre (Pepper Steak with Brandy Sauce)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 30 ml of a mixture of several kinds of peppercorns, or white peppercorns
0.9–1.1 kg steak ¾ to 1 inch thick
30 ml minced shallots or green onions
120 ml stock or canned beef bouillon
authorNotes: Steak au poivre can be very good when it is not so buried in pepper and doused with flaming brandy that the flavor of the meat is utterly disguised. In fact, we do not care at all for flaming brandy with this dish; it is too reminiscent of restaurant show-off cooking for tourists. And the alcohol taste, as it is not boiled off completely, remains in the brandy, spoiling the taste of the meat. | For 4 to 6 people, depending on your menu | Place the peppercorns in a big mixing bowl and crush them roughly with a pestle or the bottom of a bottle.

name: TOURNEDOS SAUTÉS AUX CHAMPIGNONS TOURNEDOS SAUTÉS CHASSEUR
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 6 crustless rounds of white bread, 2½ inches in diameter and 3 / 16 inch thick
225 g fresh mushrooms, whole if very small, quartered if large
30 ml minced shallots or green onions
6 filet steaks 1 inch thick and 2½ inches in diameter, each wrapped in a strip of fat as illustrated on this page
1 or 2 heavy skillets just large enough to hold the steaks easily
120 ml stock or canned beef bouillon
60 ml Madeira mixed with 5 ml of arrowroot or cornstarch
30 ml minced parsley, tarragon and chervil, or parsley only
authorNotes: [ Filet Steaks with Mushroom and Madeira Sauce] | A handsome presentation for these steaks would be a platter decorated with whole baked tomatoes, artichoke hearts cooked in butter, and potato balls sautéed in butter. Serve with them a good red Bordeaux from the Médoc district. | Sauté bread rounds in hot clarified butter to brown very lightly on each side. Reheat them for a minute in a 350-degree oven just before serving.

name: Tournedos Henri IV
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 6 filet steaks sautéed in oil and butter
6 canapés (rounds of white bread sautéed in clarified butter)
60 ml Madeira, dry white wine, or dry white vermouth
60 ml beef stock or canned beef bouillon
6 fresh artichoke bottoms cooked in butter
Potato balls sautéed in butter , and rolled in 30 ml minced parsley
authorNotes: [ Filet Steaks with Artichoke Bottoms and Béarnaise Sauce] | Sauté the steaks as described in the master recipe. Season and place on canapés on a hot platter. Keep warm for a few minutes. Pour sauté fat out of skillet, add wine and stock or bouillon, and boil down rapidly reducing liquid to 3 tablespoons while scraping coagulated sauté juices into it. Spoon liquid over steaks. Top each steak with a hot artichoke bottom filled with béarnaise. Decorate platter with the hot potatoes and asparagus. Serve immediately.

name: Tournedos Rossini (Filet Steaks with Foie Gras , Truffles, and Madeira Sauce)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 3 large, fresh artichoke bottoms cooked in a blanc
6 slices canned “block” foie gras , ¼ inch thick and about 1½ inches in diameter
45 ml rich stock, mushroom essence , or canned beef bouillon
18 to 24 slices of canned truffle, 1 / 16 inch thick
6 filet steaks 1 inch thick and 2½ inches in diameter
Juice from the foie gras and truffles
5 ml arrowroot or cornstarch blended with 30 ml Madeira
authorNotes: A platter of tournedos Rossini takes the filet steak about as far as it can go. Were you living in France during the midwinter, your foie gras and truffles would, of course, be fresh. Most recipes use canapés (rounds of white bread sautéed in butter) as a bed for the steaks; we have chosen artichoke bottoms as a further improvement to an already elegant presentation. | Fitting accompaniments would be potato balls sautéed in butter, buttered peas, asparagus tips, or braised lettuce, and an excellent, château-bottled red Bordeaux from the Médoc district. | Slice each cooked artichoke bottom in two, horizontally. Season with salt, pepper, and melted butter. Place in a covered dish. Fifteen minutes before serving, heat them in a 350-degree oven.

name: BIFTECK HACHÉ À LA LYONNAISE (Ground Beef with Onions and Herbs)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 30 ml softened butter, ground beef suet, beef marrow, or fresh pork fat
120 ml flour spread on a plate
15 ml butter and 15 ml oil, or sufficient to film the bottom of the skillet
1 or 2 heavy skillets just large enough to hold the patties easily in one layer
120 ml beef stock, canned beef bouillon, dry white wine, dry white vermouth, red wine, or 60 ml water
authorNotes: Cook the onions slowly in the butter for about 10 minutes until very tender but not browned. Place in a mixing bowl. | Add the beef, butter or fat, seasonings, and egg to the onions in the mixing bowl and beat vigorously with a wooden spoon to blend thoroughly. Correct seasoning. Form into patties ¾ inch thick. Cover with waxed paper and refrigerate until ready to use. | Just before sautéing, roll the patties lightly in the flour. Shake off excess flour.

name: Bitokes à la Russe (Hamburgers with Cream Sauce)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: Ingredients for 6 plain beef hamburgers or the preceding flavored hamburgers
60 ml stock or canned beef bouillon
30 ml minced green herbs such as parsley, chives, tarragon, chervil, or parsley only
authorNotes: Sauté the hamburgers in oil and butter as described in the preceding master recipe. Remove them to a hot serving platter. | Pour the fat out of the skillet. Add the stock or bouillon and boil it down rapidly, scraping up coagulated cooking juices, until reduced almost to a syrup. Pour in the cream and boil it down rapidly for a minute or two until it has reduced, and thickened slightly. Season to taste with salt, pepper, nutmeg, and drops of lemon juice. | Off heat, swirl in the butter by half-tablespoons until it is absorbed. Stir in the herbs, spoon the sauce over the hamburgers, and serve.

name: FILET DE BOEUF BRAISÉ PRINCE ALBERT (Braised Filet of Beef Stuffed with Foie Gras and Truffles)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 4 to 6 canned truffles about 1 inch in diameter
175 ml each: finely diced carrots and onions
A small herb bouquet: 2 parsley sprigs, ⅓ bay leaf, 1 ml thyme tied in cheesecloth
30 ml very finely minced shallots or green onions
115 g or 120 ml mousse de foie d’oie (or “block” foie gras , which is much more expensive but also much better)
A 1.4 kg trimmed filet of beef, at least 3 inches in diameter
A 2½-inch strip of fresh pork fat as long as the filet (or strips of blanched bacon )
A heavy, oval, fireproof casserole just large enough to hold the filet
475–710 ml good brown stock or canned beef bouillon (or a very good brown sauce , in which case the starch liaison at the end of the recipe is omitted)
15 ml arrowroot or cornstarch mixed with 30 ml Madeira
authorNotes: Here is a magnificent recipe for an important dinner, and it is not a difficult one in spite of the luxury of its details. We have chosen braised filet because it is more unusual than roast filet. Everything except the actual cooking of the meat may be done in advance as indicated by the asterisk in the recipe. | Braised lettuce and potato balls sautéed in butter would go beautifully with this, and you should accompany it with a fine chateau-bottled red Bordeaux from the Médoc district. See also the other vegetables suggested for steaks . | Cut the truffles in quarters. Place in a small bowl with juice from the can and the Madeira. Cover and marinate while preparing the rest of the ingredients.

name: Marinade, for Filet of Beef
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 120 ml dry white wine or dry white vermouth
45 ml minced shallots or green onions
2 or more canned truffles and their juice
authorNotes: The following marinade is particularly good if you do not have top-quality filet. | Place the raw, trimmed filet in an enameled or pyrex dish or casserole. Pour on the wines and mix in the seasonings, herbs, shallots or onions, and truffles. Cover and marinate for 6 hours or overnight, turning the meat and basting it several times. Drain and dry the meat thoroughly before browning it. Include the marinade, but not the truffles, with your braising liquid. Reserve the truffles for your sauce.

name: POTÉE NORMANDE POT-AU-FEU (Boiled Beef with Pork, Chicken, Sausage, and Vegetables)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A kettle large enough to hold all the ingredients listed
Beef (cooking time 2½ to 3 hours): a 1.8 kg boneless piece of rump pot roast, sirloin tip, bottom round, chuck pot roast, or brisket
Pork (cooking time about 3 hours): a 1.8 kg piece from the butt, picnic, rolled shoulder, or fresh ham
Chicken (cooking time 2½ to 3 hours): a 1.8 kg ready-to-cook stewing hen of good quality
Sausage (cooking time 30 minutes): 0.9 kg lightly smoked country or Polish sausage
Vegetable Garnish (cooking time 1½ hours): carrots, onions, turnips, and, if available, leeks; 1 to 2 of each vegetable per person
authorNotes: Here is a sumptuous family-style boiled dinner which will serve 12 or more, and always makes a great hit with guests. It is brought to the table in its kettle or a reasonable facsimile, looking for all the world like a plain pot-au-feu. The host starts the proceedings as usual by spearing out the beef and placing it on a platter. Then he finds a sausage, and after that a big piece of pork. Finally, to wild acclaim, he brings out a chicken. Two or three sauces may be served, such as a cream sauce with mustard and tomato, an herbal mayonnaise, and a big bowl of the cooking stock. The potée , like all boiled dinners, is easy on the cook because it can simmer quietly by itself for 4 to 5 hours and if it is done before serving time, it can remain in its kettle where it will keep warm for a good hour. | Carrots, turnips, onions, and leeks cook along with the meats. Boiled potatoes, risotto , or buttered noodles are prepared and served separately. A nice, simple red wine goes well: Beaujolais, Bordeaux, or Chianti, or a chilled rosé. | First Choice: Rump Pot Roast— Pointe de Culotte or Aiguillette de Rum-steck

name: BOEUF À LA MODE (Beef Braised in Red Wine)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: An enameled, pyrex, or porcelain bowl just large enough to hold all the ingredients listed
235 ml each: thinly sliced carrots, onions, and celery stalks
A 2.3 kg piece of braising beef trimmed and tied for cooking
1.2 l young red wine with body—Burgundy, Côtes du Rhône, Mâcon, or Chianti
A fireproof casserole or heavy roaster just large enough to hold the meat and braising ingredients
60–90 ml rendered pork fat or cooking oil
One or all of these to give body to the sauce:
0.9 kg quartered carrots braised in butter
24 to 36 small white onions, brown-braised in stock
15 ml arrowroot or cornstarch mixed with 30 ml Madeira or port, if needed
authorNotes: Braised beef is a wonderful party dish; it is not only delicious to smell, look at, and eat, but you have no worries about overdone meat, and you can cook it ahead of time if you need to. The following recipe calls for a 6- to 24-hour marination of the beef in red wine and aromatic vegetables before cooking. If you prefer to omit this step, pour the marinade ingredients into the casserole after browning the meat. | Boeuf à la mode is traditionally garnished with braised carrots and onions, and is usually accompanied by buttered noodles, parsley potatoes, or steamed rice. Other vegetables could be braised lettuce, celery, or leeks, or buttered green peas. Serve with it a good, characterful red wine, such as a Burgundy, Hermitage, Côte Rôtie, or Châteauneuf-du-Pape. | Although it is not essential, beef for braising is usually larded. That is, strips of fresh pork fat are inserted into it, going in the direction of the grain. They baste the interior of the meat as it cooks, and make an attractive design when the meat is sliced. Most butchers will lard the meat for you.

name: Pièce de Boeuf à la Cuillère (Minced Braised Beef Served in a Beef Shell)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 225 g fresh mushrooms, quartered and sautéed in oil and butter with 15 ml minced shallots or green onions
175 ml (115 g) lean, minced, boiled ham
355 ml sauce from the braised beef
2 eggs beaten in a small bowl with 5 ml water and a pinch of salt
475 ml fine, dry, white bread crumbs tossed with 120 ml grated Parmesan cheese
A rack set on a roasting pan
A vegetable garnish such as braised onions and carrots, and sautéed potatoes, OR baked tomatoes, and green beans or braised lettuce
The rest of the sauce from the braised beef poured into a gravy bowl
authorNotes: From la vieille cuisine française comes an unusual way to serve braised beef for a dinner party. First the beef is braised; then meat is cut out from its top and center to leave a shell of beef which is crumbed and browned in the oven. The removed beef is chopped, combined with sautéed mushrooms, minced ham, and sauce, and is returned to the shell for serving. A nice thing about this recipe is that all may be readied in advance for a 5- to 10-minute heating just before serving. | Braise the beef and make the sauce following the master recipe, but simmer the meat only until it is almost tender, about 3 hours for a 5-pound piece. It should still be firm enough to hold its shape when the shell is made. Choose a solid, lean piece of top round with no muscle separations. It should weigh at least 5 pounds and be cut into an even rectangular block about 5 inches wide and 5 inches deep. | When the meat is done, remove it from the sauce. Place it under a board and a 2-pound weight to preserve its shape for about an hour while it cools to tepid. Then trim it, if necessary, into a neat rectangle. Hollow out the center, leaving an open-topped rectangular trough or shell of meat with sides and bottom half an inch thick. Chop the removed meat, including any trimmed-off bits, into ⅛-inch pieces. Place them in a large, heavy-bottomed, enameled saucepan, skillet, or casserole.

name: BOEUF BOURGUIGNON BOEUF À LA BOURGUIGNONNE (Beef Stew in Red Wine, with Bacon, Onions, and Mushrooms)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A 9- to 10-inch fireproof casserole 3 inches deep
15 ml olive oil or cooking oil
1.4 kg lean stewing beef cut into 2-inch cubes (see preceding list of cuts)
710 ml of a full-bodied, young red wine such as one of those suggested for serving, or a Chianti
475–710 ml brown beef stock or canned beef bouillon
18 to 24 small white onions, brown-braised in stock
455 g quartered fresh mushrooms sautéed in butter
authorNotes: As is the case with most famous dishes, there are more ways than one to arrive at a good boeuf bourguignon. Carefully done, and perfectly flavored, it is certainly one of the most delicious beef dishes concocted by man, and can well be the main course for a buffet dinner. Fortunately you can prepare it completely ahead, even a day in advance, and it only gains in flavor when reheated. | Boiled potatoes are traditionally served with this dish. Buttered noodles or steamed rice may be substituted. If you also wish a green vegetable, buttered peas would be your best choice. Serve with the beef a fairly full-bodied, young red wine, such as Beaujolais, Côtes du Rhône, Bordeaux-St. Émilion, or Burgundy. | Remove rind, and cut bacon into lardons (sticks, ¼ inch thick and 1½ inches long). Simmer rind and bacon for 10 minutes in 1½ quarts of water. Drain and dry.

name: CARBONNADES À LA FLAMANDE (Beef and Onions Braised in Beer)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A 1.4 kg piece of lean beef from the chuck roast or rump
30–45 ml rendered fresh pork fat or good cooking oil
680 g or 6 cups of sliced onions
A 9- to 10-inch fireproof casserole about 3½ inches deep
235 ml strong beef stock or canned beef bouillon
1 large herb bouquet: 6 parsley sprigs, 1 bay leaf, and 2 ml thyme tied in cheesecloth
20 ml arrowroot or cornstarch blended with 30 ml wine vinegar
authorNotes: Beer is typical for the Belgian braise, and gives a quite different character to beef than the red wine of the bourguignon. A bit of brown sugar masks the beer’s slightly bitter quality, and a little vinegar at the end gives character. Serve this with parsley potatoes or buttered noodles, a green salad, and beer. | Preheat oven to 325 degrees. Cut the beef into slices about 2 by 4 inches across and ½ inch thick. Dry on paper towels. Put a 1 / 16 -inch layer of fat or oil in the skillet and heat until almost smoking. Brown the beef slices quickly, a few at a time, and set them aside. | Reduce heat to moderate. Stir the onions into the fat in the skillet, adding more fat if necessary, and brown the onions lightly for about 10 minutes, stirring frequently. Remove from heat, season with salt and pepper, and stir in the garlic.

name: PAUPIETTES DE BOEUF ROULADES DE BOEUF PETITES BALLOTINES DE BOEUF (Braised Stuffed Beef Rolls)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 170 g lean pork ground with 170 g lean veal and 85 g fresh pork fat, making about 355 ml ground meat
1.1 kg lean beef (top round or chuck) cut into 18 cross-grain slices ¼ inch thick and about 3 inches in diameter
30–45 ml rendered pork fat or good cooking oil
A heavy fireproof casserole about 10 inches in diameter and 2½ to 3 inches deep
235 ml dry white wine or dry white vermouth
355 ml brown stock or canned beef bouillon
A 4-inch square of fresh pork rind, bacon rind or salt-pork rind, simmered 10 minutes in a quart of water, then drained
1 large herb bouquet plus 2 cloves of garlic: 6 parsley sprigs, 1 bay leaf, 2 ml thyme, and the garlic tied together in cheesecloth
15 ml prepared mustard of the strong Dijon type blended with 80 ml whipping cream
authorNotes: Paupiettes are thin slices of beef wrapped around a filling, and braised in wine and stock with herbs and aromatic vegetables. Although they follow the same general pattern as other fricassees of beef, their pork and veal stuffing gives them a special character. Paupiettes can be cooked in advance, and any leftovers may be reheated or may be served cold. Accompany hot paupiettes with rice, risotto , or noodles, and a garniture of sautéed mushrooms, braised onions, and carrots, or with buttered green peas or green beans, broiled tomatoes, and French bread. Serve a good simple red wine such as Beaujolais, Côtes du Rhône, Chianti, or a rosé. | For about 18 paupiettes serving 6 people | Cook the onions slowly in butter for 7 to 8 minutes until they are tender but not browned. Scrape them into the mixing bowl.

name: BOEUF À LA CATALANE (Beef Stew with Rice, Onions, and Tomatoes)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A 2.84 l fireproof casserole about 3 inches deep
1.4 kg lean stewing beef cut into squares 2½ inches across and 1 inch thick (see cuts listed )
235 ml clean, unwashed, raw white rice
235 ml dry white wine or dry white vermouth
475–710 ml beef stock or canned beef bouillon
455 g ripe, red tomatoes, peeled, seeded, juiced, and chopped (this will make about 355 ml tomato pulp)
Stock or canned bouillon if necessary
235 ml (115 g) grated Swiss or Parmesan cheese
authorNotes: Here is a hearty dish from the Spanish-Mediterranean corner of France. Serve it with a green salad, French bread, and a strong, young red wine. | Preheat oven to 325 degrees. | Remove rind and cut bacon into lardons (1½-inch strips, ⅜ of an inch thick.) Simmer in 1 quart of water for 10 minutes. Drain, dry, and brown lightly in oil in the skillet. Remove with a slotted spoon to the casserole.

name: DAUBE DE BOEUF ESTOUFFADE DE BOEUF TERRINE DE BOEUF (Casserole of Beef with Wine and Vegetables — Hot or Cold)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 1.4 kg lean stewing beef cut into 2½-inch squares, 1 inch thick ( beef cuts are listed )
355 ml dry white wine, dry white vermouth, or red wine
Optional: 60 ml brandy, eau de vie , or gin
225 g lean bacon cut into 1-inch slices ¼ inch thick and 2 inches long approximately
680 g ripe, red tomatoes, peeled, seeded, juiced, and chopped (this will make about 530 ml tomato pulp)
A 5- to 5.7 l fireproof casserole 3½ inches deep
235 ml sifted flour on a plate
235–475 ml beef stock or canned beef bouillon
authorNotes: Daube comes from daubière , a covered casserole. Estouffade is a stifling or smothering, in a covered casserole. Almost every region of France has its own daubes, estouffades , and terrines. Some of them are for a whole piece of braised beef; others are like a boeuf bourguignon. In many the meat is larded, and in most it is marinated in wine with vegetables before the cooking begins. Here is a savory, country-style daube , an informal main dish to serve with boiled potatoes, risotto , or noodles, a green salad, and a simple red wine or a chilled rosé. | Note: We have not directed that the meat be larded, but you may do so if you wish, by inserting two ¼-inch strips of larding pork or blanched bacon through each piece of meat. You may also omit the marination of the meat, and add all the marinade ingredients to the casserole with the beef. | Place the beef in the bowl and mix with the wine, optional spirits, olive oil, seasonings, herbs, and vegetables. Cover and marinate at least 3 hours (6 if refrigerated), stirring up frequently.

name: Daube de Boeuf à la Provençale (Casserole of Beef with Garlic and Anchovy Sauce)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 10 flat anchovy filets packed in olive oil
45 ml olive oil from the anchovy can and/or plain olive oil
authorNotes: This is the same daube given a Provençal flavoring at the end. Any cold leftovers are delicious, served with a green salad and French bread. Follow the master recipe with these additions: | Using a fork, mash the anchovies and capers to a paste in a bowl. Beat in the other ingredients. After the daube has cooked for 2½ hours remove it from the oven and skim off the fat. Pour on the anchovy mixture and baste the beef with the cooking juices in the casserole. Cover and return to oven until the meat is tender.

name: SAUTÉ DE BOEUF À LA PARISIENNE (Beef Sauté with Cream and Mushroom Sauce)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 30 ml butter and 15 ml good cooking oil
45 ml minced shallots or green onions
1 ml salt and a pinch of pepper
1.1 kg filet of beef; the tenderloin butt and the tail of the filet are usually used (see illustrations )
30 ml butter and 15 ml cooking oil, more if needed
60 ml Madeira (best choice), or dry white vermouth
175 ml good brown stock or canned beef bouillon
10 ml cornstarch blended with 15 ml of the cream
authorNotes: This sauté of beef is good to know about if you have to entertain important guests in a hurry. It consists of small pieces of filet sautéed quickly to a nice brown outside and a rosy center, and served in a sauce. The following recipe can easily be prepared in 30 minutes, or in less than half the time if the meat has been sliced and the mushrooms sautéed ahead. In the variations at the end of the recipe, all the sauce ingredients may be prepared in advance. If the whole dish is cooked ahead of time, be very careful indeed in its reheating that the beef does not overcook. The cream and mushroom sauce here is a French version of beef Stroganoff, but less tricky as it uses fresh rather than sour cream, so you will not run into the problem of curdled sauce. | Serve the beef in a casserole, or on a platter surrounded with steamed rice, risotto , or potato balls sautéed in butter. Buttered green peas or beans could accompany it, and a good red Bordeaux wine. | Following directions sauté the mushrooms in the skillet in hot butter and oil for 4 to 5 minutes to brown them lightly. Stir in the shallots or onions, and cook for a minute longer. Season the mushrooms, and scrape them into a side dish.

name: Sauté de Boeuf à la Bourguignonne (Beef Sauté with Red Wine, Mushrooms, Bacon, and Onions)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 1.1 kg filet of beef prepared and sautéed as in preceding master recipe
355 ml brown stock or canned beef bouillon
15 ml flour mashed to a paste with 15 ml butter— beurre manié
225 g sliced mushrooms sautéed in butter
authorNotes: Sauté the beef and set it aside. Cut the blanched bacon into 1-inch strips ¼ of an inch thick. Brown lightly in the sauté skillet and pour out fat. Add rest of ingredients at left and slowly boil down by half. | Remove from heat and beat in the flour-butter paste. Simmer for 1 minute, beating with wire whip. | Add onions and mushrooms and simmer 2 minutes. Correct seasoning. Season the sautéed beef, and arrange it and the sauce, bacon, and vegetables in the serving casserole.

name: Sauté de Boeuf à la Provençale (Beef Sauté with Fresh Tomato Sauce, Olives, and Herbs)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 80 ml dry white wine or dry white vermouth
475 ml fresh tomato purée with garlic and herbs
80 ml pitted black olives, preferably the dryish Mediterranean type
30 ml mixed fresh green herbs, or parsley
authorNotes: Cut and sauté the beef as described in the preceding master recipe. Remove to a side dish. | Pour the fat out of the sauté skillet and pour in the wine. Boil it down rapidly until reduced to 2 tablespoons, scraping up coagulated sauté juices. Add the tomato purée and simmer a moment. Then add the sauteed beef and reheat without simmering. Decorate with olives and herbs or parsley, and serve immediately.

name: GIGOT DE PRÉ-SALÉ RÔTI (Roast Leg of Lamb)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 60 ml rendered fresh pork or beef fat, or a mixture of melted butter and cooking oil
A roasting pan 1½ inches deep and just large enough to hold the meat
A rack to fit the pan
A bulb baster or long-handled spoon
Optional: 3 to 6 cloves un-peeled garlic, added to pan ½ hour before end of cooking
235 ml stock, brown lamb stock, or canned beef bouillon
authorNotes: Preheat oven to 450 degrees. | Prepare the lamb for cooking as described in the preceding paragraphs, and wipe it dry with paper towels. | Brush the lamb with melted fat or butter and oil. Place it on the rack in the roasting pan and set in the upper third of the preheated oven. Turn and baste it every 4 to 5 minutes for 15 to 20 minutes, or until it has browned lightly on all sides. This sears the outside of the meat and prevents its juices from bursting out.

name: Sauce Spéciale à l’Ail pour Gigot (Garlic Sauce for Roast Lamb)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A saucepan containing 1 l of cold water
235 ml brown lamb stock, beef stock, or canned beef bouillon
A sieve, a bowl, and a wooden spoon, or an electric blender
authorNotes: This very good sauce uses a whole head of garlic which, after two blanch-ings and a long simmering, becomes tamed and develops a delicious flavor. | For 1½ to 2 cups of sauce | Separate the garlic cloves. Bring them to the boil in the saucepan and boil 30 seconds. Drain and peel. Set again in cold water, bring to the boil, and drain.

name: Gigot à la Moutarde (Herbal Mustard Coating for Roast Lamb)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
authorNotes: When lamb is brushed with this mixture, garlic slivers and herbal stuffings are not necessary, and the lamb becomes a beautiful brown as it roasts. | For a 6-lb. leg of lamb | Blend the mustard, soy sauce, garlic, herbs, and ginger together in a bowl. Beat in the olive oil by droplets to make a mayonnaise-like cream.

name: Farce aux Herbes (Garlic and Herb Stuffing)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 30 ml minced shallots or green onions
authorNotes: Mix all the ingredients together in a bowl. Spread the mixture on the lamb. Roll and tie as described in the preceding instructions.

name: Farce de Porc (Pork and Herb Stuffing)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 235 ml fresh white bread crumbs (French or homemade-type bread)
120 ml lukewarm stock or canned beef bouillon
225 g (235 ml) lean, fresh pork ground with 115 g (120 ml) fresh pork fat
1 ml ground rosemary, sage, or thyme
authorNotes: Cook the onions and butter slowly together in a small saucepan until tender but not browned. Scrape into a mixing bowl. | Soak the bread crumbs in stock or bouillon for 5 minutes. Drain in a sieve and press out as much liquid as you can. Save the liquid for your sauce, and place the bread crumbs in the mixing bowl. | Add the rest of the ingredients to the mixing bowl and beat vigorously with a wooden spoon until well mixed. Sauté a teaspoonful until cooked through, and taste for seasoning. Add more seasonings to the stuffing if you feel it necessary.

name: Farce aux Rognons (Rice and Kidney Stuffing)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 160 ml white stock or canned chicken bouillon
2 ml ground rosemary, sage, or thyme
4 lamb kidneys, or a mixture of lamb kidneys, heart, and liver making about 235 ml in all
authorNotes: In a small, heavy-bottomed saucepan, cook the onions in butter for 4 to 5 minutes until tender but not browned. Add the rice and stir over low heat for 2 to 3 minutes until it turns a milky color. Pour in the stock or chicken bouillon, bring to a boil, cover, and simmer not too slowly for 15 minutes without stirring, at which point the liquid should be absorbed and the rice almost tender. It will finish cooking in the lamb. | Fluff in the herbs, spice, pepper, and garlic with a fork. Add salt to taste. | Dry the meat in paper towels, and leave the pieces whole. Sauté quickly in hot butter and oil to brown very lightly, leaving the interior of the meat rosy. Cut into ⅛-inch slices. Season lightly with salt and pepper, and fold into the rice.

name: Farce Duxelles (Ham and Mushroom Stuffing)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 120 ml minced onions, cooked in butter
115 g minced fresh mushrooms, cooked in butter
60 ml finely minced fresh pork fat (or ham fat)

name: Farce aux Olives (Olive and Ground Lamb Stuffing)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 120 ml minced onions, cooked in butter
235 ml stale white bread crumbs soaked in stock or bouillon and squeezed dry
12 pitted black Greek olives, simmered 10 minutes in 1 l of water, drained, and chopped
Salt, pepper, herbs, allspice, and garlic

name: Farce Mentonnaise (Salmon and Anchovy Stuffing)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 6 drained, mashed anchovies (packed in olive oil)
175 ml minced onions, sautéed in butter
authorNotes: An unlikely combination, but a good one

name: GIGOT OU ÉPAULE DE PRÉ-SALÉ BRAISÉ (aux Haricots) (Braised Leg or Shoulder of Lamb— with Beans)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A 6- to 3.2 kg leg or 4- to 2.3 kg shoulder of lamb, boned, and stuffed, if you wish, with one of the preceding fillings
The lamb bones, sawed or chopped
45–60 ml rendered fresh pork fat or cooking oil
A heavy fireproof casserole or covered roaster just large enough to hold all ingredients
475 ml of dry white wine, or red wine, or 355 ml dry white vermouth
710 ml to 1 l beef stock or canned beef bouillon
590 ml dry white Great Northern beans
authorNotes: Braising is a succulent way to do almost mature lamb or young mutton, particularly if it has been stuffed with any of the preceding suggestions. Beans may finish their cooking with the lamb, and will absorb a fine flavor from the braising liquid. If you do not wish to include them, serve with the lamb a purée of lentils or chestnuts, mashed potatoes, rice, or risotto. Other vegetables to serve with braised lamb are green beans, peas, Brussels sprouts, baked tomatoes, or a garniture of glazed carrots, turnips, onions, and sautéed mushrooms. A fairly full red wine goes well—Beaujolais, Bordeaux—St. Émilion, Côtes du Rhône, or Burgundy. | Almost mature lamb or young mutton is usually braised 40 to 50 minutes per pound, long enough for the meat, its stuffing, and the braising liquid to exchange flavors. This makes 3½ to 4 hours for a leg, and around 2½ hours for a shoulder. Boned and stuffed lamb will usually take an hour longer. The meat is done when a fork pierces it easily. You may, if you wish, cut the time in half, and cook the meat only until the thermometer indicates 150 degrees for medium rare, or 160 to 165 degrees for well done; in this case, there will be little exchange of flavor between the various elements. | Beans. If you are to use dry white beans, their soaking and precooking will take a good two hours. This is done while the lamb is braising.

name: Marinade Cuite (Cooked Wine Marinade)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A 2.84 l enameled saucepan with cover
1.4 l full-bodied, young red wine: Mâcon, Côtes du Rhône, Beaujolais, Burgundy, Chianti
2 ml juniper berries, if available, or 60 ml gin
authorNotes: Cook the vegetables slowly in the olive oil in the covered saucepan for 5 minutes without allowing them to brown. | Add the wine, the vinegar, and all the rest of the ingredients. Simmer, partially covered, for 20 minutes. Allow the marinade to cool completely before using it.

name: Marinade au Laurier (Uncooked Wine Marinade with Bay Leaves)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
authorNotes: This alternative marinade needs no cooking, and is just poured over the lamb.

name: Marinating and Roasting the Lamb
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A 7- to 3.6 kg leg of well aged lamb or young mutton
authorNotes: Prepare the lamb for cooking as described earlier. It may be boned if you wish. | Place it in an enameled, pyrex, porcelain, or stainless steel bowl, roaster, or tub just large enough to hold it. Pour the marinade over it. Turn and baste the lamb 3 or 4 times a day for 4 to 5 days at room temperature, for 6 to 8 days if it is refrigerated. | Drain the lamb for half an hour or more on a rack. Just before roasting, dry it thoroughly with paper towels. Following directions in the master recipe for roast lamb on this page , baste it with fat and sear it for 15 to 20 minutes in a 450-degree oven, then roast it at 350 degrees to a medium rare, 147 to 150 degrees on the meat thermometer.

name: GIGOT À L’ANGLAISE (Boiled Leg of Lamb with Onion, Caper, or Tomato Sauce)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A 4- to 2.3 kg whole leg of genuine spring lamb
A kettle of rapidly boiling water, large enough to hold the lamb completely submerged
5 ml salt per quart of water
710 ml of one of the following sauces:
authorNotes: The English, according to the French, boil everything, thus anything boiled (or poached) and served simply is à l’anglaise in French cuisine. This method is truly a delicious and utterly simple way to cook a leg of lamb; simmer it in salted water until it is done, and it can stay quietly in that hot water for an hour or more before serving. But you do need a young tender “genuine spring leg of lamb” for this because the fat of older lamb penetrates the meat, giving it a strong taste. (See lamb discussion .) | For a family-style vegetable garnish, carrots, turnips, onions, leeks, and potatoes may be cooked for an hour with the lamb; tie them in cheesecloth bundles for easy removal. For a more formal array, here are some other suggestions to be cooked separately and combined as you wish: | Purée of turnips and potatoes

name: NAVARIN PRINTANIER (Lamb Stew with Spring Vegetables)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 1.4 kg lamb stew meat (see list preceding recipe)
30–60 ml rendered fresh pork fat or cooking oil
A fireproof covered casse-role large enough to hold the meat, and all the vegetables to come
475–710 ml brown lamb- or beef-stock or canned beef bouillon
340 g ripe, red tomatoes, peeled, seeded, juiced, and chopped (235 ml of pulp); or 45 ml tomato paste
12 to 18 peeled white onions about 1 inch in diameter
115 g or about 235 ml green beans cut into ½-inch pieces
authorNotes: Navarin printanier , a most delectable lamb stew with its carrots, onions, turnips, potatoes, peas, and green beans, is presumably done in the spring when all the vegetables are young and tender. But as it can be made any time of the year, it is not a seasonal dish any more thanks to deep freezing. Frozen peas and beans are discussed here . The written recipe is long as each detail is important if the navarin is to taste like a French masterpiece. But none of the steps is difficult and everything except the addition of the green vegetables at the very end may be made ready in the morning. The stew can then be finished in 10 to 15 minutes just before dinner time. | With the stew serve hot French bread, and a red Beaujolais or Bordeaux wine, a chilled rosé , or a fairly full-bodied, dry, chilled white wine such as a Mâcon, Hermitage, or one of the lesser Burgundies. | Preheat oven to 450 degrees.

name: MOUSSAKA (Lamb and Eggplant Mold)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: Ingredients for 710 ml of tomato sauce
2.3 kg of eggplant (five 455 g eggplants if possible, each 7 to 8 inches long)
15 ml olive oil A 2.84 l mixing bowl
30 ml minced shallots or green onions
A 1.9 l cylindrical mold (preferably a charlotte ) 4 inches high and 7 inches in diameter
160 ml thick brown sauce (preferably Numbers I or II; but the quick sauce, Number III, can be used)
authorNotes: Lamb can hardly be considered a leftover when it receives this elaborate treatment. A mold is lined with the skins of cooked eggplant, and filled with a carefully seasoned mixture of cooked lamb, eggplant, and mushrooms. It presents itself after baking and unmolding as a shiny, dark purple cylinder surrounded with a deep red tomato sauce. It is delicious either hot or cold. | Serve the moussaka with steamed rice or risotto , and buttered green beans or a green salad. A fairly full-bodied, dry, chilled white wine such as a Mâcon or Hermitage goes well with it. Moussaka also makes a handsome cold dish served with tomato salad and French bread. | Preheat oven to 400 degrees, in time to bake the eggplant.

name: VEAU POÊLÉ (Casserole-roasted Veal)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A 1.4 kg roast of veal, boned and tied
A heavy fireproof casserole just large enough to hold the veal easily
A medium herb bouquet: 4 parsley sprigs, ½ bay leaf, and 1 ml thyme tied in cheesecloth
2 strips of fat bacon, simmered for 10 minutes in 1 l of water, rinsed, drained, and dried
authorNotes: This is a very simple and savory recipe for veal. The meat renders a certain amount of juice as it roasts, so no special sauce is necessary if you are content with the French system of a spoonful per serving to moisten the meat. For more sauce, see the suggestion at the end of the recipe. | Preheat oven to 325 degrees. | Dry the veal on paper towels.

name: Veau Poêlé à la Matignon (Casserole-roasted Veal with Diced Vegetables)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 235 ml good brown stock or canned beef bouillon
15 ml arrowroot or cornstarch blended with 30 ml Madeira
Optional: 1 diced canned truffle and juice from the can
authorNotes: Follow the master recipe for roast veal, but instead of slicing the carrots and onions, cut them into ⅛-inch dice. After browning the veal, remove it and cook the vegetables slowly in butter for 10 minutes. Then add the Madeira and boil it down rapidly until it has almost completely evaporated. Return the meat to the casserole and spread half the vegetables over it, leaving the rest in the bottom of the casserole. Proceed with the recipe. | When the veal is done and has been removed from the casserole, add the stock or bouillon and simmer for 5 minutes. Then remove the herb bouquet and bacon, and degrease the sauce. Pour in the starch mixture and optional truffles and truffle juice. Simmer for 5 minutes. Correct seasoning. Sauce should be lightly thickened. | Off heat, and just before serving, add the butter by bits, swirling the sauce in the casserole until each addition has been absorbed. Ladle a spoonful of sauce and vegetables over the meat. Pour the rest into the warmed sauceboat.

name: VEAU PRINCE ORLOFF (Veal Gratinéed with Onions and Mushrooms)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A 2.3 kg boned and tied roast of veal
A 6- to 1.9 l, heavy, fireproof casserole with cover
225 g finely minced fresh mushrooms (makes 475 ml)
45 ml minced shallots or green onions
An 8-inch enameled skillet or heavy-bottomed enameled saucepan
710 ml boiling liquid: the veal-roasting juices plus milk
60 ml whipping cream, more if needed
A lightly buttered, fireproof serving platter 1½ inches deep and about 14 inches long
authorNotes: This delicious creation is fine for a party as it may be prepared in the morning and reheated in the evening. The veal is cooked and sliced, re-formed with a spreading of onions and mushrooms between each slice, and covered with a light cheese sauce. It is reheated and browned before serving. Braised lettuce or endive go particularly well with this roast, and either a red Bordeaux wine from the Médoc district or a chilled white Burgundy. | Brown the veal, and roast it for about 2½ hours (to 175 degrees on a meat thermometer) in a covered casserole as described in the master recipe, this page . Then allow the meat to rest for 30 minutes at room temperature; it will be carved when the preparations which follow are ready. | Strain the roasting juices into the saucepan and skim off fat. Boil juices down rapidly to reduce to 1 cup. They will go into your velouté sauce later.

name: VEAU SYLVIE (Veal Roasted with Ham and Cheese)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A 2.3 kg boneless roast of veal, as solid as possible and in a loaf shape
A large herb bouquet with peppercorns: 6 parsley sprigs, 1 bay leaf, 2 ml thyme, and 6 peppercorns tied in cheesecloth
6 or more large slices of boiled ham 1 / 16 inch thick
