12 or more large slices of Swiss cheese 1 / 16 inch thick
A covered fireproof casserole large enough to hold the meat
2 strips of fat bacon simmered for 10 minutes in 1 l of water, rinsed, drained, and dried
authorNotes: In this recipe, deep slits are cut in the roast of veal from one end to the other, and the meat is marinated in brandy, Madeira, and aromatic vegetables, then stuffed with slices of ham and cheese. It is roasted in a covered casserole and, when sliced, the ham and cheese appear to have melted into the veal. Serve with the roast any of the wine and vegetable suggestions . Veau Sylvie also makes a good cold roast just as it is or glazed with aspic. | The French cut for this would be a noix or top round of veal, as it is a long, cylindrical piece with no muscle separations. Rump, round, and sirloin are more difficult to handle but can be managed perfectly well; a boned section from the rib roast would be the best alternative, though more expensive. | So that the roast may be stuffed, deep slits are cut from one end to the other to make it open like the leaves of a book: make a series of deep, parallel cuts 1 to 1½ inches apart starting at the top and going with the grain the length of the meat from one end of the roast to the other, and to within ½ inch of the bottom of the roast. You will thus have 3 or 4 thick pieces of meat which are free at the top and sides, but which are all attached together at the bottom of the roast. If your meat contains many muscle separations it will look very messy and uneven at this point, but the roast is tied into shape later.

name: SAUTÉ DE VEAU MARENGO (Brown Veal Stew with Tomatoes and Mushrooms)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 1.4 kg veal stew meat from the preceding list, cut into 55 g, 2-inch pieces
30–45 ml olive oil, more if needed
475 ml dry white wine or dry white vermouth
455 g firm, ripe, red tomatoes peeled, seeded, juiced, and roughly chopped (355 ml)
225 g fresh button mushrooms or quartered larger mushrooms
5 ml cornstarch mixed with 15 ml water, if needed
30–45 ml minced fresh tarragon, basil, or parsley
authorNotes: The flavors of Provence go into this uncomplicated and hearty dish. Steamed rice or noodles go well with it, and green peas or beans. Serve a chilled rosé wine, or a strong, young, white wine. As with all stews, this one may be cooked in advance and reheated just before serving. | Preheat oven to 325 degrees. | Dry the veal on paper towels. Heat the oil in the skillet until almost smoking. Then brown the meat, a few pieces at a time, and arrange the browned pieces in the casserole.

name: BLANQUETTE DE VEAU À L’ANCIENNE (Veal Stew with Onions and Mushrooms)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 1.4 kg veal stew meat cut into 55 g, 2-inch pieces ( cuts are listed )
A 3- to 3.8 l, fireproof, enameled casserole
1.2–1.4 l cold white stock or good canned chicken broth.
A medium herb bouquet and 2 celery stalks: 8 parsley stems (not the leaves), ½ bay leaf, 2 ml thyme, and 2 medium celery stalks tied in cheesecloth
18 to 24 peeled white onions about 1 inch in diameter
120 ml of stock dipped from the simmering veal casserole
18 to 24 fresh mushroom caps about 1 inch in diameter, tossed with 15 ml lemon juice
authorNotes: Blanquette de veau , a much-loved stew in France, is veal simmered in a lightly seasoned white stock. It is served in a sauce velouté made from the veal cooking stock and enriched with cream and egg yolks. A blanquette is certainly not difficult to make, and except for the cream and egg yolk liaison at the end, which takes less than 10 minutes, all of it may be cooked in advance. However, as it is supposed to be a lovely and delicate dish, it should really not be attempted unless you can find veal of good quality and of the palest pink color. | Serve it with noodles or rice, or boiled or mashed potatoes. No other vegetables are needed with the mushrooms and onions, but you could include green peas, artichoke hearts, or baked cucumbers. A red Bordeaux-Médoc or chilled rosé wine would go well. | As veal comes to the simmer it releases a tremendous amount of gray-brown scum which must be removed by one means or another. American veal, probably because it is younger than most French veal, seems particularly scum-productive. You can skim continually while the veal simmers for the first 30 to 40 minutes. You can let it simmer for 10 minutes, then remove the veal and wash it rapidly in cold water, wash out the casserole, strain the stock through several thicknesses of damp cheesecloth, and continue with the recipe. Or you can adopt the following blanching process, which is the simplest. As long as all the scum is removed, it makes no difference which method you use; pick the one which best suits your predilections and prejudices.

name: ESCALOPES DE VEAU À LA CRÈME (Sautéed Veal Scallops with Mushrooms and Cream)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 12 veal scallops prepared according to the preceding directions
30 ml butter and 15 ml oil, more as necessary
45 ml minced shallots or green onions
120 ml dry white wine or 80 ml dry white vermouth or Madeira
160 ml brown stock or canned beef bouillon
355 ml whipping cream 5 ml arrowroot or cornstarch blended with 15 ml water
authorNotes: This recipe for veal scallops makes a perfect main course for a chic little luncheon. If you are reasonably quick you can complete it in 30 minutes or less, and you may prepare it in advance; it only needs a 5-minute heating before it is ready to eat. Serve with it buttered rice or risotto , green beans, peas, or braised endive, and a chilled white Burgundy wine. | Dry the scallops thoroughly on paper towels. The meat will not brown if it is damp. | Place the butter and oil in the skillet over moderately high heat. When you see that the butter foam has almost subsided, arrange 3 or 4 pieces of veal in the skillet. Do not crowd them together. Sauté on one side for a minute or so regulating heat so fat is very hot but is not burning. Turn, and sauté the meat on its other side. (Each scallop should be lightly browned and cooked to the point where the juices have turned from rose to yellow. It is done when it has just become springy rather than squashy to the pressure of your finger.) Remove the scallops to a dish, and continue with the rest in the same manner, adding more butter and oil as needed.

name: Escalopes de Veau à l’Estragon (Sautéed Veal Scallops with Brown Tarragon Sauce)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 45 ml minced shallots or green onions
120 ml dry white wine or 80 ml dry white vermouth
15 ml fragrant tarragon, fresh or dried
235 ml brown sauce , or 235 ml brown stock or canned beef bouillon plus 15 ml arrowroot or cornstarch blended with 15 ml water
15 ml fresh minced tarragon or minced parsley
authorNotes: Rice, noodles, or sautéed potatoes go well with this, and green peas or beans. Accompany with a red Bordeaux wine. | Prepare and sauté the veal as described in the preceding master recipe. Place on a plate while completing the following sauce. | Cook the shallots or onions for a minute in the sauté skillet, then deglaze the skillet with the wine and tarragon, reducing the liquid to 2 or 3 spoonfuls. Add the brown sauce, or the stock and starch mixture, and boil for 2 to 3 minutes until the sauce has reduced and thickened lightly. Correct seasoning.

name: Escalopes de Veau Chasseur (Sautéed Veal Scallops with Mushrooms and Tomatoes)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 60 ml minced shallots or green onions
340 g firm, ripe, red tomatoes, peeled, seeded, juiced, and chopped (makes 235 ml of pulp)
120 ml white wine or 80 ml dry white vermouth
120 ml brown sauce , or 120 ml brown stock or canned beef bouillon plus 15 ml arrowroot or cornstarch blended with 15 ml water
30 ml fresh minced tarragon, basil, or parsley
authorNotes: Serve rice, noodles, or sautéed potatoes with this, green peas or beans, or sautéed eggplant, and a Beaujolais or chilled rosé wine. | Prepare and sauté the veal as described in the preceding master recipe. Place the meat on a plate while completing the following sauce. | Cook the shallots or onions for 1 minute in the skillet. Stir in the tomatoes, garlic, seasonings, and herbs. Cover the skillet and simmer for 5 minutes. Pour in the wine, and the brown sauce or the stock and starch. Boil rapidly for 4 to 5 minutes until the sauce has reduced and thickened. Correct seasoning and remove from heat.

name: CÔTES DE VEAU AUX HERBES (Veal Chops Braised with Herbs)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A heavy, 10- to 12-inch fireproof casserole with cover6 large veal chops cut 1 inch thick
30 ml butter and 15 ml oil, more if needed
45 ml minced shallots or green onions
120 ml dry white wine or dry white vermouth
5 ml mixed basil and thyme, or tarragon
60 ml stock, canned bouillon, or cream
authorNotes: This is an excellent basic recipe for all veal chops, whether they are served with a plain deglazing sauce made from the pan juices, or with any of the suggestions listed at the end of the recipe. Sautéed potatoes, broiled tomatoes, string beans, and a chilled rosé wine would go well with chops prepared in the following manner. | Cooking Note: For 2 or 3 chops, the final cooking may be done on top of the stove in a covered skillet. For 6 chops, it is easier to finish cooking them in the oven. | Preheat oven to 325 degrees.

name: FRICADELLES DE VEAU À LA NIÇOISE (Veal Patties with Tomatoes, Onions, and Herbs)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 2 medium tomatoes, peeled, seeded, juiced, and chopped
235 ml stale white bread crumbs and 120 ml milk
OR, grind 120 ml of cooked rice with the veal
455 g or 2 cups lean raw veal, ground with 55 g (120 ml) of boiled ham and 55 g (120 ml) of ham fat or fresh pork fat
120 ml sifted flour spread on a dish
1 to 2 skillets each containing 30 ml butter and 15 ml oil
160 ml brown stock or canned beef bouillon
authorNotes: Onions, garlic, and tomatoes are particularly good mixed with ground veal. If you happened to have the remains of a ratatouille (eggplant and tomato casserole) , half a cup of it could replace the tomatoes and onions in the following recipe. | Cook the onions slowly with the butter in a small skillet for 8 to 10 minutes, until they are tender but not browned. | Add the tomatoes, and other ingredients. Cover and cook slowly for 5 minutes. Uncover, raise heat, and boil rapidly until the tomato juices have almost entirely evaporated. Scrape into the mixing bowl.

name: Fricadelles de Veau à la Crème (Veal Patties with Cream and Herb Sauce)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 120 ml dry white wine, dry white vermouth, or stock
5 ml fresh minced tarragon, basil, or parsley
authorNotes: Cook the patties and remove them to a hot platter as directed in the master recipe. Add the tarragon or basil and wine or stock to the degreased skillet. Boil down liquid to 3 tablespoons, scraping up coagulated cooking juices. Then pour in the cream and boil it down rapidly to reduce and thicken it lightly. Off heat, swirl in the butter by bits, then swirl in the herbs. Pour over the patties.

name: Fricadelles de Veau Duxelles (Veal Patties with Mushrooms)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
authorNotes: Squeeze the mushrooms, a handful at a time, in the corner of a towel to extract their juice. When the minced onions in the master recipe are tender, add the mushrooms. Raise heat and sauté for 4 to 5 minutes. Then add the tomatoes and proceed with the recipe.

name: Fricadelles de Veau Mentonnaise (Veal Patties with Tuna and Anchovies)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 120 ml drained and mashed canned tuna fish
6 canned anchovy filets drained and mashed, or 15 ml anchovy paste
authorNotes: This Italian and Mediterranean combination is especially good accompanied by braised spinach and grilled or baked tomatoes, or fried or sautéed potatoes and a salad of fresh tomatoes. | Prepare the ground veal mixture as described in the master recipe and beat into it the tuna and anchovies. Then proceed with the recipe.

name: MARINADE SÈCHE (Salt Marinade with Herbs and Spices)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
authorNotes: Fine for all types of fresh pork. This is our favorite, as it tenderizes the pork and accentuates its natural flavor. | Mix all the ingredients together and rub them into the surface of the pork. Place in a covered bowl. Turn the meat 2 or 3 times if the marinade is a short one; several times a day if it is of long duration. | Before cooking, scrape off the marinade, and dry the meat thoroughly with paper towels.

name: MARINADE SIMPLE (Lemon Juice and Herb Marinade)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
authorNotes: For chops, steaks, and small, boned roasts. This is also an effective marinade giving the pork a slightly different flavor than the dry one. | Rub salt and pepper into the meat. Mix the other ingredients in a bowl, add the pork and baste it. Place a lid over the bowl. Turn and baste the meat 3 or 4 times during its marination period. | Before cooking, scrape off the marinade, and dry the meat thoroughly with paper towels.

name: MARINADE AU VIN (Wine Marinade)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 235 ml dry white wine or 160 ml dry white vermouth
Optional: 1 ml each of basil, tarragon, sage, and mint; 5 coriander seeds; 5 juniper berries
authorNotes: May be used for chops, steaks, and small roasts, but is usually reserved for fresh hams and picnic shoulders. This is a special 2- to 4-day marinade which gives pork a taste akin to that of wild boar, marcassin. | [ NOTE: If the pork is to be marinated for more than 3 days, cook the carrots, onions, and garlic very slowly in the olive oil before proceeding with the recipe.] | Per each 3 pounds of pork

name: RÔTI DE PORC POÊLÉ (Casserole- roasted Pork)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A 1.4 kg boneless roast of pork, previously marinated, if you wish, according to one of the suggestions
60 ml rendered pork fat, lard, or cooking oil
A heavy fireproof casserole just large enough to hold the meat
A medium herb bouquet: 4 parsley sprigs, ½ bay leaf, and 1 ml thyme tied in cheesecloth
120 ml dry white wine, stock, canned bouillon, or water
authorNotes: As most French recipes call for a boneless roast, we shall so specify in this recipe and its variations. The loin is the most expensive cut and also the most attractive looking. But any other cut among those listed may be substituted, and may be boned or not. | Preheat oven to 325 degrees. Dry the meat thoroughly on paper towels. Place the fat in the casserole and set over moderately high heat. When fat is almost smoking, brown the pork on all sides. This will take about 10 minutes. Remove pork to a side dish. | Pour all but 2 spoonfuls of fat out of the casserole. If fat has burned, throw it all out and add more butter. Stir in the vegetables, optional garlic, and herb bouquet. Cover and cook slowly for 5 minutes.

name: Sauce Moutarde à la Normande (Mustard Sauce with Cream)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 10 ml dry mustard mixed with 10 ml water
authorNotes: After the pork has been cooked and placed on a platter, keep it warm for 10 to 15 minutes while preparing the sauce. | Strain the meat juices into a bowl and degrease them. | Pour the vinegar and peppercorns into the casserole and boil until the vinegar has reduced to about a tablespoon. Pour in the meat juices and boil them down rapidly until they have reduced to about ⅔ cup.

name: Rôti de Porc Grand’ Mère (Casserole- roasted Pork with Potatoes and Onions)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A 1.4 kg boneless roast of pork previously marinated in salt for several hours, if you wish
12 to 18 peeled white onions 1 to 1½ inches in diameter
12 to 18 small new potatoes or “boiling” potatoes, peeled and cut into 1½ inch ovals
30 ml rendered pork fat or cooking oil
authorNotes: Onions and potatoes absorb a distinctive flavor when they cook with pork in this manner. | Roast the pork in a covered casserole with seasonings as described in the master recipe , but omit the vegetables. After 1 hour, add potatoes and onions which have been prepared as follows: | Pierce a cross in the root end of the onions, and boil them for 5 minutes in salted water. Drain.

name: Rôti de Porc aux Choux (Casserole-roasted Pork with Cabbage)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A 1.4 kg boneless roast of pork previously marinated for several hours in salt, if you wish
455 g (about 1.4 l) green or white cabbage cut into ½-inch slices
A kettle containing 6.6–7.57 l of rapidly boiling water with 5 ml salt per quart of water
authorNotes: This is a great dish for lovers of cabbage. Serve it with boiled potatoes and a dry Alsatian wine, or beer. | Roast the pork in a covered casserole with carrots, onions, and seasonings as described in the master recipe . After an hour, add the cabbage which has been prepared as follows: | Drop the cabbage into the boiling water. Bring rapidly to the boil and boil uncovered for 2 minutes. Immediately drain in a colander, and run cold water over the cabbage for a minute or two. Drain thoroughly and set aside.

name: PORC BRAISÉ AUX CHOUX ROUGES (Pork Braised with Red Cabbage)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: Ingredients for the braised red cabbage (omit the chestnuts if you wish, but they are a good accompaniment)
A 1.4 kg boneless roast of pork, previously marinated for several hours in salt, if possible
A hot platter Salt and pepper
authorNotes: A good dish of red cabbage is even better when a roast of pork is cooked with it. The casserole of cabbage cooks for 3 hours before the pork goes into it, and needs 2 hours more in the oven until the pork is done. | Braise the cabbage for 3 hours in a 325-degree oven, following the directions in the recipe. | Brown the pork in hot fat in a skillet. After the cabbage has cooked for 3 hours, place the pork in the casserole with the cabbage. Cover the casserole and return it to the oven to braise for two hours more or until the pork is done.

name: CÔTES DE PORC POÊLÉES (Casserole-sautéed Pork Chops)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 6 pork chops cut 1 inch thick and previously marinated for several hours, if you wish, in salt, lemon juice, or wine
45–60 ml rendered pork fat, lard, or cooking oil
120 ml dry white wine, dry white vermouth, brown stock, canned beef bouillon, or marinade liquid
authorNotes: Three or four chops or one or two steaks may be cooked in a covered skillet on top of the stove. For a larger quantity oven-cooking in a covered casserole is easier. | Preheat oven to 325 degrees. | Dry the pork chops on paper towels. Heat the fat or oil in the casserole until it is moderately hot, then brown the chops, 2 or 3 at a time, on each side for 3 to 4 minutes. As they are browned, transfer them to a side dish.

name: Côtes de Porc Sauce Nénette (Pork Chops with Mustard, Cream, and Tomato Sauce)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 30 ml fresh chopped basil, chervil, or parsley
authorNotes: While the pork chops are cooking according to directions in the master recipe, prepare the following: | Simmer the cream, salt, and pepper in a small saucepan for 8 to 10 minutes, or until it has reduced to 1 cup. | Beat the mustard and tomato paste together in a small bowl, then beat in the hot cream. Set aside.

name: Côtes de Porc Robert Côtes de Porc Charcutière (Pork Chops Braised in Fresh Tomato Sauce)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 6 pork chops cut 1 inch thick and previously marinated for several hours, if you wish, according to one of the formulas on this page
5 kg ripe tomatoes peeled, seeded, and chopped (355 ml)
235 ml dry white wine or 160 ml dry vermouth (include marinade liquid, if any)
120 ml brown stock or canned beef bouillon
15–30 ml fresh chopped basil or parsley
authorNotes: Include sautéed potatoes and a chilled rosé wine on the menu with this good dish of pork chops. If you stir chopped pickles and capers into the sauce just before serving the chops, they become côtes de porc charcutière. | Preheat oven to 325 degrees. Brown the chops in hot fat in the casserole as described in the master recipe , and set them aside. | Pour out the browning fat, add the butter and onions, cover and cook slowly for 10 minutes. Mix in the flour and stir over low heat for 2 minutes more. Stir in the tomatoes and other flavorings. Cover and cook slowly for 5 minutes.

name: JAMBON BRAISÉ MORVANDELLE (Ham Braised in Wine—Cream and Mushroom Sauce)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 30 ml butter and 15 ml oil, or 45 ml rendered ham fat
A heavy covered roaster or fireproof casserole just large enough to hold the ham
An 8- to 4.5 kg cooked ham or picnic shoulder, skinned, and trimmed of excess fat
1 l white Burgundy wine (Chablis or Pouilly-Fuissé) or 710 ml dry white vermouth
1–1.4 l white or brown stock or canned beef bouillon
A shallow roasting pan containing a rack
authorNotes: Ham heated in a covered roaster with aromatic vegetables, herbs, stock, and wine absorbs these different flavors, and the braising liquid is easily transformed into a good sauce. | Preheat oven to 325 degrees. Sauté the vegetables in butter and oil or ham fat for about 10 minutes in the roaster or casserole until they are tender and very lightly browned. | Place the ham over the vegetables, its fattiest side up, and add the rest of the ingredients at the left. Bring to the simmer on top of the stove, cover, and place the roaster or casserole in the middle level of the preheated oven. Regulate heat so liquid barely simmers for about 2 hours. Baste every 20 minutes. The ham is done when a trussing needle or sharp-pronged fork will pierce the thickest part of it fairly easily.

name: Cream and Mushroom Sauce
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 45 ml minced shallots or green onions
60 ml Marc de Bourgogne, Madeira, or port
60 ml flour mixed to a paste with 60 ml softened butter
45 ml minced shallots or green onions
60 ml Marc de Bourgogne, Madeira, or port
authorNotes: Dry the mushrooms in a towel. Sauté them for 5 to 6 minutes in hot butter and oil until they are very lightly browned. Stir in the shallots or onions and sauté for a minute more. Season to taste and set aside. | Degrease the braising liquid in the roaster. Set roaster over high heat and boil rapidly until liquid has reduced to about 3 cups and is full of flavor. Add the marc or wine and simmer for a minute or two to evaporate the alcohol. Strain into a saucepan and beat in the flour and butter paste. Beat in 2 cups of cream, then stir in the sautéed mushrooms. Simmer for 5 minutes. The sauce should be just thick enough to coat a spoon very lightly. Stir in more cream if sauce seems too thick. Taste carefully for seasoning. | (*) If not to be served immediately, set aside uncovered, top of sauce filmed with a spoonful of cream to keep a skin from forming. Reheat when ready to serve.

name: Jambon Braisé au Madère (Ham Braised in Madeira Wine)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: An 8- to 4.5 kg cooked ham or picnic shoulder, skinned and trimmed of excess fat
710 ml stock or canned beef bouillon
45 ml arrowroot (preferable to cornstarch as it does not cloud the sauce)
30 ml cold stock, wine, or truffle juice
2 or 3 chopped, canned truffles and their juice OR, 120 ml mushroom duxelles (finely diced, sautéed mushrooms)
authorNotes: The combined flavors of Madeira and ham have always been a favorite in France. Spinach braised in stock, broiled or stuffed mushrooms, and a red Bordeaux-Médoc wine are good accompaniments for this dish. | Following the general directions in the preceding master recipe, preheat oven to 325 degrees. Cook the vegetables in butter and oil in the roaster until lightly browned. Place the ham in the roaster, pour in the wine, the stock or bouillon, and add the herbs. Bring to the simmer on top of the stove, cover, and bake very slowly for 2 to 2½ hours, basting every 20 minutes. When the ham is tender, glaze it with powdered sugar as described in the master recipe. | Degrease the braising liquid and boil it down rapidly to 3 cups. Strain it into a saucepan.

name: JAMBON FARCI ET BRAISÉ (Braised Ham with Mushroom Stuffing)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 120 ml minced shallots or green onions
170–200 g or 175 ml mousse de foie gras , or mousse de foie d’oie (the latter is puréed liver from a plain goose and is much less expensive)
Optional: 1 or 2 diced, canned truffles (reserve their juice for your sauce)
A 4.5 kg cooked ham, skinned, and trimmed of excess fat
A large square of well washed cheesecloth, if needed
authorNotes: A fine dish for an important dinner is ham sliced into serving pieces, reconstructed with a stuffing between each slice, then braised in Madeira. | Trim, wash, and mince the mushrooms. A handful at a time, twist them into a ball in the corner of a towel to extract their juice. Sauté in the butter and oil with the shallots or onions for 8 to 10 minutes until the mushroom pieces begin to separate from each other. | Add the wine to the mushrooms and boil rapidly until the liquid has almost completely evaporated.

name: TRANCHES DE JAMBON EN PIPÉRADE (Ham Slices Baked with Tomatoes, Onions, and Peppers)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 1.1–1.4 kg of cooked ham, sliced ½ inch thick, and cut into serving pieces if you wish
45 ml rendered ham fat or olive oil
A shallow baking dish large enough to hold ham in one layer
0.9 kg firm, ripe, red tomatoes, peeled, seeded, juiced, and sliced (makes 710 ml of pulp)
authorNotes: This savory recipe for thick slices of smoked ham may be prepared for baking several hours before it is set in the oven. Sautéed potatoes, green beans, and a light red wine or a rosé go well with it. | Trim off excess fat, and dry the ham slice or slices on paper towels. Heat the fat or olive oil in the skillet until it is almost smoking, then brown the ham lightly for a minute or two on each side. Remove skillet from heat and place the ham in the baking dish. | Lower heat, and stir the onions into the fat in the skillet. Cover and cook slowly for 5 minutes. Stir in the peppers and cook 5 minutes more or until the vegetables are tender but not browned.

name: TRANCHES DE JAMBON MORVANDELLE (Sautéed Ham Slices— Cream and Madeira Sauce)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 1.1–1.4 kg of cooked ham, sliced ¼ inch thick
30 ml minced shallots or green onions
235 ml very good ham stock, white or brown stock, or canned beef bouillon
A hot platter on which, if you wish, is a mound of spinach braised in stock
authorNotes: Placed on a bed of spinach braised in stock, surrounded with broiled mushrooms or sautéed potatoes, this is a delectable ham dish. Serve with it a light red wine, or a Chablis or Pouilly-Fuissé. | Trim off excess fat, and cut the ham into serving pieces. Dry on paper towels. | A few pieces at a time, brown the ham lightly for a minute on each side in hot butter and oil. Set the ham aside.

name: TRANCHES DE JAMBON À LA CRÈME (Sautéed Ham Slices—Fresh Cream Sauce)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 1.1–1.4 kg cooked ham, sliced ¼ inch thick
160 ml Madeira or port and 45 ml cognac
30 ml Dijon-type prepared mustard mixed with 15 ml tomato paste and 30 ml whipping cream
A hot platter on which, if you wish, is a mound of spinach braised in stock
authorNotes: This famous recipe is the same idea as the preceding one, but is made with a richer and more delicate sauce. | Trim off excess fat, cut the ham into serving pieces, and dry on paper towels. Brown the slices lightly on each side in hot butter and oil and set them aside. Pour all but a tablespoon of sautéing fat out of the skillet, stir in the shallots or onions, and cook slowly for 2 minutes. Pour in the wine and cognac and, scraping up the coagulated sauté juices with a wooden spoon, boil rapidly until the liquid has reduced to 3 or 4 tablespoons. | Add the cream to the skillet, beat in the mustard mixture, and the pepper. Simmer slowly for 10 to 15 minutes, until the cream has reduced to about 1½ cups and has thickened lightly. Correct seasoning, but do not oversalt.

name: CASSOULET DE PORC ET DE MOUTON (Beans Baked with Pork Loin, Shoulder of Mutton or Lamb, and Sausage)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 1.1 kg of boned pork loin, excess fat removed (It will taste even better if marinated overnight in salt and spices .)
0.9 kg or 5 cups dry white beans (Great Northern, preferably)
An 7.57 l kettle containing 4.73 l of rapidly boiling water
225 g fresh pork rind or salt pork rind
A 455 g chunk of fresh, unsalted, unsmoked lean bacon (or very good quality lean salt pork simmered for 10 minutes in 1.9 l of water and drained)
The pork rind and its cooking liquid
A large herb bouquet, with garlic and cloves: 6 to 8 parsley sprigs, 4 unpeeled cloves garlic, 2 cloves, 2 ml thyme, and 2 bay leaves tied in cheesecloth
No salt until later if you have used salt pork; otherwise 15 ml salt
0.9–1.1 kg boned shoulder or breast of mutton or almost mature lamb, fell (skin covering meat) and excess fat removed
60–90 ml rendered fresh pork fat, pork-roast drippings, goose fat, or cooking oil; more if needed
About 455 g cracked mutton or lamb bones; some pork bones may be included
90 ml fresh tomato purée, tomato paste, or 4 large tomatoes peeled, seeded, and juiced
710 ml dry white wine or 475 ml dry white vermouth
1 l brown stock or 710 ml canned beef bouillon and 235 ml water
Optional: 1 chopped truffle and the juice from the can
An 7.57 l fireproof casserole 5 to 6 inches high: brown earthenware glazed inside is typical, but other types of glazed pottery or enameled iron will do nicely
475 ml dry white bread crumbs mixed with 120 ml chopped parsley
45–60 ml pork roasting fat or goose fat
authorNotes: Following directions roast the pork to an internal temperature of 175 to 180 degrees. Set it aside to cool. Reserve cooking juices. | Drop the beans into the boiling water. Bring rapidly back to the boil and boil for 2 minutes. Remove from heat and let the beans soak in the water for 1 hour; they will cook in the soaking water, and the cooking should proceed as soon as possible after the soaking process is completed. | While the beans are soaking, place the rind in the saucepan and cover with 1 quart of cold water. Bring to the boil and boil 1 minute. Drain, rinse in cold water, and repeat the process. Then, with shears, cut the rind into strips ¼ inch wide; cut the strips into small triangles. Cover the rind again with a quart of cold water, bring to the simmer, and simmer very slowly for 30 minutes. Set saucepan aside. This process freshens the rind, and softens it so it will lose itself as it cooks with the beans.

name: FOIE DE VEAU SAUTÉ (Sautéed Calf’s Liver)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 6 to 12 slices of calf’s liver ⅜ inch thick, with surrounding filament removed
120 ml of sifted flour on a large plate
30 ml butter and 15 ml oil for each skillet
A vegetable garniture, water cress, or parsley
authorNotes: Just before sautéing, season the slices with salt and pepper, roll in flour, and shake off excess flour. | Place the butter and oil in the skillet or skillets and set over high heat until you see that the butter foam has almost subsided, indicating it is hot enough. Then arrange the liver in the skillet, leaving a ¼-inch space between each slice. Sauté for 2 to 3 minutes, regulating heat so butter is always very hot but not burning. Turn the liver with a spatula and sauté for a minute or so on the other side. The liver is done when its juices run a very pale pink if a slice is pricked with a fork. Remove the liver to a hot platter and serve, surrounded by whatever garniture you have chosen, or decorated with water cress or sprigs of parsley.

name: Sauce Crème à la Moutarde (Cream and Mustard Sauce)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 120 ml brown stock or canned beef bouillon
15 ml prepared mustard, mashed with 30 ml softened butter
authorNotes: As soon as the liver has been removed to a platter, pour the stock or bouillon into the skillet and boil down rapidly until it has reduced by half. Then add the cream and boil for a moment to reduce and thicken it slightly. | Off heat, swirl the mustard-butter into the skillet. Pour the sauce around the liver, decorate with parsley, and serve.

name: FOIE DE VEAU À LA MOUTARDE (Liver with Mustard, Herbs, and Bread Crumbs)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 6 slices of calf’s liver cut ½ inch thick, outside filament removed
120 ml sifted flour on a large plate
45 ml prepared mustard of the strong, Dijon type
15 ml finely minced shallots or green onions
710 ml fine, white, fresh bread crumbs spread on a large plate
authorNotes: This is an appealing way to prepare liver. It is sautéed very briefly to brown lightly, then painted with mustard and herbs, rolled in fresh bread crumbs, basted with melted butter, and set under a hot broiler to brown the crumbs. The preliminary sautéing and crumbing may be done several hours in advance of the final cooking, which takes about 5 minutes. For this recipe, the liver is sliced thicker, so it will not cook too quickly. | Season the liver with salt and pepper, dredge in flour, and sauté for 1 minute on each side in very hot butter and oil. The slices should be very lightly browned and slightly stiffened, but not cooked through. Remove to a dish. | Beat the mustard in a small bowl with the shallots or onions and seasonings. Drop by drop, beat in the liver sautéing fat to make a mayonnaiselike cream. Paint the liver slices with the mixture. One by one, lay the slices in the bread crumbs and heap bread crumbs on top, gently shake off excess, and pat the adhering crumbs in place with the flat of a knife. Arrange the liver on the broiling pan.

name: BLANCHING SWEETBREADS
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: Sweetbreads, trimmed and soaked as in preceding directions
An enameled saucepan just large enough to hold them
Per quart of water: 5 ml salt and 15 ml lemon juice
authorNotes: Place sweetbreads in saucepan and cover by 2 inches with cold water; add salt and lemon juice. Bring to simmer and cook, uncovered, at barest simmer for 15 minutes. Drain and plunge into cold water for 5 minutes. Drain. The sweetbreads are now ready for sautéing.

name: BLANCHING BRAINS
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: Brains, trimmed and soaked as in preceding directions
An enameled saucepan just large enough to hold them
Per quart of water: 5 ml salt and 15 ml lemon juice
authorNotes: Place brains in saucepan and cover by 2 inches with boiling water; add salt and lemon juice or vinegar. Heat to just below simmer and maintain water at a not-quite-simmering temperature, timing as follows: | Lamb brains, 15 minutes | Calf or pork brains, 20 minutes

name: RIS DE VEAU BRAISÉS (Braised Sweetbreads)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 60 ml each: finely diced carrots, onions, celery, and diced ham
A medium herb bouquet: 4 parsley sprigs, 1 ml thyme, and ½ bay leaf tied in cheesecloth
680 g to 0.9 kg sweetbreads previously soaked, peeled, and trimmed as directed
A buttered fireproof casserole or baking dish about 7 inches in diameter, or just large enough to hold the sweetbreads in one layer
175 ml dry white wine or 120 ml dry white vermouth
235 ml brown stock or canned beef bouillon if you are serving a brown sauce; 235 ml white stock or canned chicken broth if you are serving a white sauce
authorNotes: Braising is a preliminary cooking for sweetbreads, and you will note that no blanching is required. The soaked and peeled sweetbreads are first cooked slowly in butter to firm them a little and to render some of their juices; they are then baked with wine and other flavorings. After this cooking, or braising, which may take place as long as the day before you are to use them, the sweetbreads are ready for saucing and serving. Sauced sweetbreads may be arranged in a ring of rice or risotto , in a vol-au-vent or pastry shell, on a platter, or they may be gratinéed. Drained, braised sweetbreads are also good cold, in a salad. | Rice or risotto , and buttered peas or creamed or braised spinach go well with sweetbreads. Serve a light red wine such a Bordeaux-Médoc, or a rosé with sweetbreads in brown sauce; a white Burgundy or a white Graves with sweetbreads in cream sauce. | Cook the diced vegetables and ham slowly in the butter with the herb bouquet and seasonings for 10 to 15 minutes, until tender but not browned.

name: Ris de Veau Braisés à l’Italienne (Braised Sweetbreads with Brown Mushroom Sauce)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: The braised sweetbreads in the preceding recipe
15 ml cornstarch blended with 15 ml dry white wine or vermouth
225 g finely diced fresh mushrooms sautéed in butter
30 ml minced green herbs such as parsley, chervil, and tarragon, or parsley only
authorNotes: Remove the sweetbreads from the braising casserole. Drain, cut into ½-inch slices, and set aside. | Rapidly boil down the cooking stock in the casserole to 1½ cups. Remove from heat and discard herb bouquet. (The braising vegetables and ham remain, and become a part of the sauce.) Beat in the starch mixture and the tomato paste. Stir in the mushrooms and additional ham; simmer for 3 minutes, stirring. Correct seasoning and fold in the sliced sweetbreads. | (*) If not to be served immediately, film sauce with a spoonful of stock.

name: Ris de Veau à la Crème Ris de Veau à la Maréchale (Creamed Sweetbreads)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 680 g to 0.9 kg braised sweetbreads, master recipe
30 ml minced, mixed green herbs or parsley
authorNotes: Cut the braised sweetbreads into slices ½ inch thick and set aside. Rapidly boil down their cooking stock until it has reduced to 1¼ cups. | In a separate pan, cook the butter and flour slowly together until they foam for 2 minutes without browning. Off heat, strain in the hot cooking stock and beat vigorously to blend. Bring to the simmer, stirring, for 1 minute. Sauce will be very thick. | Beat in ½ cup of cream, simmering, then beat in more by spoonfuls until the sauce thins out and coats the spoon nicely. Correct seasoning, adding drops of lemon juice if you feel it necessary.

name: Ris de Veau à la Crème et aux Champignons (Creamed Sweetbreads with Mushrooms)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: Ingredients for the preceding creamed sweetbreads
authorNotes: After making the sauce, stir in the mushrooms and simmer for 10 minutes, allowing the sauce to reduce slightly as the mushrooms will thin it out. Then proceed with the recipe.

name: Ris de Veau au Gratin (Sweetbreads au Gratin)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: Ingredients for the braised sweetbreads and any of the preceding sauces
authorNotes: Arrange the sliced sweetbreads in a buttered baking dish or individual shells or dishes. Pour sauce over them. Sprinkle on the cheese, and dot with the butter. Set aside until ready to serve. | About 10 minutes before serving, place 7 to 8 inches under a moderately hot broiler to heat the sweetbreads through and to brown the top of the sauce lightly.

name: CERVELLES AU BEURRE NOIR (Calf’s Brains in Brown Butter Sauce)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 680 g calf’s brains, previously soaked, trimmed, and blanched according to directions
235 ml brown butter sauce , with or without capers
235 ml flour sifted into a dish
30 ml butter and 15 ml oil for each skillet
authorNotes: Brown butter sauce and calf’s brains are almost synonyms, they go so well together. To be at its best, the sauce should always be made separately, not in the pan in which you sautéed the brains. It is thus clear and unspeckled, and also much more digestible. In addition, as the brains can be sautéed only at the last minute, the dish will then be ready to serve almost at once. | Of the several methods for cervelles au beurre noir , we have chosen that of pre-cooking the brains, slicing them, marinating them in a vinaigrette, then sautéing and saucing them. As alternatives, you may simply pour a brown butter sauce over hot, blanched brains, or you may omit the pre-cooking and marination altogether. In this case, slice raw, soaked, and trimmed brains, season, dredge in flour, and sauté them; then pour the sauce over them. | This dish often constitutes a separate course, but if you wish to serve the brains as a main course, accompany them with mashed or parsley potatoes and either buttered green peas or the spinach braised in stock . Wine choices would be a light red, such as Bordeaux-Medoc, or a rosé ; good but less usual would be a white Burgundy.

name: CERVELLES EN MATELOTE (Calf’s Brains in Red Wine with Mushrooms and Onions)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 475 ml good, young, red wine (Mâcon or Burgundy)
235 ml brown stock or canned beef bouillon
An enameled saucepan just large enough to hold the brains in one layer
680 g calf’s brains, previously soaked and peeled
30 ml flour mashed to a paste with 30 ml softened butter
225 g fresh mushrooms sautéed in butter
12 heart-shaped croûtons (white bread sautéed in clarified butter)
authorNotes: This dish makes a complete course in itself. Serve a light red Burgundy or Mâcon wine. | Bring the wine and stock or bouillon to the simmer in the saucepan with the herbs and garlic. Add the brains, bring to the simmer, and cook uncovered at just below the simmer for 20 minutes. Allow the brains to cool in the cooking liquid for 20 minutes, so they will absorb flavor, and firm up. Then drain them, slice into ½-inch pieces, and arrange in the buttered serving dish. | Beat the tomato paste into the cooking stock, and boil down rapidly until the liquid has reduced to 1½ cups. Off heat, beat in the flour-butter paste. Boil, stirring, for 1 minute. Correct seasoning.

name: ROGNONS DE VEAU EN CASSEROLE (Kidneys Cooked in Butter— Mustard and Parsley Sauce)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A fireproof casserole or chafing dish which will just hold the kidneys easily side by side
3 veal kidneys, peeled and trimmed of fat
15 ml minced shallots or green onions
120 ml dry white wine or dry vermouth
20 ml prepared mustard of the strong Dijon type, mashed with 45 ml softened butter
authorNotes: If you want to serve this as a main course rather than as a hot hors d’oeuvre, potatoes sautéed in butter and braised onions make good accompaniments. Red Burgundy goes especially well with kidneys. | Heat the butter in the casserole or chafing dish until you see the foam begin to subside. Roll the kidneys in the butter, then cook them uncovered for about 10 minutes; turn them every minute or two. Regulate heat so butter is always very hot but is not discoloring. A little juice from the kidneys will exude and coagulate in the bottom of the casserole. The kidneys should stiffen but not become hard, brown very lightly, and be pink at the center when sliced. Remove them to a hot plate and cover to keep warm for a few minutes. | Stir the shallots or onions into the butter in the casserole and cook for 1 minute. Then add the wine or vermouth and lemon juice. Boil, scraping up coagulated cooking juices, until the liquids have reduced to about 4 tablespoons.

name: Rognons de Veau Flambés (Veal Kidneys Flamed in Brandy— Cream and Mushroom Sauce)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 3 veal kidneys, peeled and trimmed of fat
A fireproof casserole or chafing dish
120 ml brown sauce , or 120 ml canned beef bouillon mixed with 5 ml cornstarch 80 ml Madeira
225 g sliced fresh mushrooms sautéed in butter with 15 ml minced shallots or green onions
5 ml prepared mustard of the strong Dijon type, blended with 30 ml softened butter and 2 ml Worcestershire
authorNotes: This extremely good combination is one which is often prepared beside your table in a good restaurant. If you are making it at home in a chafing dish, have all the sauce ingredients, including the sautéed mushrooms, at hand in separate containers. Kidneys cooked this way are best as a separate course, served with hot French bread, and a full, red Burgundy wine. | Cook the kidneys for about 10 minutes in hot butter as described in the master recipe. | Pour the cognac over the kidneys. Avert your face and ignite the cognac with a lighted match. Shake the casserole or chafing dish and baste the kidneys for a few seconds until the flames have subsided. Remove the kidneys to a hot plate, and cover them.

name: Rognons de Veau à la Bordelaise (Veal Kidneys in Red Wine Sauce with Marrow)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 3 veal kidneys, peeled and trimmed of fat
A fireproof casserole or chafing dish
30 ml minced shallots or green onions
120 ml good, young, red wine such as a Burgundy or Mâcon
Big pinch each of thyme, pepper, and powdered bay leaf
235 ml brown sauce or 235 ml canned beef bouillon, mixed with 15 ml arrowroot or cornstarch
80 ml diced beef marrow softened for 2 to 3 minutes in hot water
authorNotes: Sauce à la bordelaise is a reduction of red wine, brown sauce, shallots, and herbs into which poached marrow is folded just before serving. It goes very well with kidneys. With sautéed potatoes and braised onions or buttered peas, this would make a fine main course served with a red Burgundy wine. | Cook the kidneys for about 10 minutes in hot butter in a casserole or chafing dish as described in the master recipe . Remove them to a hot plate and cover them. | Stir the shallots or onions into the casserole and cook for 1 minute. Pour in the wine, add seasonings, and boil until reduced by half.

name: ARTICHAUTS AU NATUREL (Whole Boiled Artichokes—Hot or Cold)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 6 artichokes prepared for cooking as in the preceding directions
A large kettle containing 6.6–7.57 l of rapidly boiling water
5 ml salt per quart of water
authorNotes: Artichokes should be boiled in a large kettle so that they have plenty of room. It is not necessary to tie the leaves in place. Because they must cook a comparatively long time, artichokes turn an olive green. Any Frenchman would look with disfavor on a bright green boiled artichoke, knowing that baking soda had been added to the water. | Drop the prepared artichokes in the boiling salted water. To help prevent discoloration, lay over the artichokes a double thickness of cheesecloth; this will keep their exposed tops moist. Bring the water back to the boil as rapidly as possible and boil slowly, uncovered, for 35 to 45 minutes. The artichokes are done when the leaves pull out easily and the bottoms are tender when pierced with a knife. | Immediately remove them from the kettle with skimmer or spoon and drain them upside down in a colander.

name: ARTICHAUTS BRAISÉS À LA PROVENÇALE (Artichokes Braised with Wine, Garlic, and Herbs)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A large kettle containing 6.6–7.57 l of rapidly boiling water
5 ml salt per quart of water
A 10- to 11-inch covered fireproof casserole large enough to hold the artichokes in one layer
120 ml dry white wine or dry white vermouth
355 ml stock, canned beef bouillon, or water
An herb bouquet: 4 parsley sprigs, ½ bay leaf, and 1 ml thyme tied in cheesecloth
authorNotes: Most of the many recipes for braised artichokes follow the general lines of this one. You may, if you wish, add to the casserole a cup of diced tomato pulp, or ½ cup of diced ham, and, 10 minutes before the end of the cooking, ½ pound of sautéed mushrooms. Another suggestion with different vegetables follows this recipe. Braised artichokes go well with roast or braised meats, or they can constitute a first course. As they are rather messy to eat with the fingers, guests should be furnished with a spoon as well as a knife and fork, so the flesh may be scraped off the artichoke leaves. | Prepare the artichokes for cooking as directed at the beginning of this section, but cut off the leaves so that the artichokes are only about 1½ inches long. Then slice the artichokes into lengthwise quarters and cut out the chokes. Drop the quarters in boiling water and boil for 10 minutes only. Drain. | Preheat oven to 325 degrees.

name: Artichauts Printaniers (Artichokes Braised with Carrots, Onions, Turnips, and Mushrooms)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: Ingredients for the preceding braised artichokes, including diced onions, oil (or butter), wine, stock, and seasonings
12 small white onions, about 1 inch in diameter, peeled
3 or 4 carrots, peeled, quartered and cut into 1½-inch lengths
3 or 4 white turnips, peeled and quartered
12 to 18 mushroom caps lightly sautéed in olive oil or butter
authorNotes: Except for the addition of other vegetables, this recipe is the same as the master recipe. You may wish to use butter instead of olive oil, cut down on the garlic, and omit all or part of the vinegar, increasing the wine accordingly. | Following the preceding recipe, quarter and blanch the artichokes, and cook the diced onions in the olive oil (or butter). Then add the artichokes and place the whole onions and the other vegetables around the edge of the casserole. Baste with the diced onions and oil (or butter), and season with salt and pepper. Proceed with the recipe. | About 10 minutes before the end of the cooking, add the mushroom caps. Finish the sauce and serve the casserole as in the preceding recipe.

name: FONDS D’ARTICHAUTS À BLANC (Cooked Artichoke Bottoms—Preliminary Cooking)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
authorNotes: A blanc is a solution of salted water with lemon juice and flour. It is used for the preliminary cooking of any food which discolors easily, such as artichoke bottoms, salsify, calf’s head. Flour and lemon juice blanch the food and keep its whiteness. | Never cook artichoke bottoms in anything but enamel, pyrex, stainless steel, or earthenware. Aluminum or iron pans will give them a grayish color. | For 6 to 8 large artichoke bottoms

name: FONDS D’ARTICHAUTS AU BEURRE (Buttered Artichoke Bottoms, Whole)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A covered fireproof casserole (enameled, pyrex, or stainless steel) just large enough to hold the artichokes in one layer
A round of buttered waxed paper
authorNotes: This is the method for heating cooked artichoke bottoms which are to be filled with hot vegetables, poached eggs, béarnaise sauce, truffles, or whatever is called for. | Preheat oven to 325 degrees. | Season the artichokes with salt and pepper. Heat the butter in the casserole until it is bubbling. Remove from heat. Baste each artichoke bottom with butter as you place it upside down (to keep center moist) in the casserole. Lay the buttered paper over them. Reheat the casserole, then place it in the middle level of the preheated oven for about 20 minutes, or until they are well heated through. Do not overcook.

name: Quartiers de Fonds d’Artichauts au Beurre (Buttered Artichoke Bottoms, Quartered)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 30 ml minced shallots or green onions
A round of waxed paper, buttered
authorNotes: This is basically the same as the preceding recipe, except that the artichoke bottoms are cut into quarters, and shallots or onions are included with the butter. Use quartered artichokes as a vegetable garnish or combine them with other vegetables such as braised carrots and onions, or sautéed mushrooms. They go well with veal, chicken, and egg dishes. | Cut the artichoke bottoms in quarters, and preheat oven to 325 degrees. | Melt the butter in the casserole. Stir in the shallots or onions, then fold in the artichokes. Season with salt and pepper, and lay over them the round of buttered paper. Cover the casserole and bake in middle level of preheated oven for about 20 minutes, or until the vegetables are well steeped in the butter. Do not overcook. Sprinkle with parsley before serving.

name: Fonds d’Artichauts Mirepoix (Buttered Artichoke Bottoms with Diced Vegetables)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 45 ml each: finely diced carrots, onions, and celery
30 ml finely diced, lean, boiled ham
Ingredients for the preceding buttered artichoke bottoms
authorNotes: This is particularly good if the artichoke bottoms are to be served as a separate vegetable. | Cook the carrots, onions, celery, and ham for 8 to 10 minutes in the butter called for in the preceding recipe. When the vegetables are tender but not browned, add the rest of the ingredients listed, and proceed with the recipe.

name: Fonds d’Artichauts à la Crème (Creamed Artichoke Bottoms)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: Ingredients for 6 quartered artichoke bottoms cooked as in either of the two preceding variations
5 ml lemon juice, more if needed
authorNotes: Serve creamed artichoke bottoms with roast veal or chicken, or sautéed brains or sweetbreads. They also go with omelettes. | While the artichoke bottoms are cooking as directed in either of the two preceding recipes, boil the cream in a small saucepan until it has reduced by half. Season to taste with salt, pepper, and lemon juice. When the artichokes are done, fold the hot cream into them. Simmer for a moment on top of the stove to blend flavors. Turn into a hot vegetable dish and sprinkle with parsley.

name: Fonds d’Artichauts Mornay (Artichoke Bottoms Gratinéed with Cheese Sauce)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: Ingredients for 6 buttered artichoke bottoms, quartered
A lightly buttered baking dish about 8 inches in diameter and 2 inches deep
authorNotes: Serve gratinéed artichoke bottoms with roast chicken or veal, sautéed chicken, veal scallops, or liver. Or you could use them as a hot first course or luncheon dish; in this case, you might fold into the artichoke bottoms before saucing them 1 cup of sautéed mushrooms, diced boiled ham, or diced cooked chicken. | While the artichoke bottoms are cooking, make the sauce mornay. When they are done, spread one third of the sauce in the dish and arrange the artichokes over it. Pour on the rest of the sauce, sprinkle on the cheese, and dot with butter. | About 30 minutes before serving, place in upper third of a preheated, 375-degree oven to heat through thoroughly and brown the top of the sauce lightly. Serve as soon as possible.

name: FONDS D’ARTICHAUTS AU GRATIN (Stuffed Artichoke Bottoms au Gratin)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 6 large artichoke bottoms cooked in a blanc
235–355 ml of one of the creamed fillings , such as ham, chicken, mushrooms or shellfish
authorNotes: Stuffed artichoke bottoms make an attractive hot first course or luncheon dish. | Arrange the cooked artichoke bottoms in the baking dish. Place several spoonsful of the filling in each, heaping it into a slight dome. Sprinkle with cheese and dot with butter. About 20 minutes before serving, bake in upper third of a preheated 375-degree oven until thoroughly warmed through and the cheese has browned lightly.

name: FROZEN ARTICHOKE HEARTS
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 235 ml chicken stock, canned chicken broth, half mushroom broth and half water, or water only
30 ml minced shallots or green onions (or 30 ml each: finely diced onions, celery, carrots, and ham, previously cooked until tender in the butter)
2 packages frozen artichoke hearts, partially thawed (285 g each)
authorNotes: These usually come in 10-ounce packages and are baby, halved hearts with the tender center leaves still attached. We find it to be more satisfactory if you allow the vegetables to thaw enough so they can be detached from each other. They will then cook more evenly. | Bring the liquid, shallots or onions, butter, and salt to the boil in an enameled saucepan. Add the partially thawed artichoke hearts. Cover saucepan and bring to the boil. Boil slowly for 7 to 10 minutes, or until artichoke hearts are tender. Uncover, raise heat, and boil off any remaining liquid. | Serve them sprinkled with parsley, or in the cream sauce or cheese sauce described in the previous recipes. Or you may combine them with other cooked vegetables such as sautéed mushrooms or glazed carrots and onions.

name: ASPERGES AU NATUREL (Boiled Asparagus—Hot or Cold)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A large kettle or oval casserole containing 6.6–7.57 l rapidly boiling water (for 4 to 6 bundles of prepared asparagus spears)
5 ml salt per quart of water
A serving platter covered with a folded white napkin (to absorb the asparagus liquid)
authorNotes: Kettle must be wide enough to hold the asparagus bunches horizontally. Lay the asparagus bundles in the rapidly boiling salted water. Bring water to boil again as quickly as possible. When boil is reached, reduce heat and boil slowly, uncovered, for 12 to 15 minutes. The asparagus is done when a knife pierces the butt-end easily. The spears should bend a little, but should not be limp and droopy. Eat the loose spear as a test for doneness. | As soon as the asparagus is tender, lift it out of the water bundle by bundle with 2 forks, one slipped under each round of string. Hold up for a few seconds to drain, then place the bundle carefully on the napkin. Cut and remove the strings. Proceed quickly to the next bundle. | If the asparagus is not to be served immediately, it will keep warm for 20 to 30 minutes covered with a napkin. Set the platter on top of the kettle of hot asparagus cooking water. The asparagus will lose a bit of its texture as it waits because it will continue to exude moisture, but it will retain its taste and color.

name: POINTES D’ASPERGES AU BEURRE (Buttered Asparagus Tips)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 0.9 kg asparagus tips prepared as directed in preceding paragraph
A covered baking dish or fireproof baking and serving dish
authorNotes: For 4 to 6 people as a vegetable garnish | Drop the diced asparagus stalks in the boiling salted water and boil 5 minutes. Then add the asparagus bundles and boil slowly for 5 to 8 minutes more, or until just tender. Remove bundles carefully and drain; drain the diced stalks. | (*) If you are cooking the asparagus in advance or wish to serve it cold, plunge for a minute or two in cold water to stop the cooking and set the color. Drain.

name: FROZEN ASPARAGUS
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: For each 285 g box of frozen asparagus, partially thawed:
A wide enameled saucepan or skillet
Melted butter or one of the sauces listed
authorNotes: Frozen asparagus will always be limp however you cook it; the following method is as good as any we have found. Allow the asparagus to thaw partially before cooking so the spears can be separated and will cook more evenly. If you are doing more than two boxes at a time, use two wide saucepans in order that the cooking water will boil away by the time the asparagus is done. | Bring the water, salt, and butter to the boil in the saucepan or skillet. Add the asparagus, cover, and boil slowly for 5 to 8 minutes or until asparagus is tender. Remove cover, raise heat, and quickly boil off any remaining liquid. Correct seasoning. Arrange in vegetable dish, pour on sauce or pass it separately, and serve as soon as possible.

name: TIMBALE D’ASPERGES (Asparagus Mold)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 235 ml milk brought to the boil with 60 ml butter
1.4 kg boiled, fresh asparagus, or 710 ml cooked frozen asparagus, or canned asparagus
A pan of boiling water large enough to hold the mold easily
authorNotes: This asparagus custard is served unmolded as a first course or luncheon dish, or may be served with roast or sautéed veal or chicken. The custard mixture may be prepared hours in advance of cooking, and the cooked mold may be kept warm for a considerable time or reheated. You can mold the custard in individual cups if you wish. | NOTE: Chopped, cooked Brussels sprouts, broccoli, cauliflower, spinach, or puréed green peas may be cooked in the same way; substitute 2½ to 3 cups of any of these vegetables for the asparagus. | Preheat oven to 325 degrees. Oil the mold. Roll bread crumbs in it to cover the entire inner surface. Knock out excess crumbs.

name: HARICOTS VERTS BLANCHIS (Blanched Green Beans—Preliminary Cooking)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 1.4 kg green beans, trimmed and washed
A large kettle containing at least 6.6–7.57 l of rapidly boiling water
5 ml salt per quart of water
authorNotes: Whatever recipe you choose for your beans, always give them a preliminary blanching in a very large kettle of rapidly boiling salted water. Depending on what you plan to do to them later, boil them either until tender or until almost tender, and drain immediately. This essential step in the French art of bean cookery always produces a fine, fresh, green bean of perfect texture and flavor. | A handful at a time, drop the beans into the rapidly boiling salted water. Bring the water back to the boil as quickly as possible, and boil the beans slowly, uncovered, for 10 to 15 minutes; test the beans frequently after 8 minutes by eating one. A well cooked bean should be tender, but still retain the slightest suggestion of crunchiness. Drain the beans as soon as they are done.

name: Haricots Verts à l’Anglaise (Buttered Green Beans I)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A wide, heavy-bottomed, enameled saucepan or skillet
60–120 ml butter, cut into pieces or formed into shells
authorNotes: Toss the hot beans in the saucepan or skillet over moderately high heat to evaporate their moisture. Toss briefly again with salt and pepper to taste. Turn them into the serving dish, distribute the butter over them, and serve at once.

name: Haricots Verts à la Maître d’Hôtel (Buttered Green Beans II — with Lemon Juice and Parsley)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 1.4 kg hot, blanched green beans (preceding master recipe)
A wide, heavy-bottomed, enameled saucepan or skillet
90–120 ml softened butter cut into 4 pieces
authorNotes: Toss the hot beans in the saucepan or skillet over moderately high heat to evaporate their moisture. Toss briefly again with salt, pepper and a piece of butter. Add the rest of the butter gradually while tossing the beans; alternate with drops of lemon juice. Taste for seasoning. Turn into the vegetable dish, sprinkle with parsley, and serve immediately.

name: Haricots Verts à la Crème (Creamed Green Beans I)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 1.4 kg green beans, trimmed and washed
A wide, heavy-bottomed, enameled saucepan or skillet
45 ml fresh minced savory, tarragon, or parsley
authorNotes: Blanch the beans in 7 to 8 quarts of rapidly boiling salted water as described on this page , but drain them 3 to 4 minutes before they are tender. Toss the beans in the pan over moderately high heat to evaporate their moisture. Then toss with the salt, pepper, and butter. Pour in the cream, cover the pan, and boil slowly for 5 minutes or so, until beans are tender and cream has reduced by half. Correct seasoning. | Turn into hot vegetable dish, sprinkle with herbs, and serve at once.

name: Haricots Verts, Sauce Crème (Creamed Green Beans II)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 1.4 kg green beans, trimmed and washed
A wide, heavy-bottomed, enameled saucepan or skillet
45 ml minced shallots or green onions
710 ml boiling sauce crème (béchamel with cream)
45 ml fresh minced savory, tarragon, or parsley
225–455 g sliced mushrooms sautéed in butter
authorNotes: This is less rich than the pure cream treatment in the preceding recipe. | Blanch the beans in 7 to 8 quarts of boiling salted water as described on this page , but drain them 3 to 4 minutes before they are tender. Toss the beans in the pan over moderately high heat to evaporate their moisture. | Then toss them with the seasonings, butter, and minced shallots or onions. Cover the pan and let them cook slowly for 3 to 4 minutes.

name: HARICOTS VERTS GRATINÉS, À LA MORNAY (Green Beans Gratinéed with Cheese Sauce)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 1.4 kg green beans, trimmed and washed
15 ml butter cut into pea-sized dots
authorNotes: This is a good method for ahead-of-time preparation. Serve with the same meats suggested in the preceding recipes for green beans in cream. | Blanch the beans as described until they are just tender. Drain, refresh in cold water, and dry in a towel. | Spread a third of the sauce in the baking dish. Season the beans and arrange them over the sauce. Pour on the rest of the sauce. Sprinkle with cheese, dot with butter, and set aside uncovered.

name: HARICOTS VERTS À LA PROVENÇALE (Green Beans with Tomatoes, Garlic, and Herbs)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 120 ml olive oil An enameled saucepan or skillet large enough to hold the beans
4 to 6 large, firm, ripe, red tomatoes peeled, seeded, juiced, and chopped
A medium herb bouquet with cloves: 4 parsley sprigs, ½ bay leaf, 2 ml thyme, and 2 cloves tied in cheesecloth
175 ml liquid: juice from the tomatoes plus water or water only
1.4 kg green beans OR 3 boxes partially defrosted cut green beans added directly to the cooked tomato mixture
60 ml chopped parsley, or a mixture of green herbs such as basil, savory, and tarragon plus parsley
authorNotes: These full-flavored beans go wonderfully with roast lamb or beef, steaks, chops, or broiled chicken. Tossed with diced, sautéed ham, they can serve as a main-course luncheon or supper dish. Frozen beans react nicely to this treatment. | Cook the onions slowly in the olive oil until they are tender and translucent but not browned, about 10 minutes. | Add the ingredients at the left, and simmer for 30 minutes. Then remove the herb bouquet.

name: HARICOTS MANGE-TOUT À L’ÉTUVÉE (Wax Beans Braised with Onions, Lettuce, and Cream)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 1.4 kg large but tender wax beans
A covered fireproof casserole or baking dish
5 ml salt Big pinch of pepper
1 medium herb bouquet: 4 parsley sprigs, ½ bay leaf, and 1 ml thyme tied in cheesecloth
355 ml chicken stock or canned chicken broth
475 ml light cream Salt and pepper
45 ml minced fresh savory, basil, tarragon, or parsley
authorNotes: Trim and wash the beans according to directions being sure all strings have been removed. Preheat oven to 350 degrees. | Butter the casserole or baking dish heavily. Spread the onions in the bottom, arrange the beans over them, and season with salt and pepper. Bury the herb bouquet in their midst. Distribute the lettuce over the beans. Sliver the rest of the butter over the lettuce. Pour in the stock or broth. Set casserole over heat and bring liquid to the simmer. Place the round of paper over the vegetables, cover the casserole and set in lower third of oven. Regulate heat so liquid simmers regularly and has almost evaporated in 45 minutes. Remove herb bouquet. | Bring the cream to the boil and pour it into the casserole. Bake 30 minutes more. Correct seasoning.

name: FROZEN GREEN OR WAX BEANS
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: For each 285 g package of beans:
120 ml chicken stock, canned chicken broth, canned mushroom broth, or water
15 ml minced shallots or green onions
A heavy bottomed, enameled saucepan or skillet with cover
authorNotes: Frozen beans cook more evenly if they are partially thawed and not stuck all together in a solid block. When you are doing more than two packages, use two saucepans; if too many beans are cooked in one pan, the liquid will not evaporate by the time the beans are tender. Cut beans have more flavor than Frenched beans. | Bring the liquid, shallots or onions, salt, and butter to the boil in the saucepan. Add the partially thawed beans. Cover and boil slowly for 5 to 6 minutes, tossing occasionally, until the beans are just tender. Remove cover and rapidly boil off any remaining liquid. Correct seasoning. | The beans may now be used in any of the preceding green bean recipes. If they are to be simmered in cream or in a sauce, use half the amount of liquid for their preliminary cooking, and cook only until partially tender. They will finish cooking in the sauce.

name: CHOUX DE BRUXELLES BLANCHIS (Blanched Brussels Sprouts—Preliminary Cooking)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 1–1.9 l Brussels sprouts, trimmed and washed
A large kettle containing 6.6–7.57 l of rapidly boiling water
5 ml salt per quart of water
authorNotes: Brussels sprouts, whether they are to be served with melted butter and seasonings or are to be simmered or braised, always receive a blanching in a large kettle of boiling salted water. This preliminary may be accomplished hours before the final cooking is to take place. | Drop the Brussels sprouts into the rapidly boiling salted water. Bring to the boil again as rapidly as possible. | If the vegetables are to be partially cooked and finished off later as directed in most of the following recipes, boil them slowly, uncovered, for 6 to 8 minutes, or until almost tender. Immediately remove with a skimmer and drain in a colander.

name: CHOUX DE BRUXELLES ÉTUVÉS AU BEURRE (Brussels Sprouts Braised in Butter)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A 2.37 l, fireproof, covered casserole or baking dish large enough to hold the Brussels sprouts in 1 or 2 layers
A round of lightly buttered waxed paper
authorNotes: Serve braised Brussels sprouts with roast turkey, pork, duck, or goose, steaks, chops, hamburgers, or sautéed liver. You may dress up braised Brussels sprouts with cream, cheese, or chestnuts, as suggested in the variations at the end of the recipe. | Preheat oven to 350 degrees, and smear the butter inside the casserole or baking dish. | Arrange the blanched Brussels sprouts heads up in the casserole or baking dish. Sprinkle lightly with salt and pepper, and with the melted butter.

name: Choux de Bruxelles Étuvés à la Crème (Creamed Brussels Sprouts)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 15–30 ml butter cut into pea-sized dots
authorNotes: Serve these with veal, chicken, or turkey. | Braise the Brussels sprouts as in the preceding recipe, but use only 2 tablespoons of butter. After the casserole has been in the oven for 10 minutes, pour on the boiling cream and continue baking for 10 more minutes or until the vegetables are tender. They will have absorbed most of the cream. Correct seasoning, dot with butter, and serve as soon as possible.

name: Choux de Bruxelles aux Marrons (Brussels Sprouts Braised with Chestnuts)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 1.4 l Brussels sprouts braised in butter
authorNotes: This recipe is particularly good with roast turkey, duck, or goose. | Follow the preceding master recipe for braising the Brussels sprouts, but add the braised chestnuts to the casserole to cook with them.

name: Choux de Bruxelles à la Mornay, Gratinés (Brussels Sprouts Gratinéed with Cheese Sauce)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 1.4 l Brussels sprouts braised in butter
475 ml hot sauce mornay ( béchamel with cheese)
A lightly buttered baking dish about 9 inches in diameter and 2 inches deep
15 ml butter cut into pea-sized dots
authorNotes: Serve these with roast chicken or veal, or as a luncheon or supper dish. | Follow the preceding master recipe for braising the Brussel sprouts; you may use only 2 tablespoons of butter if you wish. Prepare the sauce, and spread one third of it in the baking dish when the sprouts are done. Arrange the Brussels sprouts over the sauce, spoon the rest of the sauce over them, and sprinkle with cheese and dots of butter. Set under a moderately hot broiler for 2 to 3 minutes to brown lightly, and serve at once.

name: Choux de Bruxelles à la Milanaise (Brussels Sprouts Browned with Cheese)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 1.4 l Brussels sprouts braised in butter
120 ml grated Swiss cheese mixed with 120 ml grated Parmesan cheese
authorNotes: These cheese-coated Brussels sprouts are good with steaks and chops. | Follow the preceding master recipe for braising the Brussels sprouts, but when they have been in the oven 10 minutes, turn them into a bowl. Reset oven to 425 degrees. Sprinkle 2 to 3 tablespoons of cheese in the casserole or baking dish to coat the bottom and sides. Return the Brussels sprouts, spreading the rest of the cheese over each layer. Pour on the melted butter. Place uncovered in upper third of oven for 10 to 15 minutes, to brown the cheese nicely.

name: CHOUX DE BRUXELLES À LA CRÈME (Brussels Sprouts Chopped and Simmered in Cream)
