name: TARTE AUX FRAISES (Fresh Strawberry Tart—cold)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: Optional: a candy thermometer A pastry brush
355–475 ml chilled crème pâtissière (custard filling) with 30–45 ml kirsch or cognac
authorNotes: Fresh fruit tarts are easy to make, pretty to look at, and refreshing to eat. They consist of a fully baked tart shell which is lined with liqueur-flavored crème pâtissière (custard filling). The fresh fruit is arranged over the filling and topped with apricot or red currant glaze. Other suggestions follow this recipe. | Use either the sweet short paste , or the sugar crust for your tart shell. | Hull the strawberries. If necessary to wash them, do so very quickly, and drain them on a rack.

name: TARTE AUX POIRES À LA BOURDALOUE (Pear and Almond Tart—tepid or cold)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 680 g to 0.9 kg firm, ripe, unblemished pears
475 ml cold water and 15 ml lemon juice in a mixing bowl
60 ml red currant jelly in a small saucepan
590 ml chilled frangipane (almond custard), with 30 ml kirsch
authorNotes: Peel and halve the pears. Neatly stem and core them with a grapefruit knife. Drop each half, as it is prepared, pared, into the acidulated water to keep it from discoloring. | Bring the wine, lemon juice, sugar, and cinnamon to the boil in the saucepan. Drain the pears, and drop into the boiling syrup; bring liquid to just below the simmer for 8 to 10 minutes or until pears are tender when pierced with a knife. Do not overcook; they must hold their shape. Remove saucepan from heat and let pears cool in the syrup for 20 minutes. Drain the pears on a rack. | Rapidly boil down the syrup to the thread stage (230 degrees). Measure out ¼ cup of syrup and simmer it with the red currant jelly until jelly has dissolved and the syrup coats the spoon with a light glaze.

name: TARTE AUX CERISES, FLAMBÉE (Cherry Tart Flambée)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: An 8-inch, fully cooked pastry shell , set in a fireproof serving dish
355 ml cold crème pâtissière (custard filling) , or frangipane (almond custard) , with 30 ml kirsch or cognac
60 ml kirsch or cognac, warmed in a small saucepan
authorNotes: For a spectacular entrance, sprinkle sugar over a cooked fruit tart, caramelize it briefly under the broiler, pour on liqueur, and ignite it as you enter the dining room. The following recipe is for cherries; you may use the same technique for the apricot or peach tart , and for the variations following it. | You may use canned Bing cherries or defrosted frozen cherries instead of fresh for this recipe. In this case, omit the first step in the directions, drain the cherries thoroughly, and let them stand for at least half an hour with 3 tablespoons of kirsch or cognac and as much sugar as you feel they need. Drain them again just before using, and beat their kirsch or cognac into the cream filling. | Wash and pit the cherries. Bring the wine, lemon juice, and sugar to the boil. Drop in the cherries, and bring the liquid to just below the simmer for 5 to 6 minutes or until cherries are tender, but retain their shape. Allow cherries to cool in the syrup for 20 to 30 minutes. Drain.

name: TARTE À L’ANANAS (Pineapple Tart)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 1 No. 2 can of sliced pineapple, pineapple wedges, or crushed pineapple (355 ml fruit and about 175 ml syrup)
355–475 ml chilled crème pâtissière (custard filling) with 30–45 ml kirsch or cognac
Optional: 60 ml diced red and green glacéed fruit and 60 ml slivered almonds
authorNotes: Drain the pineapple. Boil the canned pineapple syrup for 5 minutes in a saucepan. Add the pineapple and boil for 5 minutes more. Drain the pineapple, and allow it to cool. | Boil the pineapple syrup with the jelly and liqueur until it reduces to a glaze (last drops are sticky when dropped from a spoon, 228 degrees). | Paint the interior of the shell with a coating of the pineapple glaze. Spread the crème pâtissière in the pastry shell.

name: TARTE AU CITRON TARTE AUX LIMETTES (Lemon or Lime Soufflé Tart—hot)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A 10-inch, cooked, sugar-crust shell placed on a baking sheet (use only 45 ml sugar in the pastry)
A wire whip or electric beater
A 3- to 3.8 l stainless steel bowl
The grated rind of 1 lemon or 2 limes
45 ml lemon juice or lime juice
authorNotes: This delicious, light tart is really a soufflé. The same filling is also attractive in little tart shells served for afternoon tea. Speaking of these, see also the lemon butter filling . | When you bake the shell let it barely color so it will not brown too much when it goes again into the oven. | Preheat oven to 325 degrees.

name: TARTE AU CITRON ET AUX AMANDES (Lemon and Almond Tart—cold)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: An 8-inch, cooked, sugar-crust shell placed on a baking sheet (use only 45 ml sugar in the pastry)
An electric beater or wire whip
The grated rind and strained juice of 1½ lemons
authorNotes: When you bake the shell, let it barely color so it will not brown too much when it goes again into the oven. | Remove the yellow part of the lemon skin with a vegetable peeler; cut into julienne strips 1 / 16 inch wide and 2½ inches long. Simmer 10 to 12 minutes in water. Drain thoroughly. | Boil the sugar and ⅔ cup of water to the thread stage (230 degrees); add the vanilla and lemon peel. Let stand for 30 minutes.

name: TARTE AU FROMAGE FRAIS (Cream Cheese Tart—hot or cold)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: An 8-inch partially baked pastry shell placed on a baking sheet
A wooden spoon or electric beater
authorNotes: This is really a quiche , and very simple indeed to make. | Use the recipe for sweet short paste . | Preheat oven to 375 degrees.

name: VARIATION (Cream Cheese and Prune Tart)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
authorNotes: Tarte au Fromage Frais et aux Pruneaux | Soften prunes for 5 minutes in hot water. Drain, remove pits, and dice the prunes. Stir prunes, almonds, and almond extract into tart mixture after the eggs have been beaten in.

name: CRÊPES FINES SUCRÉES (Light Batter—for crêpes Suzette)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 45 ml orange liqueur, rum, or brandy
authorNotes: (If you do not have an electric blender proceed as follows: Gradually work the egg yolks into the flour with a wooden spoon, beat in the liquids by droplets, then strain the batter through a fine sieve.) | For 10 to 12 crêpes 6 inches in diameter, or 16 to 18 crêpes 4 to 5 inches in diameter | Place the ingredients in the blender jar in the order in which they are listed. Cover and blend at top speed for 1 minute. If bits of flour adhere to sides of jar, dislodge with a rubber scraper and blend 3 seconds more. Cover and refrigerate for at least 2 hours or overnight.

name: CRÊPES À LA LEVURE (Yeast Batter—for stuffed crêpes)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: Ingredients for the preceding crêpe batter
authorNotes: The addition of yeast makes a more tender and slightly thicker crêpe. | Warm ¼ cup of the milk to blood temperature (about 90 degrees) and allow the yeast to dissolve in it. Add it to the rest of the ingredients in the blender and proceed with the recipe. | Cover the batter with a towel and let it stand at room temperature for about 2 hours, or until the yeast has worked and the batter looks bubbly on top. Use immediately, or the yeast will overferment.

name: CRÊPES SOUFFLÉES (Raised Batter—for stuffed crêpes)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: Ingredients for one of the preceding crêpe batters, either plain or with yeast
authorNotes: Beaten egg whites folded into the batter makes the crêpes puff slightly. | After the batter has rested for 2 hours, and just before you wish to make your crêpes , beat the egg whites and salt until stiff. Fold half into the batter, fold in the other half, then make the crêpes .

name: CRÊPES SUZETTE (Crêpes with Orange Butter, Flambées)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: For the processor: 120 ml granulated sugar
By hand: 4 large sugar lumps and 60 ml sugar
18 cooked crêpes 4 to 5 inches in diameter
A chafing dish set over an alcohol flame
authorNotes: Every chef has his own recipe for crêpes Suzette ; of the many we have tried, we find this one especially good. Obviously if you plan to perform in public with a chafing dish, it is a good idea to practice on your family until you become adept at folding and flaming. Crêpes 4 to 5 inches in diameter are a convenient size, and three of these per person is the usual serving. | The orange butter (in a food processor, or by hand and electric mixer) | For the processor (steel blade), place the ½ cup of sugar in the container along with the orange part of the peel (stripped from the fruit). Process for a minute or so, scraping down sides of bowl with a rubber spatula as necessary, until peel and sugar are finely blended. Cut the butter into pieces, and process until smooth and almost fluffy. By droplets, cream in ½ cup of the orange juice, then the orange liqueur, plus droplets more of the juice if the mixture will take it and still remain creamy. Cover and refrigerate.

name: CRÊPES FOURRÉES ET FLAMBÉES (Crêpes with Orange-almond Butter, Flambées)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 120 ml pulverized almonds , or pulverized macaroons
The orange butter in the preceding recipe
18 cooked crêpes 4 to 5 inches in diameter
A lightly buttered baking dish suitable for serving
80 ml orange liqueur and 80 ml cognac warmed in a small saucepan
authorNotes: These crêpes are stuffed with orange-flavored almond butter, and may be flamed in a chafing dish, or brought flaming to the table as suggested here. | The orange-almond butter | Beat the almonds or macaroons and almond extract into the orange butter.

name: CRÊPES FOURRÉES, FRANGIPANE (Crêpes with Almond Cream)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 55 g or squares of semi-sweet baking chocolate
authorNotes: This is a much lighter filling than the preceding orange-almond butter. It may be set aflame if you wish, or served with chocolate as suggested in the recipe. | Use any of the three recipes for crêpes . | Spread 2 tablespoons of frangipane on the less-good side of each crêpe. Fold the crêpes into wedge shapes, or roll them, to enclose the filling, and arrange in the baking dish. Grate the chocolate over the crêpes , sprinkle on the melted butter, then the sugar.

name: GÂTEAU DE CRÊPES À LA NORMANDE (Mound of Crêpes with Apples, Flambé)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 0.9 kg crisp cooking or eating apples
A heavy-bottomed, 2.84 l pan: saucepan, casserole, or skillet
120 ml granulated sugar, more if needed
30 ml Calvados (apple brandy), cognac, or dark rum
10 to 12 cooked crêpes 6 inches in diameter
120 ml (85 g) pulverized almonds , or pulverized macaroons
30 ml slivered almonds or pulverized macaroons
120 ml Calvados (apple brandy), cognac, or dark rum, warmed in a small saucepan
authorNotes: Instead of stuffing each crêpe separately, you can pile them one upon the other with a layer of filling between each, as in this recipe. | Quarter, core, and peel the apples. Chop them roughly. You should have about 5 cups. Cook in a covered pan over low heat for about 20 minutes, stirring occasionally, until apples are tender. Uncover, add sugar, raise heat and boil, stirring, for 5 minutes or more. Applesauce should reduce and be thick enough to hold itself in a fairly solid mass in the spoon. Add more sugar while the apples are cooking if you feel it necessary. | Stir the cream, almond extract, and brandy or rum into the applesauce.

name: CLAFOUTI (Cherry Flan)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 1 ml salt 120 ml flour ( scooped and leveled )
A 7- to 1.9 l lightly buttered, fireproof baking dish or pyrex pie plate about 1½ inches deep
authorNotes: The clafouti (also spelled with a final “s” in both singular and plural) which is traditional in the Limousin during the cherry season is peasant cooking for family meals, and about as simple a dessert to make as you can imagine: a pancake batter poured over fruit in a fireproof dish, then baked in the oven. It looks like a tart, and is usually eaten warm. | (If you have no electric blender, work the eggs into the flour with a wooden spoon, gradually beat in the liquids, then strain the batter through a fine sieve.) | Preheat oven to 350 degrees.

name: Clafouti à la Liqueur (Cherry Flan with Liqueur)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
authorNotes: Follow the master recipe but first let the cherries stand for 1 hour in the kirsch or cognac and sugar. Substitute this liquid for part of the milk called for in the batter; omit the ⅓ sugar near the end of the recipe.

name: Clafouti aux Poires (Pear Flan)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: Ingredients for the master clafouti with changes as indicated
710 ml peeled, cored, and sliced ripe pears (565–680 g pears)
60 ml sweet white wine, kirsch, or cognac
authorNotes: Follow the master recipe with these changes: Substitute pears for cherries, and let stand for 1 hour in wine, kirsch, or cognac and sugar. Substitute this liquid for part of the milk called for in the batter; omit the ⅓ cup of sugar near the end of the recipe.

name: Clafouti aux Pruneaux (Plum Flan)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: Ingredients for the master clafouti with changes as indicated
60 ml orange liqueur, kirsch, or cognac
authorNotes: Follow the master recipe with these changes: Substitute plums for cherries, and drop in boiling water for exactly 10 seconds. Peel. Slice them or leave whole. Let stand with liqueur, kirsch, or cognac and sugar for 1 hour. Substitute this liquid for part of the milk called for in the batter; omit the ⅓ cup sugar near the end of the recipe.

name: Clafouti aux Pommes (Apple Flan)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: Ingredients for the master clafouti with changes as indicated
About 565 g crisp eating or cooking apples
60 ml Calvados (apple brandy), dark rum, or cognac
authorNotes: Follow the master recipe with these changes: Substitute apples for cherries; peel, core, and cut them into lengthwise slices ¼ inch thick. You should have about 3 cups. Sauté to brown very lightly in hot butter, then let stand in the skillet for ½ hour with the brandy or rum, cinnamon, and sugar. Substitute this liquid for part of the milk called for in the batter; omit the ⅓ cup sugar near the end of the recipe.

name: Clafouti aux Mûres Clafouti aux Myrtilles (Blackberry or Blueberry Flan)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: Ingredients for the master clafouti or the variation following it with changes as indicated
710 ml (about 565 g) stemmed and washed blackberries or blueberries
authorNotes: Follow the master recipe or the variation after it with these changes: Substitute berries for cherries and, because berries are very juicy, increase the flour for your batter from ⅔ to 1¼ cups.

name: Clafouti à la Bourdaloue (Cherry or Pear Flan with Almonds)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: Ingredients for either of the 2 cherry flans , or for the pear flan
120 ml blanched almonds (can be bought in a can)
authorNotes: Follow the master recipe for cherry flan , or the variation after it, or the recipe for pear flan , but purée the almonds in the blender with the milk called for in your batter. Add the almond extract, and proceed with the recipe.

name: PÂTE À BABA ET BABAS (Baba Paste and Babas)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 12 baba , popover, or muffin cups or muffin tins, about
2 inches deep and 2 inches in diameter
authorNotes: Melt the butter, and let it cool to tepid while you are preparing the other ingredients. | Blend the yeast and water in the bowl with a wire whip and let stand until yeast has dissolved completely. Beat in the sugar, salt, and eggs. | (Be sure to measure your flour correctly to get a soft pliable dough.) Mix the flour and the cool melted butter into the yeast with a wooden spoon.

name: Babas au Rhum (Rum Babas)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 120 ml dark rum, more if needed (Jamaican rum is recommended)
12 barely warm cooked babas , the preceding recipe
A dish 2 inches deep and just large enough to hold the babas easily
A skewer, trussing needle or sharp-pronged fork
A cake rack set over a tray
authorNotes: Both the babas and the rum syrup should be lukewarm but not hot before this operation begins. If the babas are cold, heat them briefly in the oven; warm the syrup if necessary, ( NOTE : Some authorities use a stronger sugar syrup, 1½ cups of sugar to 2 of water. We prefer the lighter syrup given here.) | Bring the water and sugar to a boil. Remove from heat and stir until sugar has dissolved. When the sugar syrup has cooled to lukewarm, stir in the rum; you may add a few tablespoons more if you feel it necessary. | The babas imbibe the syrup

name: Babas au Rhum, Classique (Rum Babas)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A serving dish or frilled paper cups
authorNotes: After the babas have drained, sprinkle the top of each with a few drops of rum. Paint them with the apricot glaze, and place a cherry on top of each. Arrange in a serving dish or in paper cups.

name: Babas aux Fruits (Rum Babas with Fruit)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 710 ml to 1 l blueberries or fresh strawberries
475–710 ml crème Chantilly , (lightly whipped cream) flavored with rum and powdered sugar
authorNotes: Arrange the babas in the serving dish. Surround them with the berries which have stood for 10 to 15 minutes in leftover baba syrup. Pass the cream separately.

name: SAVARIN
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A 4- to 1.2 l ring mold 2 inches deep
The master recipe for baba paste
475 ml sugar syrup (rum baba recipe) , but flavored with 120 ml kirsch rather than rum
A skewer, trussing needle, or sharp-pronged fork
A dish 2 inches deep and just large enough to hold the savarin easily
authorNotes: The savarin uses the same paste or dough as the baba , but is baked in a ring mold, and its sugar syrup is flavored with kirsch rather than rum. The center is then filled with a cream or with fruits macerated in liqueur. | Butter the ring mold. Make the baba paste as directed and let it rise in its bowl until doubled in bulk. Deflate it by pressing it rapidly in several places with the cupped fingers of one hand. Then break off a 2-tablespoon bit of the paste and press lightly into the bottom of the mold. Continue rapidly with the rest of the paste. The mold will be from a third to a half filled. Do not bother to smooth the surface of the paste; it will even out as it rises. Place uncovered in a warm place, 80 to 100 degrees, for 1 to 2 hours or until the paste has risen to fill the mold. Proceed at once to the following step. | Preheat oven to 375 degrees in time for this step.

name: Savarin Chantilly (Savarin with Whipped Cream)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 475 ml crème Chantilly (lightly whipped cream) , flavored with powdered sugar and kirsch
authorNotes: Paint the savarin with a light coating of apricot glaze. Cut the cherries in half, and the angelica into small diamond shapes. Press the fruits and almonds over the savarin in a decorative design and paint a bit of glaze over them. Fill the center of the savarin with the cream just before serving.

name: BISCUITS À LA CUILLER (Ladyfingers)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A pastry bag with a round tube opening
½ inch in diameter 355 ml powdered sugar in a sieve or a shaker
An electric beater or a wire whip
120 ml all-purpose flour ( scooped and leveled ), turned into a flour sifter
authorNotes: For 24 to 30 ladyfingers | Preheat oven to 300 degrees. | Prepare the baking sheets: butter lightly, dust with flour, and knock off excess flour. Assemble the pastry bag. Prepare the powdered sugar. Measure out all the rest of the ingredients listed in the recipe.

name: BISCUIT AU BEURRE (Butter Spongecake)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A round cake pan, 10 inches in diameter and 2 inches deep
An electric beater or large wire whip
175 ml cake flour ( scooped and leveled ), turned into a flour sifter
authorNotes: This fine, light spongecake may be served with a sprinkling of powdered sugar, and goes well with tea, or with fruits. It is also delicious as a strawberry shortcake. Or you may fill and decorate it as suggested at the end of the recipe. | For a 10-inch cake serving 10 to 12 people | Preheat oven to 350 degrees.

name: Glaçage à l’Abricot (Apricot Glaze with Almonds or Glacéed Fruits)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 60 ml slivered almonds or glacéed fruits
authorNotes: Follow the general procedure for icing a cake illustrated : Brush crumbs off top and sides of cake, paint cake with apricot glaze. Brush almonds against the sides and decorate top with slivered almonds or with glacéed fruits cut into dice or fancy shapes.

name: GÂTEAU À L’ORANGE (Orange Spongecake)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A round cake pan 9 by 1½ inches
175 ml cake flour ( scooped and leveled ), turned into a sifter
authorNotes: For a 9-inch cake serving 8 people | Preheat oven to 350 degrees. | Butter and flour the cake pan . Measure out the ingredients.

name: Gâteau Fourré à la Crème d’ Orange (Spongecake with Orange-butter Filling)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A 9- to 10-inch cake: the preceding orange spongecake or the butter spongecake
235 ml pulverized almonds, plain or toasted
authorNotes: This orange-butter filling may be used for cakes, or as a filling for tartlets or cookies. When softened butter is beaten into it, as described in the variation at the end of the recipe, it may also serve as an icing. | For about 2 cups, enough to fill a 9- to 10-inch cake | The orange-butter filling

name: Crème au Beurre à l’Orange (Orange Butter-cream Icing)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 475 ml orange-butter filling, the preceding recipe
A wire whip or an electric beater
115 g (1 stick) softened, unsalted butter, 30–45 ml more if necessary
authorNotes: For a richer filling, or for an icing, you may turn the preceding filling into a crème au beurre , which resembles the butter cream . In the following recipe, we have suggested that you use half the original orange-butter filling for inside the cake, and beat butter into the rest to make a butter-cream icing. | For 1 cup filling and 2 cups icing , enough for a 9- to 10-inch cake | Use 1 cup of the filling to spread inside your cake as described in the preceding recipe. Re-form the cake. Place the rest of the filling in the mixing bowl and gradually beat in the softened butter. The mixture should thicken into a smooth, mayonnaiselike cream; if it looks grainy, beat in more butter a tablespoon at a time. Chill until firm but still of spreading consistency.

name: GÂTEAU À L’ORANGE ET AUX AMANDES (Orange and Almond Sponge cake)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A round cake pan, 9 by 1½ inches
120 ml cake flour ( scooped and leveled ), turned into a sifter
authorNotes: This delicious cake may be served with a sprinkling of powdered sugar, with a glazing of apricot, or with a filling and icing. | For a 9-inch cake serving 8 people | Preheat oven to 350 degrees.

name: REINE DE SABA (Chocolate and Almond Cake)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A round cake pan 8 inches in diameter and 1½ inches deep
115 g or squares semi-sweet chocolate melted with 30 ml rum or coffee
A wooden spoon or an electric beater
120 ml cake flour ( scooped and leveled ), turned into a sifter
authorNotes: This extremely good chocolate cake is baked so that its center remains slightly underdone; overcooked, the cake loses its special creamy quality. It is covered with a chocolate-butter icing, and decorated with almonds. Because of its creamy center it needs no filling. It can be made in the same manner as the preceding cakes, starting out with a beating of egg yolks and sugar, then proceeding with the rest of the ingredients. But because the chocolate and the almonds make a batter so stiff it is difficult to fold in the egg whites, we have chosen another method, that of creaming together the butter and sugar, and then incorporating the remaining items. | For an 8-inch cake serving 6 to 8 people | Preheat oven to 350 degrees.

name: LE MARQUIS (Chocolate Sponge cake)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A round cake pan 8 inches in diameter and 1½ inches deep
A pan of simmering water A wooden spoon
A wire whip or electric beater
80 ml cake flour ( scooped and leveled ), turned into a sifter
authorNotes: For an 8-inch cake serving 6 to 8 people | Preheat oven to 350 degrees. | Butter and flour the cake pan . Measure out the ingredients.

name: CRÈME AU BEURRE, MÉNAGÈRE (Butter Cream I—with powdered sugar)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: 30 ml kirsch, rum, orange liqueur, or strong coffee, OR, 15 ml vanilla extract, OR, 55 g (2 squares) melted, semisweet, baking chocolate
An electric beater (or a wire whip)
authorNotes: This should be made with an electric beater; it is heavy work by hand. | Rinse the bowl in hot water, dry it, and place in it all the ingredients listed. Beat at a moderate speed for about 5 minutes to obtain a smooth cream. Chill until the cream is cold but still malleable, then fill and ice your cake.

name: CRÈME AU BEURRE, AU SUCRE CUIT (Butter Cream II—with sugar syrup)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A wooden spoon or an electric beater
5 egg yolks, OR, 1 egg and 3 yolks
A large wire whip (or an electric beater)
A pan of not-quite-simmering water, large enough to hold the bowl
A wooden spatula or spoon, or an electric beater
30–45 ml kirsch, rum, orange liqueur, or strong coffee, OR, 15 ml vanilla extract, OR, 55 g (2 squares) melted, semi-sweet, baking chocolate
30–60 ml softened, unsalted butter, if needed
authorNotes: You may use either a wire whip or an electric beater for most of the steps in this recipe. We find, however, that a large balloon whip, such as that illustrated for beating egg whites is the quickest and most effective instrument for the beating of egg yolks and sugar syrup step. | Cream the butter until it is light and fluffy. (Directions are here .) Set aside. | Place the egg yolks (or egg and yolks) in the bowl and beat a few seconds to blend thoroughly. Set aside.

name: CRÈME AU BEURRE, À L’ ANGLAISE (Butter Cream III—with custard base)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A wire whip or electric beater
A 2.37 l mixing bowl 120 ml boiling milk
A wire whip or electric beater
225 g (2 sticks) softened, unsalted butter, more if needed
30–45 ml kirsch, rum, orange liqueur, or strong coffee, OR, 15 ml vanilla extract OR, 55 g (2 squares) melted, semi-sweet, baking chocolate
authorNotes: This final recipe is simpler to make than the preceding one with its sugar syrup and its poaching of egg yolks. The custard base here is that most familiar of custard sauces, crème anglaise which, when cooled, receives the butter. | The crème anglaise (custard sauce) | Following the general procedure in the master recipe for crème anglaise gradually beat the sugar into the egg yolks in the bowl until mixture is thick, pale yellow, and forms the ribbon. Then beat in the boiling milk by droplets. Pour into saucepan and stir with wooden spoon over moderately low heat until mixture thickens enough to coat spoon with a light cream (165 degrees on candy thermometer). At once set saucepan in cold water and beat until custard has cooled to barely tepid. Rinse out mixing bowl and strain custard back into it.

name: GLAÇAGE AU CHOCOLAT (Chocolate-butter Icing)
sourceBook: Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 — Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, Simone Beck (Alfred A. Knopf, 1961)
ingredients: A small covered pan A larger pan of almost simmering water
A bowl with a tray of ice cubes and water to cover them
A small flexible-blade metal spatula or a table knife
authorNotes: Place the chocolate and rum or coffee in the small pan, cover, and set in the larger pan of almost simmering water. Remove pans from heat and let chocolate melt for 5 minutes or so, until perfectly smooth. Lift chocolate pan out of the hot water, and beat in the butter a tablespoon at a time. Then beat over the ice and water until chocolate mixture has cooled to spreading consistency. At once spread it over your cake with spatula or knife.

name: Crostini Bianchi—Ricotta and Anchovy Canapés
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1 tablespoon butter, softened to room temperature
8 flat anchovy fillets (preferably the ones prepared at home)
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
7 slices good-quality, firm white toasting bread
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: You can prepare the crostini 2 or 3 hours in advance. Spread the ricotta and anchovy cream over the bread just before serving.

name: Hard-Boiled Eggs with Green Sauce
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ½ tablespoon chopped capers, soaked and rinsed if packed in salt, drained if in vinegar
3 flat anchovy fillets (preferably the ones prepared at home), chopped very fine
¼ teaspoon English or Dijon-style mustard
A sweet red bell pepper, diced not too fine

name: Roasted Peppers and Anchovies
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 8 sweet red and/or yellow bell peppers
16 large anchovy fillets (preferably the ones prepared at home)
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
3 tablespoons capers, soaked and rinsed if packed in salt, drained if packed in vinegar
¼ cup extra virgin olive oil
authorNotes: Note: Red and yellow peppers alone, roasted and skinned as described above, lightly salted, laid flat in a deep dish, and covered with extra virgin olive oil make a sensationally delicious appetizer. It would look most appealing on a buffet table, or take an important place in an assortment of dishes for a light lunch.

name: Roasted Eggplant with Peppers and Cucumber
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ½ teaspoon garlic chopped very fine
½ cup sweet red bell pepper, diced into ⅓-inch cubes
¼ cup yellow bell pepper, diced like the red pepper
½ cup cucumber, diced like the bell pepper
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill

name: Marinated Carrot Sticks
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill

name: Carciofi alla Romana—Artichokes, Roman Style
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 6 to 8 fresh mint leaves, chopped fine
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
½ cup extra virgin olive oil

name: Mushroom, Parmesan Cheese, and White Truffle Salad
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ½ pound firm, sound fresh mushrooms (see introductory note above)
1 to 2 tablespoons freshly squeezed lemon juice
⅔ cup celery cut crosswise into ¼-inch slices
⅔ cup parmigiano-reggiano cheese, shaved into flakes with a vegetable peeler or on a mandoline
OPTIONAL: a 1-ounce or larger white truffle
3 tablespoons extra virgin olive oil (see introductory note above)
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill

name: Tomatoes Stuffed with Shrimp
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ¾ pound small raw shrimp in the shell
Mayonnaise, made as directed, using the yolk of 1 large egg, ½ cup vegetable oil, and 2½ to 3 tablespoons freshly squeezed lemon juice
1½ tablespoons capers, soaked and rinsed if packed in salt, drained if packed in vinegar

name: Tomatoes Stuffed with Tuna
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 seven-ounce cans imported Italian tuna packed in olive oil
Mayonnaise, made as directed, using the yolk of 1 large egg, ½ cup vegetable oil, and 2 tablespoons freshly squeezed lemon juice
1½ tablespoons capers, soaked and rinsed if packed in salt, drained if packed in vinegar

name: In Carpione—Fried Marinated Fresh Sardines (or other fish)
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1 pound fresh sardines OR smelts OR other small fish OR ¾ pound fish fillets
½ cup flour, spread on a plate
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill

name: Cold Trout in Orange Marinade
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 3 trout, perch, or other fine freshwater fish, about ¾ pound each, gutted and scaled, but with heads and tails left on
½ cup extra virgin olive oil
½ cup flour, spread on a plate
2 tablespoons chopped orange peel, using only the rind, not the white pith beneath it
½ cup freshly squeezed orange juice
The freshly squeezed juice of 1 lemon
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill

name: Gamberetti all’Olio e Limone—Poached Shrimp with Olive Oil and Lemon Juice
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1½ pounds choice small raw shrimp in the shell (if only larger shrimp are available, see note below)
½ cup extra virgin olive oil
¼ cup freshly squeezed lemon juice
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
authorNotes: Note: The dish is far better if never chilled, but if you are compelled to, you can prepare it a day in advance and refrigerate it under plastic wrap. Always return it to full room temperature before serving.

name: Insalata Russa—Shrimp Salad with Assorted Vegetables
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1 pound medium shrimp in the shell
⅓ of a 10-ounce package frozen peas, thawed
6 small canned whole red beets, drained
2 tablespoons capers, soaked and rinsed if packed in salt, drained if in vinegar
2 tablespoons fine cucumber pickles, preferably cornichons, cut up
Mayonnaise, made as directed, using the yolks of 3 eggs, 1¾ cups extra virgin olive oil (see note below), 3 tablespoons freshly squeezed lemon juice, and ⅜ teaspoon salt
authorNotes: Note: Here is one of the uncommon occasions when the snappy flavor of good olive oil in mayonnaise is more desirable than the mildness of vegetable oil. Its density is also useful in pulling together the ingredients of the salad, making it more compact.; Ahead-of-time note: You can make the salad up to 2 days in advance, refrigerating it under plastic wrap, but take it out in sufficient time to be able to serve it not too much colder than room temperature. Caution: If you are preparing the dish several hours or even a day in advance, use the beets in your decorative pattern at the last moment because their color tends to run.

name: Salmon Foam
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ¼ cup extra virgin olive oil
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
1½ cups very cold heavy whipping cream
authorNotes: Optional garnish Spoon individual servings onto radicchio leaves, shaping the salmon foam into small, rounded mounds. Top each mound with a black olive—preferably not the Greek variety, but a milder one, such as the ones from California, and align half slices of lemon on either side of the olive, embedding them in the salmon.

name: Poached Tuna and Potato Roll
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 seven-ounce cans imported Italian tuna packed in olive oil, drained
¼ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese
1 whole egg plus the white of 1 egg
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
½ medium yellow onion, sliced thin
The stems only of 6 parsley sprigs
2 tablespoons coarsely chopped capers, soaked and rinsed if packed in salt, drained if in vinegar
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: : The roll can be finished up to this point one or two days in advance. When it has cooled down completely, cover tightly with plastic wrap and refrigerate. Take it out in time for it to come to room temperature before proceeding with the next step.

name: Poached Tuna and Spinach Roll
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: A 3½-ounce can imported Italian tuna packed in olive oil, drained
4 flat anchovy fillets (preferably the ones prepared at home), chopped fine
1½ slices good-quality white bread, trimmed of the crust
3 tablespoons fine, dry, unflavored bread crumbs
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
⅓ cup extra virgin olive oil
1 small carrot, sliced into very thin rounds

name: Bruschetta—Roman Garlic Bread
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 12 slices good, thick-crusted bread, ½ to ¾ inch thick, 3 to 4 inches wide
Extra virgin olive oil, fruity and young
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
All the ingredients given in the recipe above plus
8 to 12 fresh basil leaves OR a few pinches oregano

name: Carciofi alla Giudia—Crisp-Fried Whole Artichokes
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 6 medium artichokes, as young and fresh as possible
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill

name: Baked Stuffed Mushroom Caps
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: A packet dried porcini mushrooms OR, if bought loose, about 1 ounce
¼ heaping cup crumb (the fresh, soft, crustless part of bread)
4 flat anchovy fillets (preferably the ones prepared at home)
4 fresh basil leaves, torn by hand into small pieces
A small garlic clove, chopped fine
⅛ teaspoon dried marjoram OR ¼ teaspoon chopped fresh
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
½ cup dried, unflavored bread crumbs
⅓ cup extra virgin olive oil

name: Bagna Caôda—Hot Piedmontese Dip for Raw Vegetables
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ¾ cup extra virgin olive oil
8 to 10 anchovy fillets (preferably the ones prepared at home), chopped fine
authorNotes: Artichokes You don’t need to trim artichokes for bagna caôda as you do for other preparations. Rinse the artichoke in cold water and serve it whole or, if very large, cut it in half. If you cut it, rub the cut parts with lemon juice. To eat, one pulls off a leaf at a time, dips it, holding it by its tip, and bites off just the tender bottom.; Broccoli Not a Piedmontese vegetable, but very nice all the same. Cut off the florets and set them aside for any other recipe calling for broccoli. Serve just the stalks, after paring away the tough, outer skin.; Spinach Use only young, crisp spinach. Wash thoroughly in many changes of cold water until all traces of soil are gone. Serve with the stems because they provide a handy hold for dipping.; Sweet Red and Yellow Bell Peppers Wash in cold water and cut lengthwise into quarter sections. Remove the seeds and pulpy inner core.; Celery Cut in half, lengthwise or, if very thick, in quarters. Discard bruised or blemished outer stalks. Wash well in cold water.; Carrots Peel the carrots and cut them lengthwise into strips ½ inch thick.; Radishes Cut off the whiskery root tip, wash in cold water, and serve with the stems and leaves on.; Jerusalem Artichokes Soak them for a few minutes in cold water. Peel them using a potato peeler, but it isn’t necessary to pare away every bit of the peel as it is edible.; Zucchini Not a winter vegetable either, but if it’s there and it’s good, why pass it up? Do choose only the freshest, glossiest, small young zucchini. Soak in a large bowl filled with cold water for at least 20 minutes. Rinse thoroughly under cold running water, rubbing briskly with your hands or a rough cloth to remove any grit still embedded in the skin. Trim away both ends. Cut lengthwise into pieces 1 inch thick.

name: Ostriche alla Tarantina—Baked Oysters with Oil and Parsley
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 36 live oysters, washed, scrubbed, shucked, and each placed on a half shell
1½ tablespoons fine, dry, unflavored bread crumbs
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
¼ cup extra virgin olive oil

name: Grilled Mussels and Clams on the Half Shell
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 dozen littleneck clams, as small as possible
½ teaspoon garlic chopped very fine
⅓ cup extra virgin olive oil
½ cup fine, dry, unflavored bread crumbs
4 ripe, fresh plum tomatoes Lemon wedges

name: Sautéed Scallops with Garlic and Parsley
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ½ pound fresh bay scallops, OR large sea scallops cut into 3 or 4 pieces
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
1½ tablespoons fine, dry, unflavored bread crumbs
4 scallop shells, available in most cooking equipment shops, OR 4 small gratin dishes

name: Arrosticini Abruzzesi—Skewered Marinated Lamb Tidbits
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
½ teaspoon dried marjoram OR 1 teaspoon fresh chopped

name: Minestrone alla Romagnola—Vegetable Soup, Romagna Style
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ½ cup extra virgin olive oil
3 cups shredded Savoy cabbage OR regular cabbage
1½ cups canned cannellini beans, drained, OR ¾ cup dried white kidney beans, soaked and cooked as directed
6 cups Basic Homemade Meat Broth, OR 2 cups canned beef broth plus 4 cups water
OPTIONAL: the crust from a 1- to 2-pound piece of parmigiano-reggiano cheese, carefully scraped clean
⅔ cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, with their juice
⅓ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: Minestrone, unlike most cooked vegetable preparations, is even better when reheated the following day. It will keep up to a week in a tightly sealed container in the refrigerator.

name: Summer Vegetable Soup with Rice and Basil, Milan Style
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ½ cup rice, preferably Italian Arborio rice
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
¼ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese
8 to 10 fresh basil leaves, torn into several small strips
authorNotes: Note: Do not serve the soup any later than the day it is made, and do not refrigerate it before serving.

name: Spring Vegetable Soup
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1 pound fresh peas, weighed unshelled, OR ½ of a 10-ounce package frozen peas, thawed
⅓ cup extra virgin olive oil
1 pound boiling potatoes, peeled, washed, and cut into ¼-inch slices
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
OPTIONAL: 1 slice per serving of toasted crusty bread lightly rubbed with garlic
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The soup may be cooked up through Step 8 several hours ahead of time. Reheat gently and finish as in Step 9.

name: Spinach Soup
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 pounds fresh spinach OR 2 ten-ounce packages frozen whole leaf spinach, thawed
2 cups Basic Homemade Meat Broth, OR 1 cup canned beef broth diluted with 1 cup water

name: Crostini
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ½ inch up the side of the pan
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: Crostini are at their best when made just before serving. They may be prepared several hours ahead of time, however, and kept at room temperature. Do not keep overnight because they are likely to acquire a stale, rancid taste.

name: Spinach or Escarole Soup with Rice
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1 head escarole OR 1 pound fresh spinach
3½ cups Basic Homemade Meat Broth, OR 1 cup canned beef broth diluted with 2½ cups water OR 2 bouillon cubes dissolved with 3½ cups water
⅓ cup rice, preferably Italian Arborio rice
authorNotes: Note: The consistency of the soup should be dense, but still fairly runny on the spoon. If you find, while the rice is cooking, that it is becoming too thick, add a ladleful of water or of the spinach liquid, if available. But make sure not to dilute the soup too much.; Ahead-of-time note: Once the rice is in the soup, it must be finished and served at once, otherwise the rice will become mushy. If you must cook it in advance, stop at the end of Step 2, and resume cooking when ready to serve. Do start the soup the same day you plan to have it, and do not refrigerate it.

name: Risi e Bisi—Rice and Peas
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 pounds fresh, young peas, weighed with the pods
½ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese

name: Rice and Smothered Cabbage Soup
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: Smothered cabbage (It can be prepared 2 or 3 days ahead of time.)
3 cups Basic Homemade Meat Broth, OR 1 cup canned beef broth diluted with 2 cups water OR 1½ bouillon cubes dissolved in 3 cups warm water
⅔ cup rice, preferably Italian Arborio rice
⅓ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill

name: Minestrina Tricolore—Potato Soup with Carrots and Celery
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 5 tablespoons freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese, plus additional cheese at the table
2 cups Basic Homemade Meat Broth, OR ½ cup canned beef broth diluted with 1 ½ cups water

name: Potato Soup with Smothered Onions
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 3½ cups Basic Homemade Meat Broth, OR ½ cup canned beef broth diluted with 3 cups water
3 tablespoons freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese, plus additional cheese at the table

name: Potato and Green Pea Soup
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 3 cups onion cut into very thin slices
2 garlic cloves, peeled and cut into paper-thin slices
3 cups potatoes, peeled and cut into very, very fine dice
Basic Homemade Meat Broth, enough to cover all ingredients by 2 inches, OR 1 beef bouillon cube
2 pounds fresh peas, unshelled weight, OR 1 ten-ounce package frozen peas, thawed
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
Freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese for the table
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: You can make the soup a day in advance, and reheat it gently just before serving.

name: Potato Soup with Split Green Peas
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ½ pound split dried green peas
5 cups Basic Homemade Meat Broth, OR 1 cup canned beef broth diluted with 4 cups water OR 1 bouillon cube dissolved in 5 cups water
3 tablespoons freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese, plus additional cheese at the table

name: Lentil Soup
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ⅓ cup shredded pancetta OR prosciutto OR unsmoked country ham
1 cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, cut up, with their juice
4 cups Basic Homemade Meat Broth, OR 1 cup canned beef broth diluted with 3 cups water
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
3 tablespoons freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese, plus additional cheese at the table
1½ cups Basic Homemade Meat Broth, OR ½ cup canned beef broth diluted with 1 cup water
½ cup rice, preferably Italian Arborio rice
3 tablespoons freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese, plus additional cheese at the table
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The soup can be made in advance, even in large batches, and frozen, if desired. When making it ahead of time, stop at the end of Step 4, and add the butter and cheese only after reheating and just before serving.

name: Lentil Soup with Pasta, Bacon, and Garlic
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: Extra virgin olive oil, 2 tablespoons for cooking, plus more for stirring into the soup
¼ pound bacon chopped very fine
⅓ cup fresh, ripe, firm tomatoes, skinned raw with a peeler, all seeds removed, and chopped, OR canned Italian plum tomatoes, cut up, with their juice
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
¼ cup freshly grated romano cheese (see note below)
authorNotes: Note: Romano is the most widely available export version of cheese made from ewe’s milk. All such cheeses are known in Italian as pecorino. Romano is, regrettably, the sharpest of these, and if you should come across a better pecorino of grating consistency, such as fiore sardo or a Tuscan cacciotta, use it in place of romano, increasing the quantity to ⅓ cup, or more to taste.; Ahead-of-time note: You can make the soup up to this point several hours or even a day or two in advance. Reheat thoroughly, adding water if necessary, before proceeding with the next step.

name: White Bean Soup with Garlic and Parsley
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ½ cup extra virgin olive oil
2 cups dried cannellini OR other white beans, soaked and cooked and drained, OR 6 cups canned cannellini beans, drained
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
1 cup Basic Homemade Meat Broth, OR ⅓ cup canned beef broth diluted with ⅔ cup water
2 tablespoons chopped parsley OPTIONAL: thick grilled slices of crusty bread

name: Pasta e Fagioli—Pasta and Bean Soup
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ¼ cup extra virgin olive oil
3 or 4 pork ribs, OR a ham bone with some lean meat attached, OR 2 little pork chops
⅔ cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, cut up, with their juice, OR fresh tomatoes, if ripe and firm, peeled and cut up
2 pounds fresh cranberry beans, unshelled weight, OR 1 cup dried cranberry or red kidney beans, soaked and cooked, OR 3 cups canned cranberry or red kidney beans, drained
3 cups (or more if needed) Basic Homemade Meat Broth, OR 1 cup canned beef broth diluted with 2 cups water
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
Either maltagliati pasta, homemade with 1 egg and ⅔ cup flour, OR ½ pound small, tubular macaroni
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: You can prepare the soup almost entirely in advance but stop at the end of Step 5. Add and cook the pasta or rice only when you are going to make the soup ready for serving.

name: Acquacotta—Tuscan Peasant Soup with Cabbage and Beans
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 4 cups onion sliced rather thick, about ⅓ inch
½ cup extra virgin olive oil
3 cups celery chopped fine, the leaves included
3 cups Savoy cabbage shredded very fine
2 cups kale leaves, chopped very fine
1 cup fresh, ripe, firm tomato, skinned raw with a peeler, seeds removed, and cut into ¼-inch dice
8 fresh basil leaves, torn into 2 or 3 pieces
⅓ cup dried cannellini beans, soaked and cooked and drained
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
An oven-to-table ceramic casserole with a lid
12 thin toasted slices day-old Tuscan-style or other good country bread OR Olive Oil Bread
⅓ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese
⅓ cup freshly squeezed lemon juice
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: You may complete the soup up to this point several hours or even a day in advance. When keeping it overnight, if you have a cold place to store it, it would be preferable to use it instead of the refrigerator, which tends to give cooked greens a somewhat sour taste. Reheat completely before proceeding with the next step.

name: La Jota—Beans and Sauerkraut Soup
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 pounds fresh cranberry beans, unshelled weight, OR 1 cup dried cranberry beans or red kidney beans, soaked and cooked
¾ pound fresh pork jowl, OR pig’s feet, OR pork hock, see remarks above
¼ cup salt pork chopped fine to a pulp either with a knife or in the food processor
authorNotes: Note: If unfamiliar with cranberry beans, see the recipe for Pasta e fagioli Soup.; Ahead-of-time note: Although La Jota requires hours of slow cooking, these can be staggered and scheduled at your convenience because the soup should be served a day or two after it has been made to give its flavors time to develop fully and merge. You can interrupt its preparation whenever you have completed one of its major steps. Allow the soup to cool, refrigerate it, and on the following day resume cooking where you left off. Prepare and add the pestà, however, only when ready to serve.

name: Novara’s Bean and Vegetable Soup
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ¼ pound pork rind OR fresh side pork (pork belly)
2 medium onions, sliced very thin, about 1 cup
1 large stalk of celery, washed and diced
2 medium zucchini, washed, then trimmed of both ends and diced
1 pound fresh cranberry beans, unshelled weight, OR 1 cup dried cranberry or red kidney beans, soaked and drained but not cooked
⅓ cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, cut up, with their juice
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
3 cups Basic Homemade Meat Broth, OR 1 cup canned beef broth diluted with 2 cups water
Freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese for the table

name: Bean and Red Cabbage Soup
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ¾ pound pork rind OR fresh pig’s feet OR pork hock
¼ cup extra virgin olive oil
1 pound shredded red cabbage, about 4 cups
3 tablespoons canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, drained
3 cups (or more) Basic Homemade Meat Broth, OR 1 cup canned beef broth diluted with 2 cups water
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
½ pound mild fresh pork sausage that does not contain fennel seeds or other herbs
1 cup dried cannellini OR other white kidney beans, soaked and cooked, OR 3 cups canned cannellini beans, drained
OPTIONAL: thick slices of grilled or toasted crusty bread
2 to 3 garlic cloves, lightly mashed with a knife handle and peeled
½ teaspoon chopped dried rosemary leaves OR a small sprig of fresh rosemary

name: Chick Pea Soup
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ⅓ cup extra virgin olive oil
1½ teaspoons dried rosemary leaves, crushed fine almost to a powder, OR a small sprig of fresh rosemary
⅔ cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, cut up, with their juice
¾ cup dried chick peas, soaked and cooked, OR 2¼ cups canned chick peas, drained
1 cup Basic Homemade Meat Broth, OR 1 bouillon cube dissolved in 1 cup water
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
3 cups (or more) Basic Homemade Meat Broth, OR 2 bouillon cubes dissolved with 3 cups water
1 cup rice, preferably Italian Arborio rice
2 cups (or more) Basic Homemade Meat Broth, OR 2 bouillon cubes dissolved with 2 cups water
Either maltagliati pasta, homemade with 1 egg and ⅔ cup flour, OR ½ pound small, tubular macaroni
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The soup can be made in advance and refrigerated for at least a week in a tightly sealed container. If making it ahead of time, do not add any salt or pepper until you reheat it just before serving.

name: Barley Soup in the Style of Trent
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ¼ cup plus 2 tablespoons extra virgin olive oil
⅓ cup prosciutto OR pancetta OR country ham OR boiled unsmoked ham, chopped fine
½ teaspoon dried rosemary leaves OR 1 teaspoon fresh chopped very fine
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
2 to 3 tablespoons freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The soup may be prepared one or two days in advance, but add the grated cheese only when you reheat it.

name: Broccoli and Egg Barley Soup
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ⅓ cup extra virgin olive oil
⅔ cup manfrigul, homemade pasta barley, OR cooked barley, OR ½ cup small, coarse, boxed soup pasta
Freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese at the table

name: Passatelli—Egg and Parmesan Strands in Broth
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ¾ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese, plus additional cheese at the table
⅓ cup fine, dry, unflavored bread crumbs
authorNotes: Note: The flavor of good homemade meat broth is so vital to this soup that no commercial substitute should be used.

name: Stuffed Lettuce Soup
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ½ pound veal, any cut as long as it is all solid meat
1 whole chicken breast, boned and skinned
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
½ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese, plus additional cheese for each serving
1 teaspoon fresh marjoram OR ¾ teaspoon dried
For each serving: 1 slice of bread toasted dark or browned in butter

name: Clam Soup
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 3 dozen small littleneck clams live in their shells
½ cup extra virgin olive oil
1½ tablespoons shallots OR onions chopped very fine
¼ teaspoon cornstarch dissolved in ⅔ cup dry white wine
⅛ teaspoon chopped hot red chili pepper
For each serving: 1 thick slice of crusty bread, grilled
authorNotes: Note: In Italy, no cook book or cook will ever advise you to discard any clams that don’t open while cooking. Clams stay clamped shut because they are alive. The most reluctant ones to loosen their hold and unclench their shells are the most vigorously alive of all. When eating them raw on the half shell, how does anyone know which ones would not have opened in the pot? The clams you should discard are those that stay open when you handle them before cooking, because they are dead.

name: Clam and Pea Soup
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 3 dozen small littleneck clams live in their shells
3 pounds fresh peas, unshelled weight, OR 3 ten-ounce packages frozen peas, thawed
⅓ cup extra virgin olive oil
⅔ cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, cut up, with their juice
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill

name: Mussel Soup
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 pounds mussels live in their shells
⅓ cup extra virgin olive oil
1 cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, drained and cut up
⅛ teaspoon chopped hot red chili pepper
For each serving: 1 thick slice of grilled or browned in the oven crusty bread, lightly rubbed with a peeled mashed garlic clove

name: Squid and Artichoke Soup
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1 pound fresh OR frozen whole squid, thawed, preferably with sacs under 5 inches long
½ cup extra virgin olive oil
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
For each serving: 1 thick slice of grilled or browned in the oven crusty bread, rubbed lightly with a peeled mashed garlic clove

name: Tomato Sauce with Onion and Butter
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 pounds fresh, ripe tomatoes, prepared as described, OR 2 cups canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, cut up, with their juice
1 medium onion, peeled and cut in half
Freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese for the table
authorNotes: Note: May be frozen when done. Discard the onion before freezing.

name: Tomato Sauce with Olive Oil and Chopped Vegetables
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 pounds fresh, ripe tomatoes, prepared as described, OR 2 cups canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, cut up, with their juice
⅓ cup extra virgin olive oil
Marjoram, 2 teaspoons if fresh, 1 if dried
2 teaspoons dried rosemary leaves, chopped very fine, OR a small sprig of fresh rosemary
½ cup pancetta sliced thin and cut into narrow julienne strips
authorNotes: Note: May be frozen when done.; Recommended pasta Excellent with spaghetti, but even better with the thicker, hollow shape, bucatini or perciatelli.; Recommended pasta A shape with crevices or hollows, such as ruote di carro (“cartwheels”), or conchiglie, or fusilli, would be a good choice.

name: Tomato Sauce withSautéed Vegetables and Olive Oil
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 pounds fresh, ripe tomatoes, prepared as described, OR 2 cups canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, cut up, with their juice
authorNotes: Note: May be frozen when done.

name: Tomato Sauce with Heavy Cream
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 3 tablespoons each onion, carrot, celery, all chopped very fine
2½ pounds fresh, ripe tomatoes, prepared by the food mill method, OR 2½ cups canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, with their juice
Freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese for the table
authorNotes: Note: May be frozen up to this point.

name: Tomato Sauce with Garlic and Basil
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 pounds fresh, ripe tomatoes, prepared as described, OR 2 cups canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, drained and cut up
5 garlic cloves, peeled and chopped fine
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
authorNotes: Recommended pasta The ideal shape for tomato alla carrettiera is thin spaghetti—spaghettini—but regular spaghetti would also be satisfactory.

name: Amatriciana—Tomato Saucewith Pancetta and Chili Pepper
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: A ¼-inch-thick slice of pancetta, cut into strips ½ inch wide and 1 inch long
1½ cups canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, drained and cut up
Chopped hot red chili pepper, to taste

name: Tomato Sauce with Porcini Mushrooms
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 tablespoons shallot OR onion chopped fine
2 tablespoons pancetta, prosciutto, OR unsmoked ham, cut into ¼-inch-wide strips
1½ cups fresh, ripe tomatoes, peeled and chopped, OR canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, cut up, with their juice
A small packet OR 1 ounce dried porcini mushrooms, reconstituted
Filtered water from the mushroom soak
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
Freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese for the table

name: Mushroom Sauce with Ham and Tomato
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ⅓ cup extra virgin olive oil
2 garlic cloves, peeled and lightly mashed
⅓ cup boiled unsmoked ham cut into very narrow julienne strips, ⅛ inch wide or less
A small packet OR 1 ounce dried porcini mushrooms, reconstituted
Filtered water from the mushroom soak
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
1 cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, chopped fine, with their juice
authorNotes: Note: Do not expect the fresh mushrooms to be firm; they are being cooked in the manner of porcini and, like porcini, they will be very tender when done.

name: Eggplant Sauce with Tomato and Red Chili Pepper
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: Vegetable oil for frying the eggplant
1¾ cups canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, cut up, with their juice
Chopped hot red chili pepper, to taste
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: You can fry the eggplant a day or two in advance of making the sauce, or make the entire sauce in advance and refrigerate it for up to 3 or 4 days before reheating.

name: Eggplant and Ricotta Sauce, Sicilian Style
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ⅓ cup extra virgin olive oil
½ cup onion sliced very thin
2 cups fresh, ripe Italian plum tomatoes, skinned with a peeler, split lengthwise to pick out the seeds, and cut into narrow strips
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
Freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese for the table

name: Spinach Sauce with Ricotta and Ham
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 pounds fresh spinach OR 2 ten-ounce packages frozen leaf spinach, thawed
½ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese, plus additional cheese at the table

name: Peas, Bacon, and Ricotta Sauce
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1 pound fresh, young peas, unshelled weight, OR ½ of a 10-ounce package tiny frozen peas, thawed
¼ pound bacon, preferably lean, slab bacon
⅓ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese, plus additional cheese at the table
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill

name: Peas, Peppers, and Prosciutto Sauce with Cream
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: A ½-inch-thick slice of prosciutto OR country ham, OR plain boiled unsmoked ham, about 6 ounces, diced very fine
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
1 cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese, plus additional cheese at the table

name: Roasted Red and Yellow Pepper Sauce with Garlic and Basil
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 3 meaty bell peppers, some red, some yellow
⅔ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese

name: Zucchini Sauce with Basil and Beaten Egg Yolk
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1 teaspoon all-purpose flour, dissolved in ⅓ cup milk
1 egg yolk, beaten lightly with a fork (see warning about salmonella poisoning)
½ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese
¼ cup freshly grated romano cheese
⅔ cup fresh basil leaves torn by hand into several pieces OR equal amount of chopped parsley
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: You can fry the zucchini several hours in advance of cooking the pasta and completing the sauce.

name: Fried Zucchini Sauce with Garlic and Basil
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ½ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese, plus additional cheese at the table
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The sauce may be prepared several hours in advance up to this point, but do not refrigerate the zucchini.

name: Smothered Onions Sauce
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: Either 2 tablespoons lard OR 2 tablespoons butter with 2 tablespoons extra virgin olive oil
1½ pounds onions, sliced very thin, about 6 cups
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
⅓ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: You can cook the sauce entirely in advance up to the point where you add the parsley. When you are nearly ready to toss it with the pasta, reheat the sauce over medium heat and add the parsley just before draining the pasta.

name: Butter and Rosemary Sauce
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 6 tablespoons butter, cut into small pieces
⅓ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese, plus additional cheese at the table

name: “Aio e Oio”—Roman Garlic and Oil Sauce
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ⅓ cup extra virgin olive oil
Chopped hot red chili pepper, to taste

name: “Aio e Oio” Raw Version
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ⅓ cup extra virgin olive oil
Chopped hot red chili pepper, to taste
authorNotes: Recommended pasta For both raw versions of aio e oio, thin spaghetti, spaghettini, is the pasta of choice.

name: Cauliflower Sauce with Garlic, Oil, and Chili Pepper
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ½ cup extra virgin olive oil
2 large garlic cloves, peeled and chopped
6 flat anchovy fillets (preferably the ones prepared at home), chopped very fine
Chopped hot red chili pepper, to taste
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: You can prepare the sauce several hours in advance up to this point. Do not refrigerate it. Reheat it gently when the pasta is nearly ready to be drained and tossed.

name: Broccoli and Anchovy Sauce
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: A large bunch fresh broccoli, about 1 pound
⅓ cup extra virgin olive oil
6 flat anchovy fillets (preferably the ones prepared at home), chopped very fine
Chopped hot red chili pepper, to taste
¼ cup freshly grated romano cheese
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The sauce may be prepared a few hours in advance up to this point, but do not refrigerate the cooked broccoli.

name: Tomato and Anchovy Sauce
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ⅓ cup extra virgin olive oil
4 flat anchovy fillets (preferably the ones prepared at home), chopped coarse
1½ cups canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, cut up, with their juice
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The sauce may be prepared several hours in advance and gently reheated when the pasta is nearly ready to be drained and tossed. Do not refrigerate.; Note: The pecorino cheese known as fiore sardo, which is used in the Riviera for making pesto, is much less harsh than romano. But up to now at least, romano is the one that is available. In the recipes given here, the proportion of romano to parmigiano-reggiano is less than what you will want to use if you can get fiore sardo.

name: Pesto by the Food Processor Method
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 cups tightly packed fresh basil leaves
½ cup extra virgin olive oil
2 garlic cloves, chopped fine before putting in the processor
½ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese
3 tablespoons butter, softened to room temperature
authorNotes: Freezing pesto Make the sauce by the food processor method through to the end of Step 2, and freeze it without cheese and butter in it. Add the cheese and butter when it is thawed, just before using.

name: Pesto by the Mortar Method
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: A large marble mortar with a hardwood pestle
2 cups tightly packed fresh basil leaves
½ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese
½ cup extra virgin olive oil
3 tablespoons butter, softened to room temperature
authorNotes: Recommended pasta Spaghetti is perfect with pesto and so are the Potato Gnocchi. In Genoa, a homemade noodle locally called trenette is the classic pasta for pesto. It is virtually identical to fettuccine, and if you’d like to serve pesto on fresh pasta, follow the instructions for making fettuccine. Even more appealing, if less orthodox than fettuccine, would be another kind of homemade noodle, tonnarelli.

name: Pasta and Pesto with Potatoes and Green Beans
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: The pesto from the preceding recipe

name: Black Truffle Sauce
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 3 tablespoons extra virgin olive oil, plus a little more oil for the pasta
1 flat anchovy fillet (preferably the kind prepared at home), chopped very fine

name: Tuna Sauce with Tomatoes and Garlic
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ½ teaspoon garlic chopped very fine
1½ cups canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, cut up, with their juice
12 ounces imported Italian tuna packed in olive oil, see Tuna
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The sauce can be prepared up to this point several hours or even a day or two in advance. When ready to use, reheat gently.

name: Clam Sauce with Tomatoes
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 3 tablespoons extra virgin olive oil, plus a little more for the pasta
2 cups canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, cut up, with their juice, OR fresh, ripe tomatoes, peeled and chopped
1 flat anchovy fillet (preferably the kind prepared at home), chopped very fine
Chopped hot red chili pepper, to taste
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The sauce may be prepared several hours in advance up to this point. Reheat gently when preparing to toss it with pasta.

name: White Clam Sauce
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 large garlic cloves, peeled and sliced paper thin
Chopped fresh hot chili pepper, 2 teaspoons, or to taste
1 fresh, ripe, firm plum tomato, cut into ½-inch dice with its skin on, but drained of juice and all seeds removed
6 fresh basil leaves, torn into 2 or 3 pieces

name: Sardinian Bottarga Sauce
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1 ounce mullet bottarga, sliced, then chopped, following directions given below, to produce ¼ loosely packed cup
⅔ cup scallions, both leaves and bulbs cut into very thin rounds, OR chopped onion
Butter, 1½ tablespoons for cooking onion, plus 1 tablespoon to toss the pasta
¼ teaspoon lemon peel grated without digging into the white pith
OPTIONAL: a tiny amount of chopped hot red chili pepper, to taste
authorNotes: Note: Because fine mullet bottarga is expensive, I have scaled the recipe to produce enough for two. I don’t think of bottarga as a condiment for a crowd, but you can easily double or triple the recipe to serve four or six.; Note: The chili pepper is, to my mind, unnecessary and competes with the flavor of bottarga. Many people do enjoy it, however, so it is up to you to decide.

name: Scallop Sauce with Olive Oil, Garlic, and Hot Pepper
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1 pound fresh bay OR deep sea scallops
½ cup extra virgin olive oil
Chopped hot red chili pepper, to taste
½ cup dry, unflavored bread crumbs, lightly toasted in the oven or in a skillet

name: Fish Sauce
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1½ to 2 pounds assorted fresh fish heads, from such fish as sea bass, red snapper, or porgie
⅔ cup extra virgin olive oil
1½ cups canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, cut up, with their juice
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: Everything can be prepared several hours in advance up to this point. Pour a little bit of the sauce from the pan over the meat from the fish heads that had been set aside and cover the bowl or saucer with plastic wrap.

name: Sicilian Sardine Sauce
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1 pound fresh sardines OR 8 ounces, net weight, drained choice canned sardines packed in olive oil
2 cups finocchio leaf tops (see note below) OR 1¾ cups fresh wild fennel
½ cup extra virgin olive oil
4 flat anchovy fillets (preferably the ones prepared at home), chopped fine
1½ tablespoons tomato paste, dissolved in 1 cup lukewarm water together with a large pinch of powdered saffron OR ½ teaspoon crumbled saffron threads
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
½ cup dry, unflavored bread crumbs, lightly toasted in the oven or in a skillet
authorNotes: Recommended pasta We should do as Palermo does, and choose the thick, hollow spaghetti that in Sicily is called u pirciatu. It is the same shape as the one identified on boxes as bucatini or perciatelli.; Ahead-of-time note: The sauce can be prepared several hours in advance up to this point, but if you are using canned sardines, put them in only when reheating the sauce to toss it with the pasta.

name: Pink Shrimp Sauce with Cream
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ½ pound medium shrimp, unshelled weight
⅓ cup extra virgin olive oil
2 garlic cloves, peeled and chopped fine
