1½ tablespoons tomato paste dissolved in ½ cup dry white wine
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
Homemade tortellini OR 1 pound other pasta

name: Butter and Parmesan Cheese Sauce
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)

name: Butter and Sage Sauce
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 6 to 8 whole sage leaves, preferably fresh
Freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese for the table

name: Cream and Butter Sauce
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1¼ pounds homemade fettuccine, OR tortellini, OR green tortellini
⅔ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese, plus additional cheese at the table
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill

name: Gorgonzola Sauce
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ¼ pound gorgonzola (see prefatory remarks above), kept at room temperature for 6 hours
⅓ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese, plus additional cheese at the table

name: Mushroom, Ham, and Cream Sauce
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ¾ pound fresh mushrooms, either white button OR cremini
2 tablespoons shallot OR onion chopped fine
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
6 ounces boiled unsmoked ham, cut into very narrow julienne strips
½ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese, plus additional cheese at the table

name: Red and Yellow Bell Pepper Sauce with Sausages
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 4 sweet sausages without fennel seeds, chili pepper, or other strong seasonings, cut into ½-inch pieces, about 1½ cups
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
1 cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, drained and cut up
The fresh pappardelle suggested below OR 1½ pounds boxed dry pasta
⅔ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese, plus additional cheese at the table
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The sauce may be prepared up to this point a few hours before serving. Do not refrigerate it. Reheat gently just before tossing with pasta.

name: Embogoné—Cranberry Beans, Sage, and Rosemary Sauce
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 3 pounds fresh cranberry beans, unshelled weight, OR 1½ cups dried cranberry OR red kidney beans, soaked and cooked
Extra virgin olive oil, 1 tablespoon for the sauce, 2 tablespoons for tossing the pasta
¼ pound pancetta chopped very fine to a pulp
Chopped sage leaves, 1 teaspoon if fresh, ½ teaspoon if dried
Chopped rosemary, 1 teaspoon if fresh, ½ teaspoon if dried
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
The fresh pappardelle suggested below OR 1 pound boxed dry pasta
½ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese, plus additional cheese at the table
authorNotes: Note: If you are not acquainted with cranberry beans, please see the explanation that accompanies Pasta and Bean Soup.

name: Asparagus Sauce with Ham and Cream
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ⅔ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese, plus additional cheese at the table

name: Sausages and Cream Sauce
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ½ pound sweet sausage containing no fennel seed, chili pepper, or other strong seasonings
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
Freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese at the table

name: Prosciutto and Cream Sauce
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ¼ pound sliced prosciutto OR country ham
¼ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese, plus additional cheese at the table

name: Carbonara Sauce
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ½ pound pancetta, cut as a single ½-inch-thick slice, OR its equivalent in good slab bacon
2 large eggs (see warning about salmonella poisoning)
¼ cup freshly grated romano cheese
½ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill

name: Bolognese Meat Sauce
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 3 tablespoons butter plus 1 tablespoon for tossing the pasta
¾ pound ground beef chuck (see prefatory note above)
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
1½ cups canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, cut up, with their juice
Freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese at the table
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: If you cannot watch the sauce for a 3- to 4-hour stretch, you can turn off the heat whenever you need to leave, and resume cooking later on, as long as you complete the sauce within the same day. Once done, you can refrigerate the sauce in a tightly sealed container for 3 days, or you can freeze it. Before tossing with pasta, reheat it, letting it simmer for 15 minutes and stirring it once or twice.

name: Chicken Liver Sauce
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ¼ teaspoon garlic chopped very fine
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
1 teaspoon tomato paste, dissolved in ¼ cup dry white vermouth
Freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese at the table

name: Tortellini with Meat and Cheese Filling
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ¼ pound pork, preferably from the neck OR Boston butt
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
Homemade yellow pasta dough, made as directed by the machine method, OR by the hand-rolled method, using 4 large eggs, approximately 2 cups unbleached flour, and 1 tablespoon milk

name: Green Tortellini with Meat and Ricotta Stuffing
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ¼ pound pork, preferably from the neck OR Boston butt
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
⅓ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese

Homemade green pasta dough, made by the machine method, OR by the hand-rolled method, using 2 large eggs, ⅓ package frozen leaf spinach OR 6 ounces fresh spinach, salt, approximately 1½ cups unbleached flour, and 1 tablespoon milk

name: Tortellini with Fish Stuffing
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: A 1-pound piece of fish, in a single slice if possible, preferably sea bass, OR other fish with comparable delicate flavor and juicy flesh
⅛ teaspoon dried marjoram OR a few fresh leaves
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
Homemade yellow pasta dough, made by the machine method, OR by the hand-rolled method, using 3 large eggs, approximately 1⅔ cups unbleached flour, and 1 tablespoon milk

name: Tortelli Stuffed with Parsley and Ricotta
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: Homemade yellow pasta dough, made by the machine method, OR by the hand-rolled method, using 3 large eggs, 1⅔ cups unbleached flour, and 1 tablespoon milk

name: Tortelloni Stuffed with Swiss Chard, Prosciutto, and Ricotta
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 pounds Swiss chard, if the stalks are very thin, OR 2½ pounds, if the stalks are broad, OR 2 pounds fresh spinach
3½ tablespoons chopped prosciutto OR pancetta OR unsmoked boiled ham
⅔ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese
Homemade yellow pasta dough, made by the machine method, OR by the hand-rolled method, using 3 large eggs, approximately 1⅔ cups unbleached flour, and 1 tablespoon milk

name: Cappellacci—Ravioli Filled with Sweet Potatoes
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1¾ pounds orange-fleshed sweet potatoes, see prefatory remarks above
A pair of imported Italian amaretti cookies
Homemade yellow pasta dough, made by the machine method, OR by the hand-rolled method, using 3 large eggs, approximately 1⅔ cups unbleached flour, and 1 tablespoon milk

name: Baked Rigatoni with Bolognese Meat Sauce
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: A medium-thick Béchamel Sauce, using 2 cups milk, 4 tablespoons (½ stick) butter, 3 tablespoons flour, and ¼ teaspoon salt
Butter for smearing and dotting the dish

name: Baked Green Lasagne with Meat Sauce, Bolognese Style
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: Bolognese Meat Sauce, the full amount
Béchamel Sauce, using 3 cups milk, 6 tablespoons butter, 4½ tablespoons flour, and ¼ teaspoon salt

Homemade green pasta dough, made by the machine method, OR by the hand-rolled method, using 2 large eggs, ⅓ package frozen leaf spinach OR 6 ounces fresh spinach, salt, and approximately 1½ cups unbleached flour
2 tablespoons butter plus more for greasing a 9- by 12-inch bake-and-serve lasagne pan, no less than
2½ inches high ⅔ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese
authorNotes: Explanatory note The washing, wringing, and drying of pasta for lasagne is something of a nuisance, but it is necessary. You first dip the partly cooked pasta into cold water to stop the cooking instantly. This is important because if lasagne pasta is not kept very firm at this stage it will become horribly mushy later when it is baked. And you must afterward rinse off the moist starch on its surface, or the dough will become glued to the towel on which it is laid out to dry, and tear when you are ready to use it.; Ahead-of-time note: The lasagne may be completed up to 2 days in advance up to this point. Refrigerate under tightly sealing plastic wrap.

name: Lasagne with Mushrooms and Ham
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1½ pounds fresh, firm, white button mushrooms
3 tablespoons butter plus more butter for greasing and dotting a 9- by 12-inch bake-and-serve lasagne pan, no less than 2½ inches high
⅓ cup onion chopped very fine
Two small packets OR 2 ounces dried porcini mushrooms, reconstituted
Filtered water from the mushroom soak
⅓ cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, drained and chopped
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
Homemade yellow pasta dough, made by the machine method, OR by the hand-rolled method, using 3 large eggs and approximately 1⅔ cups unbleached flour
Béchamel Sauce, using 2 cups milk, 4 tablespoons butter, 3 tablespoons flour, and ¼ teaspoon salt
⅔ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese, plus additional cheese at the table
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The lasagne may be completed up to 2 days in advance up to this point. Refrigerate under tightly sealing plastic wrap.

name: Lasagne with Artichokes
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 3 tablespoons butter plus more butter for greasing and dotting a 9- by 12-inch bake-and-serve lasagne pan, no less than 2½ inches high
Béchamel Sauce, using 2 cups milk, 4 tablespoons (½ stick) butter, 3 tablespoons flour, and ¼ teaspoon salt
Homemade yellow pasta dough, made by the machine method, OR by the hand-rolled method, using 3 large eggs and approximately 1⅔ cups unbleached flour
⅔ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: You can prepare the artichokes up to this point several hours in advance.; Ahead-of-time note: The lasagne may be completed up to a day in advance up to this point. Refrigerate under tightly sealing plastic wrap.

name: Lasagne with Ricotta Pesto
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: Homemade yellow pasta dough, made by the machine method, OR by the hand-rolled method, using 3 large eggs and approximately 1⅔ cups unbleached flour
Freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese at the table
authorNotes: Note: If the pasta is very fresh, you can cook it in two batches as suggested above, because it will cook so quickly that the first batch will not have had time to get cold by the time the second batch is done. If it is on the dry side, it will take longer to cook, so you must do the two batches simultaneously in two separate pots.

name: Cannelloni with Meat Stuffing
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: Béchamel Sauce, using 2 cups milk, 4 tablespoons (½ stick) butter, 3 tablespoons flour, and ¼ teaspoon salt
½ cup chopped unsmoked boiled ham
½ cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, chopped, with their juice
Homemade yellow pasta dough, made by the machine method, OR by the hand-rolled method, using 3 large eggs and approximately 1⅔ cups unbleached flour
⅓ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese
3 tablespoons butter, plus more for smearing
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The cannelloni may be completed up to two days in advance up to this point. Refrigerate under tightly sealing plastic wrap.

name: Sliced Pasta Roll with Spinach and Ham Filling
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: Tomato Sauce with Onion and Butter, ½ the recipe's quantity
2 pounds fresh spinach OR 2 ten-ounce packages frozen leaf spinach, thawed
Homemade yellow pasta dough, made by the machine method, OR by the hand-rolled method, using 3 large eggs and approximately 1⅔ cups unbleached flour
Béchamel Sauce, using 1 cup milk, 2 tablespoons butter, 1½ tablespoons flour, and ⅛ teaspoon salt
⅓ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese
About 2 tablespoons butter for dotting the baking dish
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The dish can be assembled completely up to this point several hours in advance, but not overnight. Do not refrigerate because cooked spinach acquires a sour, metallic taste in the refrigerator.

name: Pasta Wrappers Filled with Spinach Fettuccine, Porcini Mushrooms, and Ham
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: Homemade green pasta dough, made by the machine method, OR by the hand-rolled method, using 2 large eggs, ⅓ package frozen leaf spinach OR 6 ounces fresh spinach, salt, and approximately 1½ cups unbleached flour
Two small packets OR 2 ounces dried porcini mushrooms, reconstituted
Filtered water from the mushroom soak
⅔ cup unsmoked boiled ham, cut into ¼-inch strips
⅓ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese
OPTIONAL: ½ ounce (or more if affordable) fresh OR canned white truffle
Homemade yellow pasta dough made by the machine method, OR by the hand-rolled method, using 3 large eggs and approximately 1⅔ cups unbleached flour
Béchamel Sauce, using 3 cups milk, 6 tablespoons butter, 4½ tablespoons flour, and ¼ teaspoon salt
6 gratin pans, preferably earthenware, about 4½ inches in diameter
Butter for greasing the pans Wooden toothpicks
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: You can prepare the wrappers several hours in advance up to this point. They can be done in the morning for the evening, but not overnight, and they are not to be refrigerated.

name: Pizzoccheri — Hazan
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: Homemade pasta dough, made by the machine method, OR by the hand-rolled method, using 3 large eggs and approximately 1¼ cups fine-grained buckwheat flour, ½ cup plus 1 tablespoon unbleached flour, 1 tablespoon milk, 1 tablespoon water, and ½ teaspoon salt
3 to 3½ cups Swiss chard stalks (leafy tops completely removed), cut into pieces 2 to 3 inches long and about ½ inch wide
1 cup potatoes, preferably new, peeled and sliced ¼ inch thick
4 large garlic cloves, lightly mashed with a knife handle and peeled
A 12- to 14-inch oven-to-table baking dish and butter to smear it
¼ pound imported Italian fontina cheese, sliced into thin slivers
⅔ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The pasta can be prepared up to this point days or even weeks ahead of time. See instructions on drying pasta for storage. Bear in mind when cooking it later that dried pasta takes longer than the freshly made.; Cooking the Pasta

name: Orecchiette
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1 cup semolina, the yellow flour from hard wheat, ground very fine
authorNotes: Recommended sauce The most suitable is the Broccoli and Anchovy Sauce. Other good choices are Tomato and Anchovy Sauce, and Cauliflower Sauce with Garlic, Oil, and Chili Pepper.

name: Risotto with Parmesan Cheese
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)

name: Risotto with Saffron, Milanese Style
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)

name: Risotto with Porcini Mushrooms

name: Risotto with Asparagus

name: Risotto with Celery
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)

name: Risotto with Zucchini
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)

authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The recipe may be cooked several hours or a day or two in advance up to this point. If resuming the same day, do not refrigerate the zucchini. When refrigerating, store tightly covered with plastic wrap.

name: Risotto with Spring Vegetables, Tomato, and Basil
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)

⅓ cup carrot diced very fine
⅓ cup celery diced very fine
2 cups Arborio or other imported Italian risotto rice

1 ripe, firm, fresh tomato, skinned raw with a peeler, seeded, and diced fine
⅓ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese
6 or more fresh basil leaves, washed and shredded by hand

name: Paniscia—Risotto with Vegetables and Red Wine
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ¼ cup tender, mild salami chopped fine (see prefatory remarks above)
2 cups Arborio or other imported Italian risotto rice
2 cups dry red wine (see prefatory remarks above)
2½ cups Novara’s Bean and Vegetable Soup.
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
Freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese at the table

name: Risotto with Clams
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 3 dozen littleneck clams, the smallest you can find
2 cups Arborio or other imported Italian risotto rice
Chopped hot red chili pepper, to taste
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The steps above may be completed 2 or 3 hours in advance. When doing so, spoon some of the filtered juice over the clam meat to keep it moist.

name: Risotto with Beef, Rosemary, Sage, and Barolo Wine, Alba Style
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 5 cups Basic Homemade Meat Broth, OR 1 cup canned beef broth diluted with 4 cups water
Chopped rosemary leaves, 1½ teaspoons if fresh, ¾ teaspoon if dried
Chopped sage leaves, 2 teaspoons if fresh, 1 teaspoon if dried
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
2 cups Arborio or other imported Italian risotto rice
⅓ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese, plus additional cheese at the table
authorNotes: Note: Barolo, perhaps Italy’s greatest red wine, and certainly its most profound in flavor, can satisfactorily be replaced in this preparation by its closest relative, Barbaresco. For other substitutions, look for wines derived from the same distinctive nebbiolo grape, such as Gattinara, Spanna, Carema, or Sfursat. You could try still other red wines, and although you might well make an excellent risotto with them, it would not be this risotto.

name: Risotto with Bolognese Meat Sauce
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)

name: Molded Parmesan Risotto with Chicken Liver Sauce
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)

Chicken Liver Sauce, reducing the butter to just 1 tablespoon

name: Boiled Rice with Parmesan, Mozzarella, and Basil
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)

name: Potato Gnocchi
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
authorNotes: Recommended sauce Gnocchi take well to many sauces, but three particularly happy choices are Tomato Sauce with Onion and Butter; Pesto; and Gorgonzola Sauce.; Note: If the potatoes you work with produce gnocchi dough that dissolves or collapses in cooking, you must add 1 whole egg to the puréed potatoes.

name: Spinach and Ricotta Gnocchi
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1 pound fresh spinach OR 1 ten-ounce package frozen leaf spinach, thawed
2 tablespoons chopped prosciutto OR for milder flavor, boiled unsmoked ham
1 cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese, plus additional cheese at the table
authorNotes: Recommended sauce Tomato Sauce with Heavy Cream may be the most appealing, both in flavor and appearance; another excellent combination is with Butter and Sage Sauce. Spinach and Ricotta Gnocchi are delicious as soup dumplings, served in Basic Homemade Meat Broth.

name: Gratinéed Spinach and Ricotta Gnocchi
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 3 tablespoons butter plus more butter for greasing the pan
½ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese, plus additional cheese at the table

name: Baked Semolina Gnocchi
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1 cup semolina, coarsely ground yellow hard-wheat flour
3 egg yolks, lightly beaten in a saucer
An oven-to-table baking dish and butter to smear it
¼ pound prosciutto OR bacon OR boiled ham, cut into small strips
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: Semolina gnocchi can be completely prepared and assembled in their baking dish up to 2 days in advance. Cover tightly with plastic wrap before refrigerating. Keep the trimmings, too, in the refrigerator, in a tightly sealed container.

name: Crespelle
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: You can make crespelle several hours in advance, or even days. If you are doing them days ahead of time, interleave them with wax paper before refrigerating. If you are keeping them longer than 3 days, freeze them.

name: Baked Crespelle with Bolognese Meat Sauce
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)

Béchamel Sauce, prepared as directed, using 1 cup milk, 2 tablespoons butter, 1½ tablespoons flour, and ¼ teaspoon salt

Flameproof ware for baking and serving
Butter for smearing and dotting the baking dish
⅓ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese

name: Baked Crespelle with Spinach, Prosciutto, and Parmesan Filling
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: Crespelle (thin pancakes), produced with this recipe
1 pound fresh spinach OR 1 ten-ounce package frozen leaf spinach, thawed
Béchamel Sauce, prepared as directed, using 2 cups milk, 4 tablespoons (½ stick) butter, 3 tablespoons flour, and ¼ teaspoon salt
1 tablespoon butter plus more for greasing and dotting the baking dish
Flameproof ware for baking and serving

name: Layered Crespelle with Tomato, Prosciutto, and Cheese
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 8 to 9 crespelle (thin pancakes) made with this recipe, using half the recipe, or crêpes made by any other comparable method
A tomato sauce made using: 3 tablespoons extra virgin olive oil
⅔ cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, cut up, with their juice
½ cup prosciutto shredded very fine

½ cup mozzarella, preferably imported Italian buffalo-milk mozzarella, diced very, very fine

name: Making Polenta
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1⅔ cups coarse-grained imported Italian yellow cornmeal
An 8- to 10-cup bowl, preferably steel or copper
authorNotes: Note: on consistency As it begins to cool, polenta should be thick, and when moved, firm enough to quiver. From an Italian point of view, it is least appealing when it is as thin and runny as breakfast oatmeal.; Note: If you are going to let it become completely cold and firm and later slice it, do not put the hot polenta in a bowl, but spread it flat on a board to a thickness of about 3 inches.; Ahead-of-time note: If you are planning to slice polenta and grill it, bake it, or fry it, you must make it several hours in advance. It will keep for several days in the refrigerator. If you are refrigerating it for a few days, keep it whole, in one piece, and wrap it tightly with foil or plastic wrap.; Cleaning the pot After emptying the polenta from the pot, fill it with cold water and set it aside to soak overnight. In the morning most of the cornmeal film attached to the pot lifts off easily. If you are using an Italian-made unlined copper polenta pot, after emptying it in the morning and scraping away all the loosened residues, clean it with ¼ cup of vinegar and some salt. Rinse with plain water, without using any detergent, and wipe dry. Whenever you use an unlined copper pot, go over it again with vinegar and salt, and rinse thoroughly with plain water before each use.

name: Instant Polenta
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 cups imported Italian instant polenta flour

name: Baked Polenta with Bolognese Meat Sauce
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)

Polenta, produced with this this recipe, allowed to become cold and firm for slicing
A lasagne pan and butter for smearing it
2 cups Bolognese Meat Sauce, prepared with this recipe

name: Frittata with Onions
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)

authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: You can cook the onions up to this point several hours or even a day or two in advance. You do not need to refrigerate them if you are going to use them later the same day. Let them come to room temperature before mixing them with the eggs.

name: Frittata with Zucchini and Basil

name: Frittata with Tomato, Onion, and Basil

authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: You can cook the onion and tomatoes up to this point several hours or even a day or two in advance. You do not need to refrigerate them if you are going to use them later the same day. If refrigerated, bring them to room temperature before proceeding with the frittata.

name: Frittata with Artichokes
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
¼ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese

sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill

sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)

sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)

name: Grilled Fish, Romagna Style

sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)

sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)

½ package active dry yeast (about 1¼ teaspoons), dissolved in 1 cup lukewarm water

name: Fried Tidbits of Swordfish or Other Fish

Freshly squeezed juice of ½ lemon

name: Sautéed Snapper or Other Whole Fish with Mushrooms

A 2- to 2½-pound whole fish, such as red snapper or sea bass, gutted and scaled, but with head and tail on
1 flat anchovy fillet, chopped fine, to a pulp

2 tablespoons capers, soaked and rinsed as described if packed in salt, drained if in vinegar

⅔ cup flour, spread on a plate

name: Shrimp with Tomatoes and Chili Pepper
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ¼ cup extra virgin olive oil
Chopped hot red chili pepper, to taste
1⅔ cups canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, cut up, with their juice (see fresh tomato note below)
Grilled or oven-browned slices of crusty bread
authorNotes: Note: If in season and available, use the same amount of fresh, ripe plum tomatoes, skinned with a peeler and diced very fine.; Ahead-of-time note: The recipe may be completed several hours or even a day in advance up to this point. Refrigerate the sauce if you are not using it the same day. Bring to a simmer when you are ready to add the shrimp.; Note: It’s possible that the shrimp may shed liquid that will make the sauce thin and runny. Should this happen, uncover the pan, transfer the shrimp to a warm, deep serving platter using a slotted spoon or spatula, turn the heat under the pan up to high, and boil down the sauce until it regains its original density. Pour over the shrimp and serve at once.

name: Baked Sea Bass or Other Whole Fish Stuffed with Shellfish
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)

½ cup extra virgin olive oil
