⅓ cup fine, dry, unflavored bread crumbs
A 4- to 5-pound whole sea bass, red snapper, or small salmon, or similar fish, boned as described above
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The steps above may be completed 2 or 3 hours in advance.

name: Baked Bluefish Fillets with Potatoes, Garlic, and Olive Oil, Genoese Style
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: A bake-and-serve dish, approximately 16 by 10 inches, preferably enameled cast-iron ware
½ cup extra virgin olive oil
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
2 bluefish fillets with the skin on, approximately 1 pound each, OR the equivalent in other thick, firm fish fillets

name: Bass or Other Whole Fish Baked with Artichokes
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ¼ cup extra virgin olive oil
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
A 2- to 2½-pound sea bass OR red snapper OR similar fine-fleshed fish, scaled and gutted, but with head and tail on
An oval or rectangular bake-and-serve dish
A small sprig of fresh rosemary OR 1 teaspoon dried leaves, chopped very fine
authorNotes: Note: If you have difficulty finding a whole small fish as indicated in the list of ingredients, and do not want to increase the recipe by using a much bigger fish, buy a 2-pound fillet with the skin on cut from grouper or other large fish. First bake the artichoke slices alone, along with the oil and lemon juice. After 20 minutes, put the fish skin side down in the baking dish, and spoon over it some of the artichokes to cover. Cook for 15 minutes, basting it midway with the juices in the dish.

name: Baked Fillet of Sole with Tomato, Oregano, and Hot Pepper
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ⅔ cup onion sliced very thin
½ teaspoon garlic chopped very fine
1 cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, cut up, with their juice
2 tablespoons capers, soaked and rinsed as described if packed in salt, drained if in vinegar
2 teaspoons fresh oregano OR 1 teaspoon dried
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill, OR chopped hot red chili pepper, to taste
2 pounds gray sole fillets OR other flatfish fillets

name: My Father’s Fish Soup
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 3 to 4 pounds assorted fish (see note above), scaled and gutted
½ pound or more unshelled shrimp
¾ pound cleaned squid, sliced into rings
⅓ cup extra virgin olive oil
1 cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, cut up, with their juice
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill, OR chopped hot red chili pepper, to taste
Grilled or toasted slices of crusty bread
authorNotes: Note: You need at least 3 or 4 heads for this recipe, so if you won’t be buying that many whole fish, ask your dealer for extra heads. Make sure you have a food mill with interchangeable disks; the disk with the largest holes is indispensable for pureeing the heads.

name: Halibut over Squid Sauce
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 pounds whole squid OR 1½ pounds cleaned squid, sliced into narrow rings
½ cup extra virgin olive oil
1½ cups fresh, ripe, firm tomatoes skinned raw with a peeler and chopped OR canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, cut up with their juice
Chopped hot red chili pepper, to taste
3 pounds halibut, cut into steaks 1 inch thick, OR other fish steaks (see recommendations above)
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: You can complete the recipe up to this point several hours in advance. Reheat the squid gently, but completely before proceeding with the next step.

name: A Single Fish Cooked Fish-Soup Style
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: A 2½- to 3-pound whole fish, scaled and gutted, but with head and tail on, OR 1½ to 2 pounds fish steaks (see recommended varieties above)
¼ cup extra virgin olive oil
¾ cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, chopped, with their juice
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill, OR chopped hot red chili pepper, to taste

name: All–Shellfish and Mollusks Soup
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 pounds whole squid OR 1½ pounds cleaned squid, sliced into rings
2 dozen live littleneck clams in the shell
1 dozen live mussels in the shell
½ cup extra virgin olive oil
1½ cups canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, cut up, with their juice
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill, OR chopped hot red chili pepper, to taste
Thick, grilled or oven-toasted slices of crusty bread

name: Fried Calamari
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2½ pounds whole squid OR 2 pounds cleaned squid, sliced into rings
1 cup flour, spread on a plate
authorNotes: Note: If the squid rings are rather small, they are fully cooked the moment they become colored a light gold. If they are medium to large in size, they will take just a few seconds longer.

name: Squid with Tomatoes and Peas, Tuscan Style
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2½ pounds small to medium whole squid OR 2 pounds cleaned squid, sliced into rings
¾ cup fresh, ripe tomatoes, peeled and coarsely chopped, OR canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, coarsely chopped, with their juice
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
2 pounds unshelled fresh peas OR 1 ten-ounce package frozen peas, thawed
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: You can stop the cooking at the end of Step 3 and resume it several hours or even a day later. Bring to a simmer before adding the peas. You may even complete the dish a day or two in advance, reheating it very gently before serving. It tastes sweetest, however, when prepared and eaten the same day.

name: Squid and Potatoes, Genoa Style
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 3 pounds small to medium whole squid OR 2½ pounds cleaned squid, sliced into rings
1 cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, cut up, with their juice
½ teaspoon chopped fresh marjoram or oregano OR ¼ teaspoon dried
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
authorNotes: Note: Marjoram is what a Genoese cook would use and it should be your first choice if you prefer a lighter fragrance. With oregano, the dish assumes an emphatically accented style that can be rather enjoyable.; Ahead-of-time note: The dish may be prepared entirely in advance, and kept for a day or two. Reheat gently before serving. If possible, eat it the same day it is cooked, when its flavor has not been impaired by refrigeration.

name: Stuffed Whole Squid Braised with Tomatoes and White Wine
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 6 whole squid with sacs measuring 4½ to 5 inches in length, not including the tentacles
Approximately 1 tablespoon extra virgin olive oil
¼ cup fine, dry unflavored bread crumbs
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
Extra virgin olive oil for cooking
½ cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, chopped, with their juice
authorNotes: Darning needle and cotton thread OR strong, round toothpicks; Ahead-of-time note: If necessary, the dish can be finished 3 or 4 days in advance. Reheat it as follows: Preheat oven to 300°. Transfer the squid slices and their sauce to a bake-and-serve dish, add 2 or 3 tablespoons of water, and place on the middle rack of the preheated oven. Turn and baste the slices as they warm up, handling them gently to keep them from breaking apart. Serve when warmed all the way through.

name: Squid with Porcini Mushroom Stuffing
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: A small packet OR 1 ounce dried porcini mushrooms, reconstituted as described
The filtered water from the mushroom soak, see instructions
4 whole squid with sacs measuring 4½ to 5 inches in length, not including the tentacles
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
⅓ cup fine, dry unflavored bread crumbs
Extra virgin olive oil: 1 tablespoon for the stuffing plus 3 tablespoons for cooking
Darning needle and cotton thread OR strong, round toothpicks

name: Roast Chicken with Lemons
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: If you want to eat it while it is warm, plan to have it the moment it comes out of the oven. If there are leftovers, they will be very tasty cold, kept moist with some of the cooking juices and eaten not straight out of the refrigerator, but at room temperature.

name: Oven-Roasted Chicken with Garlic and Rosemary
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 sprigs of fresh rosemary OR 1 heaping teaspoon dried leaves
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill

name: Pan-Roasted Chicken with Rosemary, Garlic, and White Wine
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1 sprig of fresh rosemary broken in two OR ½ teaspoon dried rosemary leaves
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill

name: Chicken Fricassee, Cacciatora Style
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: A 3- to 4-pound chicken, cut into 6 to 8 pieces
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
⅓ cup onion sliced very thin
1 sweet yellow or red bell pepper, seeds and core removed and cut into thin julienne strips
1 carrot, peeled and cut into thin disks
½ stalk celery sliced thin crosswise
1 garlic clove, peeled and chopped very fine
⅔ cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, chopped coarse, with their juice
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The dish can be cooked through to the end up to a day in advance. Let the chicken cool completely in the pan juices before refrigerating. Reheat in a covered pan at a slow simmer, turning the chicken pieces until they are warmed all the way through.

name: Chicken Cacciatora, New Version
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: A 3- to 4-pound chicken, cut into 6 to 8 pieces
2 garlic cloves, peeled and sliced very thin
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
1½ cups fresh, very ripe, firm meaty tomatoes, skinned raw with a peeler and chopped, OR canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, cut up, with their juice
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The recommendations in the preceding cacciatora recipe are applicable here.

name: Chicken Fricassee with Porcini Mushrooms, White Wine, and Tomatoes
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
A small packet OR 1 ounce dried porcini mushrooms, reconstituted as described and cut up
The filtered water from the mushroom soak, see instructions
¼ cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, chopped coarse, with their juice

name: Chicken Fricassee with Red Cabbage
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ¼ cup extra virgin olive oil plus 1 tablespoon
2 garlic cloves, peeled and each cut into 4 pieces
4 cups red cabbage shredded fine, about 1 pound
A 3- to 4-pound chicken, cut into 8 pieces
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The dish can be prepared up to this point even 2 or 3 days in advance. Reheat completely in a covered pan before proceeding to the next step.

name: Fricasseed Chicken with Rosemary and Lemon Juice
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: A 3- to 4-pound chicken, cut into 8 pieces
1 sprig of fresh rosemary OR 1 teaspoon dried leaves
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
Lemon peel with none of the white pith, cut into 6 thin julienne strips

name: Fricasseed Chicken with Egg and Lemon, Marches Style
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: A 3- to 4-pound chicken, cut into 8 pieces
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
1 cup Basic Homemade Meat Broth, prepared as directed, OR 1 bouillon cube dissolved in 1 cup water
2 egg yolks (see warning about salmonella poisoning)
¼ cup freshly squeezed lemon juice

name: Grilled Chicken alla Diavola, Roman Style
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ⅓ cup freshly squeezed lemon juice
OPTIONAL: a charcoal or wood-burning grill

name: Rolled Fillets of Breast of Chicken with Pork and Rosemary Filling
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
2 teaspoons fresh rosemary leaves OR 1 teaspoon dried
2 whole chicken breasts, filleted as directed
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The rolls can be prepared up to this point several hours in advance.

name: Sautéed Fillets of Breast of Chicken with Lemon and Parsley, Siena Style
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 3 whole chicken breasts, filleted as directed
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
The freshly squeezed juice of 1 lemon

name: Pan-Roasted Whole Boned Chicken with Beef and Parmesan Stuffing
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ⅔ cup crumb, the soft, crustless part of bread, cut into 1-inch pieces
½ teaspoon garlic chopped very fine
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
⅔ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese
A 3- to 4-pound chicken, boned as directed
Trussing needle and string OR a darning needle and strong cotton thread

name: Pan-Roasted Squab Pigeons
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 4 fresh squab, about 1 pound each, plucked thoroughly clean (if the squab don’t come with the livers, add 4 fresh chicken livers)
Pancetta, cut into 4 thin strips, 1½ inches long and ½ inch wide
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill

name: Roast Duck
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: Rosemary leaves, chopped very fine, 2 teaspoons if fresh, 1 teaspoon if dried
Sage leaves, chopped or crumbled very fine, 1 tablespoon if fresh, 1½ teaspoons if dried
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
⅔ cup duck liver, or duck and chicken liver (see remarks above) chopped very fine

name: Rabbit with Rosemary and White Wine
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: A 3- to 3½-pound rabbit, cut into 8 piece
⅓ cup extra virgin olive oil
2 sprigs of fresh rosemary OR 1½ teaspoons dried leaves
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
1 bouillon cube and 2 tablespoons tomato paste, dissolved in ⅓ cup warm water
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: You can finish cooking the rabbit several hours or a day in advance. Reheat in a covered pan over low heat, adding 2 or 3 tablespoons of water. Turn the rabbit pieces from time to time until they are warmed all the way through.

name: Pan–Roasted Veal with Garlic,Rosemary, and White Wine
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 pounds boned veal roast (see note below)
A sprig of rosemary OR 1 teaspoon dried rosemary leaves
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
authorNotes: Note: A juicy, flavorful, and not expensive cut for this roast would be boned, rolled shoulder of veal.; Ahead-of-time note: The cooking can be completed up to this point several hours in advance. Reheat gently in a covered pan with 1 or 2 tablespoons of water if necessary.

name: Rolled–Up Breast of Veal with Pancetta
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: Breast of veal in a single piece, 4½ to 5 pounds with the bones, yielding approximately 1¾ pounds of meat when boned either by the butcher or as described above
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
¼ pound pancetta, sliced very, very thin
A sprig or two of rosemary OR 1 teaspoon dried rosemary leaves
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: Follow the recommendations for Pan-Roasted Veal with Garlic, Rosemary, and White Wine.

name: Pan-Roasted Breast of Veal
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 3 tablespoons extra virgin olive oil 3 whole garlic cloves, peeled
3½ pounds breast of veal with rib bones in
2 sprigs of fresh rosemary OR 1 teaspoon dried leaves
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill

name: Ossobuco—Braised Veal Shanks, Milanese Style
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 strips lemon peel with none of the white pith beneath it
8 1½-inch-thick slices of veal hind shank, each tied tightly around the middle
1 cup Basic Homemade Meat Broth, prepared as directed, OR ½ cup canned beef broth with
1½ cups canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, coarsely chopped, with their juice
½ teaspoon fresh thyme OR ¼ teaspoon dried
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
1 teaspoon grated lemon peel, taking care to avoid the white pith
¼ teaspoon garlic chopped very, very fine
authorNotes: Note: When you are buying a whole shank, ask the butcher to saw off both ends for you. You don’t want them in the ossobuco because they don’t have much meat, but they make a splendid addition to the assorted components of a homemade meat broth.; When the oil is quite hot—it should sizzle when the veal goes in—slip in the shanks and brown them deeply all over. Remove them from the skillet using a slotted spoon or spatula, and stand them side by side over the chopped vegetables in the pot.; Note: Do not flour the veal, or anything else that needs to be browned, in advance because the flour will become soggy and make it impossible to achieve a crisp surface.; Ahead-of-time note: Ossobucocan be completely cooked a day or two in advance. It should be reheated gently over the stove, adding 1 or 2 tablespoons of water, if needed. If you are using the gremolada, add it only when reheating the meat.

name: Ossobuco in Bianco—Tomato-Less Braised Veal Shanks
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ¼ cup extra virgin olive oil
8 1½-inch-thick slices of veal hind shank, each tied tightly around the middle
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
2 tablespoons lemon peel with none of the white pith beneath it, chopped very fine
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The light, fragrant flavor of this particular ossobuco does not withstand refrigeration well, so it is not advisable to prepare it very long in advance. It can certainly be made early on the day it is to be served; reheat it in the pan it was cooked in, covered, over low heat, for 10 or 15 minutes until the meat is warmed all the way through. If the juices in the pan become insufficient, replenish with 1 or 2 tablespoons water.

name: Stinco—Braised Whole Veal Shank, Trieste Style
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
6 flat anchovy fillets (preferably the ones prepared at home as described)
authorNotes: Note: The shanks must come from the hind leg. Have the joint at the broad end of the shank sawed off flat so that the bone can be brought to the table standing up, surrounded by the carved slices. Also have the butcher take off enough of the bone at the narrow end to expose the marrow, which, at table, can be picked out with a narrow implement and is most delectable.; Ahead-of-time note: The entire dish can be made several hours in advance. When doing so, instead of pouring the pot juices over the veal, put the sliced meat into the pot together with the bones. Reheat over gentle heat just before serving, turning the slices in the juice. Arrange on a warm platter as described above, and serve at once. Use the dish the same day you make it, because its flavor will deteriorate if kept overnight.

name: Veal Scaloppine with Marsala
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1 pound veal scaloppine, cut from the top round, and flattened as described
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill

name: Veal Scaloppine with Marsala and Cream
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1 pound veal scaloppine, cut from the top round, and flattened as described
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill

name: Veal Scaloppine with Lemon
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1 pound veal scaloppine, cut from the top round, and flattened as described
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
authorNotes: Note: One sometimes sees scaloppine with lemon topped with a sprinkling of fresh chopped parsley. It’s perfectly all right as long as you don’t make the sauce with parsley. The color of cooked parsley contrasts unappetizingly with that of the fresh. If you use one, omit the other.

name: Veal Scaloppine with Mozzarella
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ½ pound mozzarella, preferably buffalo-milk mozzarella
1 pound veal scaloppine, cut from the top round, and flattened as described
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill

name: Veal Scaloppine with Tomato, Oregano, and Capers
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1 pound veal scaloppine, cut from the top round, and flattened as described
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
½ cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, chopped, with their juice
1 teaspoon fresh oregano OR ½ teaspoon dried
2 tablespoons capers, soaked and rinsed as described if packed in salt, drained if in vinegar

name: Veal Scaloppine with Ham, Anchovies, Capers, and Grappa
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 4 flat anchovy fillets (preferably the ones prepared at home as described), chopped very fine
¼ pound boiled unsmoked ham, sliced ¼ inch thick and diced fine
1½ tablespoons capers, soaked and rinsed as described if packed in salt, drained if in vinegar, and chopped
1 pound veal scaloppine, cut from the top round, and flattened as described
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
authorNotes: Note: Grappa is a pungently fragrant distilled spirit made from pomace, a residue of winemaking. It is usually obtainable in stores stocking Italian wines, but it can be substituted with marc, a French spirit made like grappa. If neither is available, use calvados, French apple brandy, or a good grape brandy.

name: Veal Scaloppine in Parchment with Asparagus and Fontina Cheese
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 tablespoons butter plus butter for dotting the finished dish
1 pound veal scaloppine, cut from the top round, and flattened as described
Cooking parchment or heavy-duty aluminum foil
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill

name: Messicani—Stuffed Veal Rolls with Ham, Parmesan, Nutmeg, and White Wine
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ⅓ cup crumb, the soft, crustless part of bread, preferably from good Italian or French bread
2 ounces boiled unsmoked ham, chopped fine
2 ounces pork, ground or chopped fine
⅓ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
1 pound veal scaloppine, cut from the top round, and flattened as described
½ cup Basic Homemade Meat Broth, prepared as directed, OR ½ bouillon cube dissolved in ½ cup water

name: Veal Rolls with Pancetta and Parmesan
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1 pound veal scaloppine, cut from the top round, and flattened as described
¼ pound pancetta, sliced very, very thin
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
⅔ cup fresh, ripe tomatoes, peeled and chopped, OR canned Italian plum tomatoes, cut up, with their juice
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: These veal rolls don’t take that long to do and they taste best when served the moment they are made. If you must make them in advance, cook them through to the end up to several hours ahead of time, then reheat gently in their sauce.

name: Veal Rolls with Anchovy Fillets and Mozzarella
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 8 flat anchovy fillets (preferably the ones prepared at home as described)
⅓ cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, drained of juice
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
½ pound mozzarella, preferably imported buffalo-milk mozzarella
1½ pounds veal scaloppine, cut from the top round, and flattened as described

name: Veal Roll with Spinach and Prosciutto Stuffing
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ¼ pound prosciutto OR pancetta chopped very fine
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
A 1-pound slice of veal, preferably from the top round (see remarks above), pounded flat to a thickness of ⅜ inch
authorNotes: Uncover the pot, add the cream, turn the heat up, and use a wooden spoon to loosen cooking residues from the bottom and sides of the pot. Remove from heat as soon as the cream thickens a bit and becomes colored a light nut brown.; Ahead-of-time note: You can complete the recipe up to this point several hours in advance. Before proceeding, reheat gently, adding 1 tablespoon water if needed. Do not refrigerate at any time or the spinach will acquire a sour taste.

name: Sautéed Breaded Veal Chops, Milanese Style
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 6 veal chops, with either 6 or 3 ribs, depending on the size, the bones trimmed clean and the meat flattened (see explanatory remarks above)
1½ cups fine, dry, unflavored bread crumbs spread on a plate

name: Variation in the Sicilian Style, with Garlic and Rosemary
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: Rosemary leaves, chopped very fine, 1 tablespoon if fresh, 2 teaspoons if dried
4 garlic cloves, lightly mashed with a knife handle and peeled

name: Sautéed Veal Chops with Sage and White Wine
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 4 veal loin chops less than 1 inch thick
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
⅓ cup dry white wine 1 tablespoon butter

name: Sautéed Veal Chops with Garlic, Anchovies, and Parsley
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 flat anchovy fillets (preferably the ones prepared at home as described), chopped very, very fine
4 veal loin chops less than 1 inch thick
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill

name: Veal Stew with Sage, White Wine, and Cream
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1½ pounds boned veal shoulder or shank, cut into cubes of approximately 1½ inches
2 tablespoons chopped onion 18 dried sage leaves ⅔ cup dry white wine
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: Like most stews, this one can be prepared several days in advance and refrigerated until needed. Reheat it gently until the meat has been warmed through and through, either on the stove or in a preheated 325° oven. Add 2 tablespoons of water when reheating.

name: Veal Stew with Tomatoes and Peas
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1½ pounds boned veal shoulder or shank, cut into 1½-inch cubes
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
1 cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, chopped coarse, with their juice
2 pounds fresh peas in their pods (please see note), OR 1 ten-ounce package frozen small peas, thawed
authorNotes: Note: If you are using fresh peas, you will add to their sweetness and that of the stew by utilizing some of the pods. It is an optional procedure, however, and if you choose to, you can omit it.; Ahead-of time note Please follow these suggestions.

name: Veal Stew with Mushrooms
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1½ pounds boned veal shoulder or shank, cut into 1½-inch cubes
1 large garlic clove, peeled and chopped fine
½ cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, cut up, with their juice
1 or 2 sprigs of fresh rosemary OR 1 teaspoon dried leaves, chopped fine
4 or 5 fresh sage leaves OR 2 or 3 dried whole leaves
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
½ pound fresh, white button mushrooms
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The dish may be completed several hours in advance the same day you are going to eat it, and reheated gently just before serving.

name: Skewered Veal Cubes and Pork Sausage Pan-Roasted with Sage and White Wine
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1¼ pounds mild pork sausage made without strong spices or herbs
20 whole sage leaves, fresh if possible
1 pound boneless shank or shoulder of veal, cut into 1½-inch cubes
½ pound pancetta in a single slice, unrolled and cut into 1½-inch pieces

name: Vitello Tonnato—Cold Sliced Veal with Tuna Sauce
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 to 2½ pounds lean veal roast, preferably the top round, firmly trussed, OR turkey breast OR pork loin
Mayonnaise prepared as described, using 2 egg yolks, 1¼ cups extra virgin olive oil (see note below), 2 tablespoons freshly squeezed lemon juice, and ¼ teaspoon salt
1 seven-ounce can imported tuna packed in olive oil
5 flat anchovy fillets (preferably the ones prepared at home as described)
3 tablespoons capers, soaked and rinsed as described if packed in salt, drained if in vinegar
Pitted black olives cut into narrow wedges
authorNotes: Note: This is one of the rare instances in which olive oil in mayonnaise is really to be preferred. Its intense flavor gives the dish greater depth. Please see warning about salmonella poisoning. If you would like to omit the mayonnaise, make the sauce doubling the quantity of tuna, adding 1 anchovy fillet, 1 cup olive oil, and 2 tablespoons lemon juice.

name: La Fiorentina—Grilled T-Bone Steak, Florentine Style
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: Black peppercorns, ground very coarse or crushed with a pestle in a mortar
1 T-bone beef steak, 1½ inches thick, brought to room temperature
OPTIONAL: a lightly crushed and peeled garlic clove
authorNotes: Note: I have seen cooks rub the steak with oil before putting it on the grill, but the scorched oil imparts a taste of tallow to the meat that I prefer to avoid.

name: Pan-Broiled Steaks with Marsala and Chili Pepper
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 4 sirloin steaks or equivalent boneless cut, ¾ inch thick, brought to room temperature before cooking
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
1 tablespoon tomato paste, diluted with 2 tablespoons water
Chopped hot red chili pepper, to taste

name: Pan-Broiled Thin Beef Steaks with Tomatoes and Olives
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ½ medium onion sliced very thin
2 medium garlic cloves, peeled and sliced very thin
⅔ cup fresh, ripe tomatoes, peeled and chopped, OR canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, coarsely chopped, with their juice
Oregano, ½ teaspoon if fresh, ¼ teaspoon if dried
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
1 dozen black, round Greek olives, pitted and quartered
1 pound boneless beef steaks, preferably chuck, sliced less than ½ inch thick, flattened as described above, and brought to room temperature before cooking
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The recipe can be completed up to this point several hours or even a day in advance. Stop short of putting in the olives, adding them only after reheating the sauce, before it is combined with the steaks.

name: Pan-Fried Thin Beef Steaks, Cacciatora Style
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ½ cup onion sliced very thin
4 pan-frying beef steaks, cut less than ½ inch thick from chuck or the round
1 cup flour, spread on a plate
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
A small packet OR 1 ounce dried porcini mushrooms, reconstituted as described and chopped into coarse pieces
The filtered water from the mushroom soak, see instructions
½ cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, cut up, with their juice

name: Pan-Fried Beef Braciole Filled with Cheese and Ham
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1 pound braciole steaks, sliced as thin as possible (see note below)
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
4 very thin slices of fontina cheese
4 thin slices prosciutto OR boiled unsmoked ham
1 cup flour, spread on a plate
Fine, dry, unflavored bread crumbs, spread on a plate
authorNotes: Note: Braciole steaks come from the center cut of the top or bottom round. If they are taken from the broadest section of the round, the slices will be very long, about 10 to 12 inches. In this case, you only need 4 slices, which you will then cut in half. If the slices are from the narrower end section, you’ll need 8 of them, which you will leave whole. If they are any thicker than ¼ inch, have your butcher flatten them some, or do it yourself as described.; Ahead-of-time note: The braciole can be prepared up to this point several hours in advance on the same day you plan to cook them.

name: Farsumauro—Stuffed Large Braciole, Sicilian Style
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: Approximately 1½ pounds beef braciole, cut either in 1 large slice from the center section of the top or bottom round, ½ inch thick, OR 2 smaller slices of the same thickness
¾ pound not too lean ground pork
1 medium garlic clove, peeled and chopped fine
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
⅔ cup flour, spread on a plate

name: Beef Rolls with Red Cabbage and Chianti Wine
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 4 to 5 cups red cabbage shredded very fine
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
1 pound beef round, sliced extra thin, preferably by machine
6 ounces sliced Italian fontina OR comparable tender, fine-flavored cheese
⅔ cup Chianti or other fruity, full-bodied, dry red wine
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: You can cook the rolls up to this point several hours in advance, and reheat them briefly before proceeding to the next step. You may also cook the dish through to the end, and when ready to serve it a few hours later, reheat it gently in its juices.; Note: It’s possible that some of the cheese will run out of the beef rolls while they are cooking. It’s not a cause for concern; mix the cheese into the cabbage, thus enriching the sauce for the beef.

name: Pot Roast of Beef Braised in Red Wine
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 4 pounds boneless beef roast, preferably chuck
2 tablespoons celery chopped very fine 1½ cups dry red wine (see note below)
1 cup or more Basic Homemade Meat Broth, prepared as directed, OR ½ cup canned beef broth plus ½ cup or more water
1½ tablespoons chopped canned imported Italian plum tomatoes
¼ teaspoon fresh marjoram or ⅛ teaspoon dried
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
authorNotes: Wine note This pot roast is a version of Piedmont’s stracotto al Barolo and an ideal rendition of it would call for Barolo in the pot as well as Barolo in your glass. If you must make a substitution, try to use another Piedmontese red, such as Barbaresco or a fine Barbera. Other suitable choices: Rhone wine, California Syrah or Zinfandel, or Shiraz from Australia or South Africa.

name: Pot Roast of Beef Braised in Amarone Wine
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ¾ cup onion chopped very fine
2 garlic cloves, lightly mashed and peeled
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
1¾ cups Amarone wine (see introductory note above)
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: You can complete the recipe up to this point several hours before serving. When ready to serve, warm the meat in its sauce over very low heat. If the roast has absorbed all the sauce, add 1 or 2 tablespoons of water while reheating.

name: Beef Roast Braised with Onions
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ¼ pound pancetta OR salt porkin a single piece
2 pounds boneless beef roast, preferably the brisket
4 medium onions sliced very, very thin
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill

name: Beef Tenderloin with Red Wine
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ⅔ cup flour, spread on a plate
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
⅔ cup full-bodied dry red wine (follow suggestions in wine note to recipe Pot Roast of Beef Braised in Red Wine)

name: Beef Stew with Red Wine and Vegetables
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 pounds boneless beef chuck, cut into 1½- to 2-inch stewing cubes
1½ cups sturdy red wine, preferably a Barbera from Piedmont
1½ pounds fresh peas, unshelled weight, OR 1½ ten-ounce packages frozen peas, thawed
¼ cup extra virgin olive oil
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: Like all stews, this one will have excellent flavor when prepared a day or two in advance. Reheat gently just before serving.

name: Meatballs and Tomatoes
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: A slice of good-quality white bread
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
Fine, dry, unflavored bread crumbs, spread on a plate
1 cup fresh, ripe tomatoes, peeled and chopped, OR canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, chopped up, with their juice
authorNotes: A head-of-time note The dish can be cooked entirely in advance and stored in a tightly covered container in the refrigerator for several days. Reheat gently before serving.

name: Winter Meatballs with Savoy Cabbage
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: A slice of good-quality white bread, trimmed of its crust
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
1 cup fine, dry, unflavored bread crumbs, spread on a plate
⅔ cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, drained and cut up into coarse pieces

name: Beef Patties Baked with Anchovies and Mozzarella
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ½ slice of good-quality white bread
Fine, dry, unflavored bread crumbs, spread on a plate
6 whole canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, drained of juice, OR fresh tomatoes (see note)
Oregano, 1 teaspoon if fresh, ½ teaspoon if dried
6 slices mozzarella, each cut ¼ inch thick
12 flat anchovy fillets (preferably the ones prepared at home as described)
authorNotes: Note: You can use fresh plum tomatoes if they are at the height of their season and very ripe and meaty.; Ahead-of-time note: The baking dish with the patties may be prepared several hours in advance, before proceeding to the final step.

name: Tuscan Meat Roll with White Wine and Porcini Mushrooms
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: A 2- by 2-inch slice of good-quality white bread, trimmed of all the crust
1 tablespoon onion chopped very, very fine
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
2 tablespoons chopped prosciutto OR pancetta OR boiled unsmoked ham
⅓ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese
Fine, dry, unflavored bread crumbs, spread on a plate
A small packet OR 1 ounce dried porcini mushrooms, reconstituted as described, and chopped into coarse pieces
The filtered water from the mushroom soak, see instructions
⅔ cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, chopped, with their juice, OR fresh, ripe tomatoes, peeled and chopped

name: Bollito Misto—Mixed Boiled Meat Platter
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ½ red or yellow sweet bell pepper, its seeds and pulpy core removed
2 to 3 pounds boneless beef brisket or chuck
¼ cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, drained and cut up, OR 1 whole fresh, very ripe tomato
3 pounds veal breast with the short ribs in
1 cotechino sausage, boiled separately as described, and kept warm in its own broth
authorNotes: Serving notes Although a platter piled with steaming meat makes a hearty spectacle, the bollito will be much more succulent if kept submerged in its broth. Transfer to serving bowls or tureens, if you like, but pull out a piece of meat only to carve it and serve immediately. Keep the cotechino and its broth separate; the broth must not be mixed with that of the other meats and should be discarded when the sausage has been served.; Ahead-of-time note: Bollito misto can be kept in its broth and refrigerated if you plan on serving it the following day. Please see remarks on storing broth for longer than 3 days.

name: Roast Easter Lamb with White Wine
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 to 2½ pounds spring lamb (see note below), preferably the shoulder
A sprig of fresh rosemary, cut into 2 or 3 pieces, OR ½ teaspoon dried rosemary leaves
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
authorNotes: Note: The recipe given below works well with any lamb, but it is most successful with a very young, small one.

name: Abbacchio—Baby Lamb, Pan-Roasted Roman Style
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 3 pounds shoulder, with some loin attached, of very young lamb, cut into 3-inch pieces, with the larger bones removed, if you like
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
6 to 8 fresh sage leaves OR ½ teaspoon dried leaves, chopped fine
A sprig of fresh rosemary OR 1 teaspoon chopped dried
4 flat anchovy fillets (preferably the ones prepared at home as described), chopped to a pulp
authorNotes: Note: In Rome, the fat traditionally used for roasting abbacchio is lard, and no other produces comparably fine flavor or the same light, crisp surface on the meat. But if you prefer not to use lard, you can replace it with olive oil, or a combination of butter and vegetable oil, or all vegetable oil.; Ahead-of-time note: The lamb tastes best when served the moment it is done, but it can be prepared several hours in advance, up to but not including the step with the anchovies. Reheat it gently, adding a little bit of water if its juices are skimpy. When it is warm all the way through, carry out the procedure with the anchovies as described above in Step 4.

name: Pan–Roasted Lamb with Juniper Berries
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2½ pounds lamb shoulder, cut into 3- to 4-inch pieces, with the bone in
2 garlic cloves, mashed lightly with a knife handle, the skin removed
A sprig of fresh rosemary OR ½ teaspoon chopped dried
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: If you are going to have it that same evening, the lamb can be prepared that morning. It could even be prepared a day in advance, but its flavor will acquire a sharp edge. When making it ahead of time, do not reduce the pot juices or spoon off the fat until after you have reheated it.

name: Thin Lamb Chops Fried in Parmesan Batter
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 12 single rib lamb chops, partly boned and flattened as described above
½ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese, spread on a plate
2 eggs, beaten lightly in a deep dish
1 cup fine, dry, unflavored bread crumbs, spread on a plate
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: You can prepare the chops up to this point as much as 1 hour in advance or, if you refrigerate them, even 3 or 4 hours. If refrigerated, allow enough time for the meat to return to room temperature before cooking it.; Note: If the chops are as thin as directed, they will be cooked in the time it takes to form a crust on both sides. If they are much thicker, they need to be cooked a little longer.

name: Lamb Chops Pan-Roasted in White Wine, Finished Marches Style with Egg and Lemon
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ⅓ cup, about 1½ ounces, pancetta, cut into thin julienne strips
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
1 egg yolk (see warning about salmonella poisoning)

name: Lamb Stew with Vinegar and Green Beans
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ¼ cup extra virgin olive oil
3 pounds lamb shoulder, cut into 2-inch cubes, with the bone in
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
½ cup good red wine vinegar
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The dish may be prepared entirely in advance and reheated gently just before serving. As with any dish with greens, it will taste best if consumed the day it is made without subjecting it to refrigeration.

name: Lamb Stew with Ham and Red Bell Pepper
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 3 pounds lamb shoulder, cut into 2-inch cubes, with the bone in
A sprig of fresh rosemary OR ½ teaspoon dried rosemary leaves
4 or 5 fresh sage leaves OR 2 or 3 dried ones
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
¼ pound boiled unsmoked ham, cut into thin strips
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The stew can be prepared up to this point several hours or a day in advance. Reheat gently, but thoroughly before proceeding with the next step.

name: Pork Loin Braised in Milk, Bolognese Style
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2½ pounds pork rib roast (see note below)
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
authorNotes: Note: The cut of meat specified above includes the rib bones to which the pork’s loin is attached. Have the butcher detach the meat in one piece from the ribs and split the ribs into two or three parts. By having had the loin boned, you can brown it more thoroughly, and by cooking it along with the bones, the roast benefits from the substantial contribution of flavor the bones make.

name: Roast Pork with Vinegar and Bay Leaves
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 pounds pork loin roast, boneless, OR Boston butt, in one piece
1 teaspoon whole black peppercorns 3 bay leaves
½ cup good red wine vinegar

name: Drunk Roast Pork
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 3½ to 4 pounds pork center loin OR Boston butt, trussed up tightly with string
2 tablespoons grappa, marc, calvados, or grape brandy (see note)
1½ cups or more dry red wine (see suggestions above in prefacing note)
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The roast can be finished several hours in advance, early in the day of the evening you plan to serve it. Reheat it gently in a covered pot, long enough for the meat to warm up all the way through, adding 2 or 3 tablespoons of water if it becomes necessary.

name: Braised Pork Chops with Tomatoes, Cream, and Porcini Mushrooms
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 pounds pork chops, preferably from the center loin, cut ¾ inch thick
½ cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, drained and cut up
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
A small packet OR 1 ounce dried porcini mushrooms, reconstituted as described on this page and cut up
The filtered water from the mushroom soak, see this page for instructions
½ pound fresh, white button mushrooms

name: Braised Pork Chops with Sage and Tomatoes, Modena Style
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 4 pork loin chops, preferably bottom loin, ¾ inch thick
6 to 8 fresh sage leaves OR 3 to 4 dried ones
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
¾ cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, cut up, with their juice, OR fresh, ripe tomatoes, peeled and chopped

name: Braised Pork Chops with Two Wines
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 4 pork loin chops, preferably bottom loin, ¾ inch thick
1 tablespoon tomato paste, dissolved in a mixture of ½ cup dry Marsala and ½ cup dry young red wine (see remarks above)
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill

name: Stewed Pork with Porcini Mushrooms and Juniper
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1½ pounds boned pork shoulder, cut into pieces about 1 inch thick and 2 inches wide
⅓ cup extra virgin olive oil
A small packet OR 1 ounce dried porcini mushrooms, reconstituted as described on this page and cut up
The filtered water from the mushroom soak, see this page for instructions
3 flat anchovy fillets (preferably the ones prepared at home as described on this page), chopped to a pulp
½ teaspoon fresh marjoram OR ¼ teaspoon dried
2 bay leaves, chopped if fresh, crumbled fine if dried
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The dish can be completed 2 or 3 days in advance, but do not reduce the juices or discard any fat until after you have gently, but thoroughly reheated the stew.

name: Spareribs Pan-Roasted with Sage and White Wine, Treviso Style
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: A 3-pound rack of spareribs, divided into single ribs
3 garlic cloves, peeled and cut into very thin slices
2 tablespoons fresh sage leaves OR 2 teaspoons whole dried ones, chopped
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill

name: Spareribs with Tomatoes and Vegetables for Polenta
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: A 3-pound rack of spareribs, cut by the butcher into finger-size pieces
1½ cups canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, chopped, with their juice, OR fresh, ripe tomatoes, peeled and cut up
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: You can cook the ribs through to the end up to 1 day in advance. Reheat gently, but thoroughly before serving.; Note: If you like sausages, you can replace half the amount of ribs called for with 1 pound of sausages, and cook them together. It makes a very tasty dish. Cut the sausages in half before cooking, and use the plainest pork sausage you can buy, without fennel seeds, cumin, chili pepper, or other extraneous flavors.

name: Grilled Marinated Spareribs
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ¼ cup extra virgin olive oil
1 tablespoon fresh rosemary leaves chopped very fine OR 2 teaspoons dried, chopped
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
A 3-pound rack of pork spareribs in one piece

name: Pork Sausages with Red Cabbage
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1 tablespoon chopped garlic ¼ cup extra virgin olive oil
1½ pounds red cabbage, cut into fine strips, about 8 cups
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
1 pound mild pork sausage, containing no herbs or strong spices

name: Pork Sausages with Smothered Onions and Tomatoes
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 cups onion sliced very, very fine
1 cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, chopped up, with just a little of their juice, OR fresh, ripe tomatoes, peeled and cut up
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
1 yellow or red sweet bell pepper
1 pound mild pork sausage, containing no herbs or strong spices
authorNotes: Leftover note: If sausage is left over, cut it into small pieces and combine it with the fat you had kept aside to form the flavor base of a risotto, see instructions or crumble it, reheat it with a little of its reserved fat, and toss it with pasta; or drain it well of fat, combine it with grated Parmesan cheese, and add to a frittata.; Ahead-of-time note: The sausages may be cooked all the way to the end several hours or a day or two in advance. Do not degrease until after they have been reheated.

name: Pork Sausages with Black-Eyed Peas and Tomatoes
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1 cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, chopped coarse, with their juice, OR fresh, ripe tomatoes, peeled and cut up
1 pound mild pork sausage, containing no herbs or strong spices
1 cup dried black-eyed peas, soaked for at least 1 hour in lukewarm water
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
authorNotes: Oven note If you prefer to use the oven, after the peas have been added and the contents brought to a simmer as described in Step 3 you can transfer the pot to the middle level of a preheated 350° oven. Make sure the pot handles are ovenproof. If, when the peas are done, the juices need to be reduced, do so on top of the stove.; Ahead-of-time note: The dish can be cooked entirely in advance several days ahead. If the cooking juices need to be reduced, do it only after thoroughly reheating the sausages and peas.

name: Pork Sausages with Red Wine and Porcini Mushrooms
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1½ pounds mild pork sausage, containing no herbs or strong spices
A small packet OR 1 ounce dried porcini mushrooms, reconstituted as described on this page
The filtered water from the mushroom soak, see instructions

name: Cotechino—Boiled Large Sausage with Lentils
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1 cup lentils, washed in cold water and drained
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill

name: Pizza Rustica—Pork and Cheese Pie, Abruzzi Style
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 8 tablespoons (1 stick) butter, cut into small pieces
¼ pound prosciutto OR country ham OR boiled unsmoked ham, chopped rather coarse
¼ pound mozzarella, preferably buffalo-milk mozzarella, cut up in small pieces
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
The cold sweet egg pastry made as directed above

name: Sautéed Calf’s Liver with Lemon, Piccata Style
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1 pound choice, pale pink calf’s liver, cut into slices no more than ¼ inch thick
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill

name: Sautéed Calf’s Liver and Onions, Venetian Style
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1½ pounds choice, pale pink calf’s liver, cut into slices no more than ¼ inch thick
3 cups onion sliced very, very thin
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: You can complete the recipe up to this point several hours in advance.

name: Breaded Calf’s Liver
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1½ pounds choice, pale pink calf’s liver, cut into slices no more than ¼ inch thick
¾ cup fine, dry, unflavored bread crumbs, lightly toasted in the oven or in a skillet
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill

name: Grilled Calf’s or Pork Liver Wrapped in Caul
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: About 1 pound fresh or thawed caul, in one or several pieces
1½ pounds calf or pork liver, cut into pieces about 1 inch thick, 3 inches long and 2 inches wide
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
Preferably a charcoal or gas-fired lava rock grill

name: Sautéed Chicken Livers with Sage and White Wine
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill

name: Sautéed Sweetbreads with Tomatoes and Peas
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ⅔ cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, chopped coarse, with their juice
2 pounds unshelled fresh young peas OR 1 ten-ounce package frozen small, early peas, thawed
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: You can complete the recipe up to this point even a day in advance, stopping short, however, of cutting the sweetbreads into pieces. Refrigerate tightly wrapped in plastic wrap or in an airtight plastic bag. Cut into pieces when cold out of the refrigerator, but let them come to room temperature before proceeding with the next step.

name: Sautéed Lamb Kidneys with Onion, Treviso Style
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill

name: Sautéed Lamb Kidneys with Onion, Garlic, and White Wine
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ¼ cup extra virgin olive oil
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
½ teaspoon cornstarch mixed into ¾ cup dry white wine

name: Fried Calf’s Brains
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1 cup fine, dry, unflavored bread crumbs, lightly toasted in a skillet or in the oven, spread on a plate
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: You can prepare the brains up to this point several hours in advance on the same day you expect to fry them. Also see the alternative to frying in the variation below with poached brains.

name: Oxtail, Vaccinara Style
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: OPTIONAL: ¼ pound fresh pork jowl OR pig’s feet OR pork hock (see note above)
¼ cup extra virgin olive oil plus 1 tablespoon lard or ham fat OR 1 additional tablespoon olive oil
2½ pounds oxtail (thawed, if frozen), severed at each joint
½ cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, drained and chopped very coarse
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
authorNotes: Note: An ingredient of the traditional recipe is pork rind, which usually comes from the jowl. It makes an interesting contribution to the consistency as well as to the underlying flavor of the dish, but it is not an absolutely indispensable component, and if you cannot find it, it is preferable to omit it rather than forego making the oxtail. Also see, in this connection, the introductory remarks in the recipe for Beans and Sauerkraut Soup.; Note: If you prefer to use the oven, you can put the pot into a preheated 350° oven after adding the tomatoes and bringing them to a simmer.; Ahead-of-time note: You can complete the dish several hours or even 2 or 3 days in advance. Reheat it gently over the stove in a covered pot until the meat is very warm through and through; cold oxtail is not very appealing. If any meat is left over, it can be used with its juices to sauce pasta.

name: Honeycomb Tripe with Parmesan Cheese
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 pounds ready-to-cook honeycomb tripe, thawed if frozen
2 medium garlic cloves, mashed lightly with a knife handle and peeled
Chopped rosemary leaves, 1 teaspoon if fresh, ½ teaspoon if dried 1 cup dry white wine
1 cup canned imported Italian plum tomatoes, cut up, with their juice, OR, if very ripe and firm, fresh tomatoes, peeled and cut up
Chopped hot red chili pepper, to taste
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
1 cup Basic Homemade Meat Broth, prepared as directed on this page, OR ½ cup canned beef broth diluted with ½ cup water
¾ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: It is fortunate, considering the long cooking time, that tripe tastes still better the day after it is cooked. It can even be prepared several days in advance and refrigerated in a tightly sealed container. Reheat over the stove, with the lid on slightly ajar, until the tripe is hot again. Replenish the cooking liquid if it becomes insufficient with 2 or 3 tablespoons of water. When preparing it ahead of time, swirl in the fresh butter and grated Parmesan only after reheating, just before serving.

name: Tripe with Beans Variation
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 1½ pounds fresh cranberry beans, unshelled weight, OR ¾ cup dried cranberry or white cannellini beans, soaked and cooked as described on this page, with their liquid, OR 2¼ cups drained canned cranberry or white cannellini beans
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The comments appended to the tripe recipe apply here. As in that recipe, swirl in the fresh butter and the grated cheese only after reheating, just before serving.

name: Braised Artichokes and Peas
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 large globe artichokes OR 3 to 4 medium size
½ teaspoon garlic chopped very fine
2 pounds fresh unshelled peas OR 1 ten-ounce package frozen peas, thawed
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The dish can be prepared any time in advance on the same day it will be served. Do not refrigerate or its flavor will be altered. Reheat gently in a covered pot, with 1 tablespoon water, if necessary.

name: Braised Artichokes and Leeks
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 3 large globe artichokes OR 5 or 6 medium size
4 large leeks, about 1¾ inch thick, OR 6 smaller ones
¼ cup extra virgin olive oil
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The note at the end of Braised Artichokes and Peas is applicable here.

name: Braised Artichokes and Potatoes
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ¼ cup extra virgin olive oil
¼ teaspoon garlic chopped very fine
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The note at the end of Braised Artichokes and Peas is applicable here.

name: La Frittedda—Smothered Artichokes, Fava Beans, and Peas with Fennel, Palermo Style
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 3 medium OR 5 small artichokes, very fresh, with no black spots or other discoloration
2 pounds fresh, small fava beans in their pods OR ⅔ of a 15-ounce can “green fave,” drained
1 pound fresh small peas in their pods OR ½ of a 10-ounce package choice quality, frozen small peas, thawed
1 cup fresh wild fennel OR 1½ cups leafy finocchio tops OR ⅔ cup fresh dill
1½ cups sweet raw onion sliced very thin (see note below)
⅓ cup extra virgin olive oil Salt
authorNotes: Note: Use the sweetest variety of onion available: Vidalia, Maui, or Bermuda. If you can obtain none of these, soak sliced yellow onion in several changes of cold water for 30 minutes, gently squeezing the onion in your hand each time you change the water; drain before using.

name: Crisp-Fried Artichoke Wedges
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 3 medium artichokes OR 1 ten-ounce package frozen artichoke hearts, thawed
If using fresh artichokes: ½ lemon and 1 tablespoon freshly squeezed lemon juice
1 cup fine, dry, unflavored bread crumbs, spread on a plate
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The recipe can be completed up to this point several hours in advance, but if refrigerating the crumbed vegetables, take them out in sufficient time to come fully to room temperature.

name: Gratin of Artichokes
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: Butter for smearing the dish and dotting
½ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese

name: Gratin of Artichokes, Potatoes, and Onions
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: 2 large globe artichokes OR 4 medium size
2 tablespoons butter plus more for dotting the baking dish
3 medium potatoes, peeled and sliced potato-chip thin
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
½ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese

name: Artichoke Torta in a Flaky Crust
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
½ cup freshly grated parmigiano-reggiano cheese
MAKING THE PASTRY CRUST AND COMPLETING THE TORTA
8 tablespoons (1 stick) butter, softened to room temperature
Butter and flour for the pan
authorNotes: How to use When sliced very thin, raw sunchokes are crisp and juicy at the same time, with a nutty flavor that is most welcome in a salad. When sautéed or gratinéed, their texture is a blend of cream and silk, and their taste vaguely recalls that of artichoke hearts, but is sweeter, with none of the artichoke’s underlying bitterness. The thin skin can be left on when they are to be eaten raw, but must be removed for cooking because it hardens.; How to buy Sunchokes are in season from fall through early spring. They are at their best when very firm; as they lose freshness, they become spongy.

name: Sautéed Sunchokes

authorNotes: How to keep Ideally, asparagus should go from the market into the pot, but hours or even a day may elapse during which you’ll want to keep it as fresh as possible. Bunch the asparagus if loose, and stand it with its butts in a container with 1 or 2 inches of cold water. You can store it thus in a cool place, unrefrigerated, for up to a day or a day and a half.; How to prepare for cooking All but a small woody portion at the bottom of the stalk can be made edible, if the asparagus is properly prepared. Begin by slicing off about 1 inch at the thick butt end. If you find that the end of the stalk you exposed is parched and stringy, slice off a little more until you reach a moister part. The younger the asparagus is, the less you will need to trim from the bottom.; How to cook Choose any pan that can later contain the trimmed spears lying flat. Fill with water and bring it to a boil. Add 1 tablespoon salt for every pound of asparagus, and as the water starts boiling rapidly again, put in the asparagus. Cover the pan to hasten the water’s return to a boil. When it does so, you can uncover the pan. After 10 minutes, you can begin testing the asparagus by prodding the thickest part of the stalk with a fork. It is done when easily pierced. (If very thin and exceptionally fresh, it may take a minute or two less.) Drain immediately when cooked.

name: Gratinéed Asparagus with Parmesan
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: Butter for smearing and dotting the dish

name: Asparagus and Prosciutto Bundles
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: ½ pound Italian fontina cheese, cut into thin slices (see note below)
2 tablespoons butter plus more for smearing and dotting a baking dish
authorNotes: Note: If you cannot find true imported Italian fontina, rather than substituting bland imitation fontina from other sources, use parmigiano-reggiano cheese shaved into thin, long slivers. If you do so, substitute boiled unsmoked ham for the prosciutto because both Parmesan and prosciutto are salty and the two combined might make the asparagus bundles too salty.; When and how to buy Their season lasts from April to June, but the best beans are the earliest and youngest. When shelled, they should be the size of lima beans or only slightly larger. Bigger fava are tougher, drier, and more starchy. Look for pods that do not bulge too thickly with overgrown beans.; How to cook When cooking fresh fava beans, the best advice is, do as the Romans do. The classic Roman preparation, which in the spring you can sample in every trattoria in the city, has few peers among great bean dishes. I have never gone through a single spring without cooking it at least half a dozen times, and no food I can put on the table is ever more warmly received. In Rome, the dish is known as fave al guanciale, because the beans are cooked with pork jowl, guanciale. In the version given here, pancetta—which is far easier to find—replaces pork jowl with total success.

name: Fava Beans, Roman Style
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)
ingredients: Pancetta in a single slice ½ inch thick
3 pounds unshelled young fresh fava beans
Black pepper, ground fresh from the mill
authorNotes: How to buy Although they are now available throughout the year, the ones grown locally in spring and summer are still the best. Their color should be a uniform green, either light or dark, but even, without spots or yellowing patches. Their skin should be fresh looking, almost moist, not dull. And the beans should not be a mixed lot, but all of one size, preferably not too thick. If you can, take a bean from the basket and snap it: It should snap sharply and crisply.; How to cook Beans must be cooked long enough to develop a round, nutty, sweet flavor: They should not be overcooked, but not undercooked either. When undercooked, theirs is not the taste of beans, but the raw taste of grass. When boiling beans, you must add salt to the boiling water before dropping in the beans in order to keep their color a bright green. This principle applies to all green vegetables, particularly spinach and Swiss chard. The vegetable does not become salty because virtually all the salt remains dissolved in the water.; Boiling 1 pound of green beans

authorNotes: When and how to buy The best season for finocchio, when it is juiciest and its fragrance is sweetest and freshest, is from fall to spring, but it is also available in summer. Italians distinguish between male and female finocchio, the first with a stocky, round bulb, the latter flat and elongated. The “male” is crisper and less stringy, and it has a finer scent, qualities that are particularly desirable when it is to be eaten raw. For cooking, the flatter bulb is acceptable, but as long as it’s equally fresh, the thicker, rounder one will always taste better.

authorNotes: White mushrooms It is, by an overwhelming margin, the most common market mushroom. In Italy it goes either by the French name champignon, or its Italian equivalent, prataiolo, both words meaning “of the field.” It is claimed that size does not affect taste, but I find the texture of the small, young mushroom called “button” to be distinctly superior to that of the more mature, larger examples.; Cremini This light- to dark-brown mushroom was the one cultivated variety available long before the white mushroom was developed. It has more depth of flavor than the white, but it is also more expensive. If cost is not a consideration, it can be used with success in any recipe that calls for white button mushrooms.; Shiitake The stems of this brown Japanese variety are too tough to use, but the caps give marvelous results when cooked by the method applied in Italy to fresh porcini.; How to buy and store Look for firmness as an indication of freshness, and avoid flabbiness, which is a signal of staleness. When buying white button or cremini mushrooms, choose those with smooth, closed caps, whereas the caps of shiitake are always open. Do not store mushrooms in plastic, which accelerates their absorption of moisture and deterioration, but in paper bags. If very fresh, they will stay in good condition in a refrigerator or a in a cold room in winter for 2 or 3 days.

authorNotes: How to buy Good zucchini is available most of the year, but it has the most flavor during its natural season, late spring and early summer. Choose zucchini from a basket or vegetable bin whose contents seem to be all of a size, which indicates they come from a single lot rather than a mixed one of varying age and freshness. In Italy, zucchini is sometimes brought to market with the blossoms attached. The blossom wilts rapidly and drops off, so when it is still on and looks bright it is proof of the freshness of the zucchini.; How to clean The thin zucchini skin is easily penetrated by soil, which is hard to detect until you are eating. Although zucchini is occasionally fairly free of grit, the prudent course is to assume there is soil embedded in the skin and proceed to loosen it and remove it as described below.

authorNotes: Note: Some pound cakes absorb more than others, and the rum and coffee mixture may not suffice for soaking all the slices; have additional coffee and rum available and prepare more, if you need to, guided by the proportions given above.; Ahead-of-time note: You can leave the diplomatico in the refrigerator for up to a week before proceeding with the next step.

2 tablespoons Cointreau or white Curaçao liqueur
5 ounces semisweet chocolate in drops or squares
A mixing bowl kept in the freezer
2 cups very cold heavy whipping cream
authorNotes: Note: Maraschino is a fine Italian liqueur made from the pulp and crushed pits of the Dalmatian marasca cherry, with a distinctive flavor that no other fruit cordial duplicates. One of its most appealing uses is with marinated fresh fruit. Do not confuse the liqueur with the cherries that go by the same name; the latter are quite ordinary preserved and artificially colored cherries. There’s no perfect substitute for Maraschino, but if you must use something else, look for a not too sweet cherry liqueur.; How to choose In Italy one distinguishes between two types of edible chestnuts; one is small and flat, each prickly bur that contains them bears two, and it is known as castagna comune, or common chestnut; the other is larger and much plumper, each bur contains just one, and it is known as marrone. The latter is the kind to look for, because its flesh is much juicier and sweeter. When truly fresh, chestnut skins should be glossy, unwrinkled, and the nut should feel heavy in the hand.; How to prepare The whole secret of cooking fresh chestnuts successfully rests in learning how to cut their shell, so that when they are cooked both the shell and the tough thin skin beneath will easily come away.

name: Monte Bianco—Puréed Chestnut and Chocolate Mound
sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)

A mixing bowl kept in the freezer
2 cups very cold heavy whipping cream
authorNotes: Ahead-of-time note: The chestnut and chocolate mixture can be prepared up to this point a day in advance and refrigerated until you are ready to proceed.; Ahead-of-time note: The monte bianco can be completed as described above, and served up to 4 to 6 hours later. Refrigerate it, but do not cover it. Also refrigerate the remaining half of the whipped cream.

Throughout the Italian peninsula, and beyond it to the Sicilian islands, ravishing sweet muscat wines are made, and if you chance upon one of these, do not pass it by. If you are obliged to choose a substitute, any fine, natural, late-harvest sweet white wine from Germany, South Africa, or California will do.

authorNotes: Freezing the gelato mixture Pour the mixture, making sure it is already cold, into the container of your ice-cream maker, and freeze it following the manufacturer’s instructions. When done, the gelato is ready to eat, but if you want to serve it later, or prefer it when it’s more compact, transfer it to a container that closes tightly and place it in the freezer. If you are going to leave it in the freezer overnight or longer, let the gelato soften a little before serving by putting it in the refrigerator for 30 minutes.

A true Champagne-method sparkler has a yeastier, more complex flavor than is desirable for this preparation.

authorNotes: Note: The authentic flavor of Naples’s pizza owes much to the very ripe, firm, fresh San Marzano plum tomatoes that go raw into the topping. In the brief season when local tomatoes of exceptional ripeness and firmness are available, omit the preliminary cooking procedure described in steps 1 and 2 below and use them as follows:

name: Sfinciuni—Palermo’s Stuffed Pizza

CONZA DI SAN VITO—MEAT AND CHEESE FILLING

authorNotes: Food processor note The previous two steps may be carried out in the food processor.; Note: Palermo’s primo sale and fresh caciocavallo cheeses that are both part of this stuffing are not yet, as I write, available outside Sicily. I have replaced them with a combination of fontina and ricotta to achieve mild flavor with a tart accent. I think it works, but if you have other ideas, try them out.

name: Tomato and Anchovy Conza

6 flat anchovy fillets (preferably the ones prepared at home as described), chopped to a pulp
Oregano, 1 teaspoon if fresh, ½ teaspoon if dried
¼ cup plain, unflavored bread crumbs, lightly toasted
The dough made using this recipe

sourceBook: Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking — Marcella Hazan (1992)

¼ cup extra virgin olive oil
2 tablespoons plain, unflavored bread crumbs, lightly toasted
